a. Review the Report on the Utilization of LDRRMF and monitor the balance
available for use in the disaster risk reduction and management activities within
the next five years as provided in the LDRRMFIP;
b. Require the CDRRMO to coordinate with the City Engineer in ensuring that
the object of expenditures, such as Infrastructure projects for LDRRMF, are
monitored and accomplished to avoid the accumulating balance of unexpended
funds; and
c. Require the CDRRMO, City Budget Officer, and the City Accountant to
coordinate with each other in ensuring that the utilization rate for the
LDRRMF-Continuing Appropriation and STF is improved through constant
monitoring of the LDRRMF.

We commended the City Government for fully utilizing the LGSF-SBDP-NTF-
ELCAC, thereby addressing the existing issues and development gaps at the
barangay level, which greatly benefited their constituents.
However, we recommended that Management require the LFC to prepare the
mandatory quarterly report and post it in at least three conspicuous public places
within the locality, as well as on the LGU’s website. After the posting, a written
notice should be sent to the Secretary of DBM, Secretary of DILG, Chairperson of
the NTF-ELCAC, coursed through the NTF-ELCAC Vice-Chairperson and
concerned RTF-ELCAC, Speaker of the House of Representatives, President of
the Senate of the Philippines, and the Chairpersons of the House Committee on
Appropriations and Senate Committee on Finance, in consonance with Sections
5.3 and 5.4 of LBC No. 151.

We recommended that Management:
a. Secure copies of the documents supporting the acquisition of the right of way
and submit them to the Audit Team for verification; and
b. Strictly comply with Section 17.6 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 and
ensure that the acquisition of the right of way for future infrastructure projects
is properly carried out.

We recommended that the City Government, through its GFPS/TWG, continue
to design and implement PPAs that recognize gender responsive governance,
promote protection and fulfillment of women and children’s human rights, and
address women’s practical and strategic needs. Likewise, regularly monitor and
evaluate the utilization of the GAD PB to ensure that all PPAs are fully
implemented as planned and that challenges that cause non-implementation, if
any, are identified and addressed.
Further, we recommended that the Management instruct the GAD FPS to
strictly observe the timeline in the preparation and submission of GAD PB and
GAD AR pursuant to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-001 and COA
Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2024.
Furthermore, we recommended that GAD FPS ensure that the submitted GAD
AR is duly supported with copies of the reported policy issuances, if any, results
of the HGDG tests, and actions taken by the LGU on the COA audit findings and
recommendations in the prior years, as supporting documents of the GAD AR,
as required by the JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Management create or assign a Responsibility
Center (RC) for their GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), or maintain a subsidiary
ledger for GAD accounts and assign an SL code, to facilitate proper accounting
and monitoring of GAD related expenses and other GAD related financial
transactions for the GAD funds of the City.

We commended the Management for providing their JOW personnel with the
minimum wage for CY 2024, ensuring that their JOs are fairly compensated.
However, we recommended that Management evaluate and determine the
appropriate personnel complement of each office/department in the City
Government, make necessary amendments if warranted, and require the CHRMO
to formulate a Human Resource Plan and issue a uniform policy guideline on the
hiring of JO personnel to appropriately determine the necessity of the hiring
thereof, as well as to strengthen the internal controls pertaining thereto.

We recommended that Management consider the use of the ATM facilities
available at the AGSBs in paying the wages of the Job Order Personnel, to
safeguard the government funds against the risk of loss through theft, misuse, or
misappropriation, and improve cash management to ensure efficient and
economical use of government funds.
We also recommended that Management request the City Accountant, the City
Treasurer, and the City Human Resource and Management Officer to coordinate
with the Agency’s AGSBs for the use of their ATM service facilities in paying the
payroll of the LGU.

We commended the Management for ensuring that all PPAs chargeable against
the SEF comply with Item 4 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of
2017.
However, we recommended that Management continue to maximize and ensure
that the SEF of the City is efficiently utilized and the implementation of all
identified PPAs be given priority to warrant the full attainment of goals in
accordance with the aforecited Joint Circular.

We recommended that recipient schools of PPAs be identified in the plan and
budget, SEF Budget Preparation Form 1 and LSN Resolution authorizing the
appropriation in compliance with Section 5.2.2 of the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint
Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2017 that the budget shall be presented by program,
activity, and project (PAP), by allotment class, and per school.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management direct the
BAC and the BAC-TWG:
a. To include in their post-qualification a comparative evaluation showing the
number of projects with the same equipment pledged and assigned by the same
lowest calculated bidder for different projects with simultaneous or overlapping
implementation periods; and
b. To consider formulating a policy on the sufficiency and availability of
equipment pledged by the contractors with regard to projects with
simultaneous or overlapping implementation periods awarded to the same
contractors to avoid delay in project implementation, without affecting the
quality and timeliness of the project implementation.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management instruct
the BAC and the TWG to properly check and verify the veracity and truthfulness of
the documents submitted by the bidders during the bidding and for post-qualification
to accurately determine the Lowest Calculated Responsive Bid. Also, the BAC to
strictly enforce the penalties provided under Rules XXI and XXIII of this IRR so that
bidders will strictly comply with the requirements of the bidding.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the BAC to ensure that the contractor’s safety cost aligns with the
DOLE-approved CSHP before issuing the Notice to Proceed. If there is a
discrepancy, it must be addressed through clarification or contract adjustment,
if necessary;
b. Instruct the BAC to require from the contractors the submission of the CSHP
duly approved by the DOLE; and
c. Require the City Engineer to integrate the cost of the Construction and Safety
Health Program into the total construction cost of the projects to be
implemented by the City.

We recommended that Management require the Bids and Awards Committee
to post the APP in the Transparency Seal section of the agency’s website.
We further recommended that Management submit an electronic copy of the
APP to the GPPB in both Microsoft Excel and Portable Document Format files on or
before January 31 of the current year, including a certification confirming that the
APP has been posted on the agency's website or in a conspicuous place within the
City Government.

We commended the Management for their continued efforts to improve their
revenue forecasting system and collection efforts that will ensure the realization of
revenue targets, thus, provide funding for plans and programs outlined in the
budget of the City, thereby ensuring the delivery of public services.
Henceforth, the Management require the City Treasury and other Departments
involved in generating revenues to meet its targets by conducting periodic review
of collection performance by the concerned officials to ascertain whether
collection targets are attained and to analyze the reasons for the shortfall in
collections and find alternative solutions to minimize, reduce or avoid incurrence
of shortfall in the future.

We recommended that Management conduct a periodic review of
accomplishments to have an assurance that all revenue sources are tapped to maximize
its revenue- generating powers as mandated by law.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Treasurer, and
other concerned Department Heads to prepare and submit to the Sangguniang
Panlungsod for approval, a consolidated proposed adjustment on tax rates to fully
exercise the LGU's taxing power, pursuant to the above-cited provisions of the Code,
to increase tax revenues and receipts through updating of the rates in the existing
tax code, which will augment the budget provision for a better delivery of
government services.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the City Accountant to analyze the balance of the "Due to BIR"
account, identify the taxes withheld that were included in the one-time
remittance in March 2025, and provide the Audit Team with copies of the
alpha list or a breakdown of the remitted taxes;
b. Immediately remit the taxes still due for remittance, in compliance with the
related laws and regulations; and
c. Require the City Accountant to attach the proof of payment to the BIR,
payment/remittance forms, and the alpha lists to the Disbursement Voucher
for the remittance of taxes and to include them in the monthly submission of
accounts to the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management designate an employee in the Accounting
Office whose only task would focus on the analysis and reconciliation of the Due
to GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and PhilHealth accounts, and cause the immediate remittances
of the determined unremitted contributions and loan amortization due to GSIS,
Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth, if there is any, in compliance with the related laws to
avoid the unnecessary accumulation of interest and penalties.

We recommended that the Accountant immediately submit the December
2024 accounts and henceforth prepare and submit the monthly reports of transactions
together with the complete supporting documents on or before the 10th day of the
following month in accordance with the abovementioned rules and regulations, to
avoid any adverse audit action.

a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to evaluate and
determine the cause/s of the discrepancies and record the necessary adjustments
in their books. Henceforth, both officials shall reconcile their records regularly,
at least quarterly, and adjustments, if any, shall be promptly and appropriately
recorded;
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to promptly take up necessary adjusting
entries for the noted book reconciling items to reflect the correct balances of
pertinent accounts in the financial statements; and
c. Require the Municipal Treasurer to monitor, review, and retrieve the records
on the four non-moving/dormant accounts for possible return to source agencies
or closure and transfer of balances to active accounts and other possible
remedies to avoid unnecessary depletion of the funds due to bank-imposed
charges, if any.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer maintain a permanent printed/hard
copy of the cashbook to record daily all her collections, deposits, and balances, and to reflect
all adjustments, instead of replacing the balances without the proper disclosure on the
changes made.

a. Instruct the Accounting Office to maintain complete, updated, and properly
accomplished PPELCs for each class of PPE, listing therein the data as to its
acquisition, description, custody, useful life, and other necessary
information;
b. Follow the provisions of COA Circular 2020-006 on the one-time cleansing of
PPE account balances and direct the Inventory Committee to complete the
inventory taking of all PPE of the Municipal Government, prepare the Inventory
Count Form, and upon completion of the physical count, prepare the RPCPPE for
submission to the COA Audit Team.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendations that the Local Chief
Executive (LCE) require:
a. The Inventory Committee to conduct an inventory of the local roads under the
LGU’s jurisdiction and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Road
Network System;
b. The Municipal Treasurer/General Services Officer to render a report on the
Local Road Network at the end of the accounting period, to maintain a Local
Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network
Property Card for all roads and its components;
c. The Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant with the
complete description and cost segregation of road components for road
projects;
d. The Municipal Accountant to keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads
and road components for every road network and reconcile the same to the
result of the physical inventory taking at the end of each year; and
e. The Municipal Accountant to include the necessary disclosure on the total road
network system in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that Management:
a. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact and in full all her collections
and/or collections remitted to her by the collectors with the authorized
depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day in compliance with
existing regulations; and
b. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to require all AOs to prepare their individual
MRAAF and submit to her Office for consolidation and attach these reports
with other monthly reports to be submitted to the Accounting Office, which
shall transmit the same to the Office of the Auditor for verification.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Accountable Officers to liquidate/settle their outstanding cash
advances without delay by submitting the duly accomplished liquidation reports
on or before the prescribed period;
a. Prepare and issue demand letters to concerned officers and employees with
outstanding cash advances, requiring them to immediately settle their
unliquidated cash advances. Henceforth, strictly enforce rules and regulations
on the liquidation of cash advances as required in COA Circular No. 97-002;
and
b. Prepare a Request for Authority to Write-off the dormant receivable accounts
amounting to ₱90,310.26 following the procedures provided in Sections 8.2 and
8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management instruct the
GSO/Property Officer and concerned accountable officers/end-user
offices/departments to conduct the inventory of all supplies and materials in their
custody at the end of the year and to prepare the corresponding report certified correct
by the Inventory Committee.
We also recommended that the GSO/Property Officer maintain supply stock
cards and that the Accounting and Property Offices reconcile their records and record
the necessary adjustments to ensure that the Inventory accounts are properly stated in
the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to conduct the necessary analysis of the
accounts and periodic reconciliation of their records to establish the balance
of the Inter-agency accounts and remit the BIR taxes, GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and
PhilHealth premiums due;
b. Require the Human Resource Management Office, in coordination with the
Municipal Treasurer, to ensure that the contractual employees of the
Municipality secure their Pag-IBIG account numbers, as a prerequisite in the
acceptance of remittances, to avoid further accumulation of the unremitted
balances of Due to Pag-IBIG account; and
c. Require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to ensure the
timely remittance of the mandatory deductions to the concerned government
agencies at the end of each month.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that Management require:
a. The concerned offices/departments to implement immediately the various
PPAs funded by the NGAs, if still on-going and warranted;
b. The Accounting Office to:
b.1 Prepare the Fund Utilization Report (FUR) for each program/project
as of December 31, 2024, for submission to the Source Agencies for
proper recording, a copy furnished to the Office of the Auditor; and
b.2 Inquire in writing from the Source Agencies of the idle accounts,
regarding the proper disposition of the balance of fund transfers in the
absence of copies of the pertinent MOAs.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management exert its best
effort to implement all programmed projects under the 20 percent EDF during the
current year to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental
management outcomes essential to the promotion of the general welfare of its
constituents.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, Budget Officer, and
Engineer reconcile their records at year-end to ensure accurate reporting and properly
account for the status of infrastructure projects, particularly those that are delayed or
not yet implemented.

We recommended that Management:
a. Direct the HRMO to ensure that a copy of the duly executed Job Order
contract, together with the monthly accomplishment reports, is verified and
signed by the immediate supervisor, and is attached to the payroll as required
under Section 1.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001. Each Job Order
personnel should likewise be provided with a copy of their respective
contracts;
b. Clearly stipulate in Job Order contracts the fund source, specific work
assignments, duties and responsibilities, expected quantity of work, and
40
desired outputs to enable proper evaluation and monitoring of performance;
c. Direct the HRMO to issue a memorandum instructing approving officers to
write their names below their signatures when certifying DTRs, and to
maintain a logbook for recording actual time in and time out of personnel;
d. Require the HRMO to formulate a Human Resource Plan and issue a
uniform policy guideline on the hiring of JO personnel/contract of service to
determine the necessity of the hiring, and as well as to strengthen the internal
controls;
e. Direct the HRMO to adopt a schedule of standard rates for JOs per position
based on qualification and the nature of work/assignment, taking into
consideration the prevailing minimum wage in the locality, in order not to
create inequality in the workforce of the LGU;
f. Instruct the HRMO to enforce the use of locator slips for all personnel
leaving their assigned stations during office hours, regardless of whether the
purpose is official or personal, to properly account for time and movement
during working hours; and
g. Conduct regular orientation and/or re-orientation sessions for all Job Order
and Contract of Service personnel to reinforce awareness of government
service responsibilities, workplace policies, and attendance requirements.

We recommended that the Management:
a. Intensify efforts to implement approved PPAs included in the Local Disaster
Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) to
ensure that the intended services and benefits are delivered to the community
on time;
b. Evaluate and prioritize PPAs based on immediate needs and urgency to avoid
further non-implementation, particularly the 18 current PPAs amounting to
₱2,956,400.00, as well as those under continuing and prior years’ appropriations
transferred to the Special Trust Fund totaling ₱28,664,095.79;
c. Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to conduct
regular reconciliation of their records to ensure the accurate recording and
reporting of fund status and utilization; and
d. Ensure that the supplemental budget amounting to ₱177,000.00 is properly
recorded as an additional appropriation for the MDRRMF, and submit the
revised and corrected reports to the concerned agency/committee, copy
furnished to the Audit Team for verification.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the LDRRMO to
prepare an Annual Accomplishment Report on the implementation of the PPAs
under the LDRRMF. The report should be aligned with the approved
LDRRMFIP and based on the Report on Sources and Utilization of the DRRMF,
certified by the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Require the officers involved in the formulation and preparation of the GAD
Plan and Budget to conduct regular coordination meetings to reconcile and
finalize a unified GAD Plan and Budget;
b. Direct the Budget Officer and GAD Focal Person to identify and allocate the
required five per cent GAD budget from any supplemental appropriations for
PPAs, in compliance with GAD budgetary requirements;
c. Instruct the concerned officers to maximize the implementation of approved
GAD PPAs to ensure that identified beneficiaries receive the intended services
and are not deprived of access due to non-implementation;
d. Require the GAD Focal Person and other responsible personnel to prepare
the GAD Accomplishment Report in accordance with the approved GAD Plan
and Budget. Any changes should be supported by revised plans and submitted
on time to the appropriate oversight bodies, with copies furnished to the COA
for verification; and
e. Mandate close coordination among the GAD Focal Person, the Budget Office,
and the Planning Office during the planning and accomplishment reporting
stages to ensure consistency and reconciliation of GAD-related data and
outputs;
f. Ensure that the GAD AR and the required accompanying reports such as: 1)
brief summary of the reported program or project; (2) complete results of
HGDG tests; and (3) actions taken by the LGU on the COA audit findings and
recommendations be submitted to the province for review and consolidation
and the DILG Regional Office for the preparation of the annual GAD
accomplishments and furnish a copy to the Auditor’s Office for verification;
and
g. Require the Accounting Office to maintain a separate subsidiary ledger to
record the approved budget and transactions on the implementation of the
GAD budget.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to remind the officers/personnel
in-charge of the formulation of GAD Plan and Budget to evaluate PPAs which are
GAD- related that contribute to poverty alleviation, economic empowerment
especially of marginalized women, protection, promotion, and fulfillment of
women’s human’s rights, and practice of gender-responsiveness with the
corresponding equivalent amount per PPAs.
We also recommended that management cease charging unauthorized or non-
GAD-related purchases and procurements to the GAD Budget, to preserve the
integrity of GAD fund utilization and to ensure compliance with GAD guidelines
and audit regulations.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management direct that
GFPS Profile Form for the newly organized GFPS be accomplished to facilitate the
determination/evaluation on who among the members needs training and what GAD
capacity building programs need to be undertaken. Henceforth, require the GFPS to
attend/undergo trainings to advance the GAD advocacy of the Municipality. The
annual GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipality should consistently include
capacity building for the GFPS members.

We recommended that Management and the LSB strive to fully utilize the
Special Education Fund to fulfill its intended purpose of providing supplementary
budgetary needs of schools and learning centers.
We also recommended that the LSB prepare and submit to the Audit Team the
detailed annual accomplishment report for the Special Education Fund for CY 2024 to
facilitate the validation and verification of the reported utilization of the fund.

We recommended that the Management:
a. Furnish the Audit Team with documents that supported the formulation of
plans and programs chargeable against SEF for verification and evaluation;
b. Provide the LSB with complete, timely, and accurate reports, data, and
information as the basis for the preparation of budget estimates, in accordance
with DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017;
c. Inform the LSB of the actual unexpended balances, including previous years,
for the preparation of PPAs that would cater to the priority needs of public
schools and students; and
d. Instruct the LSB to identify the specific beneficiary schools in the approved
SEF Plan and Budget.

We recommended that the Local School Board/concerned officials give attention and
priority to the following critical areas: (a) the operation and maintenance of public
schools, and (b) the construction and repair of school buildings, facilities, and
equipment, particularly those that support and promote the delivery of quality basic
education.
We also recommend that the School Board judiciously identify and prioritize
programs and projects to be funded for the upcoming year, ensuring that planned
interventions are implemented within the year of appropriation and immediately
benefit the intended stakeholders.
Further, we strongly recommended that the LSB formally raise the concern on the
shortage of school buildings to higher authorities, such as the DepEd Regional Office,
to seek additional support and resources for urgent infrastructure needs.

We recommended that Management:
a. Ensure that all expected sources of revenue are properly projected and
reflected during the budget preparation process to provide reasonable
assurance of the availability of funds necessary for the implementation of
planned PPAs;
b. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to conduct a quarterly review of collection
accomplishment reports to assess actual performance against targets and
formulate strategies or remedial measures to improve revenue collection
efficiency;
c. Require the Municipal Treasurer, as the supervisor of the Local Revenue
Collecting Officers (LRCO) and in-Charge of the Market, to supervise proper
implementation of internal controls in revenue collection, particularly in the
Market and Slaughterhouse, such as:
For slaughterhouse –
1. Assign and orient the staff in charge of the collection to the proper and
systematic collection procedures;
2. Instruct the collector to collect the fees on the day of the use of the facility
or as soon as the services are rendered;
For market –
1. Ensure the timely collection of Market Stall Rentals which is on or before
the 5th day of the month;
2. Conduct inspection and identify the lessee with overdue accounts for
cancellation of contract;
3. Make an announcement or information to all lessee/stall vendors of the
imposition of penalty and interest on late payment, in compliance with
60
the Local Revenue Code;
d. Direct the Treasurer’s Office to coordinate with the Accounting Office on the
use of account titles, grouping of account titles or sub-titles, particularly on
Income Accounts, as stipulated in Section 10, Chapter 2 of the Manual of
NGAS for Local Government Units, for efficiency of recording and
preparation of collection reports.

We recommended that Management together with the Sangguniang Bayan give
priority to the revision and updating of the 2013 Revenue Code of the Municipality,
according to the provisions of the Local Government Code, to increase tax revenues
and receipts through updating of the rates in the existing tax code and provision for
new revenue sources, which will augment their budget for a better delivery of
government services.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that Management instruct the
Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Planning Officer to prepare a separate
budgetary allocation for LEEs of the Municipality.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare a complete set
of FS for each of the Municipality’s economic enterprises, which shall be attached to
the General Fund trial balance, to provide adequate information on the financial
position and performance of the enterprises.

However, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Property Officer to ensure that all properties have complete and readable asset tags for easy identification and preparing and placing property tags for those assets without tags or unreadable tags.
Likewise, we recommended that the MAO and GSO complete the details needed in the CIP Ledger Cards, RPLCs, and PCs.

However, we recommended that Management:
Require all AOs to liquidate/settle their outstanding cash advances without delay by submitting the duly accomplished liquidation reports on or before the reglementary period; and
Consider appointing/designating additional DOs to have better management of funds and to avoid overlapping cash advances.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GSO:
To conduct proper inventory planning to ensure that inventory procurement aligns with the operational needs and supplies are available to meet at least one quarter’s requirement;
Maintain a stockroom to properly safekeep inventory items, preventing damages and loss; and
Regular monitoring of the procurement and availability of the inventory to avoid possible stock shortages.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the earnest effort to reconcile with the lending banks to identify the transaction that resulted in negative balances and prepare the necessary adjusting entry.

We recommended that Management require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and GSO to submit the copies of Contracts/POs to the AT, together with the required supporting documents, within five days after perfection thereof, and the related Notification of Delivery of Services/Supplies and Materials, within 24 hours after acceptance of such deliveries of services/supplies and materials, in compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular 96-010.

We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to identify the status and establish the correct balance of the Due to NGAs account for each SA and thereafter:
Return/refund to the concerned SA the unutilized balance of the fund transfer wherein the agreement requires the return thereof;
Revert to the Government Equity the balances of the fund transfers in the nature of financial assistance and subsidy wherein the agreement does not mention the disposition of the unused balance and that future utilization thereof will be subject to the usual budgeting, accounting and auditing laws, rules and regulations; and
Retain special purpose funds.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to review and analyze the unexpended balances of appropriations of completed projects for reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund in accordance with Section 322 of RA No. 7160.
We also recommended that Management revert to the General Fund for re-appropriation of all excess balances of projects already implemented.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the members of the BAC, BAC Secretariat, and its Technical Working Group (TWG) to conduct a detailed review of the actual procurement process and exert effort to align the same with the ideal procurement timelines set forth under Annex “C” of the amended IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to require the contractors to post performance securities to cover the period until issuance of the Certificate of Final Acceptance as a condition before the signing of contracts.

We commended the MGC for establishing written guidelines to address the necessity and propriety of hiring COS/JO personnel. These guidelines help ensure a fair, transparent, and standardized hiring process that aligns with MGC’s operational needs. Additionally, we recommended that MGC’s Human Resources Office conduct periodic evaluations of these guidelines to ensure continued relevance and effectiveness.

We recommended that the LDRRM Council (LDRRMC) to:
Monitor the utilization of the LDRRMF unexpended balance and donations, and incorporate all available funds in the LDRRMFIP;
Refrain from incurring and charging expenses unrelated to disaster risk reduction and management activities to the LDRRMF pursuant to RA No. 10121; and
Ensure that QRF is used for the intended purpose in accordance with the abovementioned guidelines.

We commended the LSB for the substantial utilization of SEF, thus ensuring the immediate realization of identified priority PPAs.  Nevertheless, we recommended that the LSB implement fully all PPAs targeted during the year within the timeframe to ensure optimal utilization of the SEF.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the ISWMB to periodically review the solid waste management plan and conduct in-depth research on the possible factors contributing to the increasing waste and the non-attainment of proper waste segregation. Furthermore, the updates should include the impact of the pandemic and strategies to address the challenges of the new normal era.
We recommended that the MGC provide the necessary funds for the implementation of the proposed investment projects targeted in the approved 10-year SWMP.

We recommended that Management instruct the GFPS to:
Send member/s of the GFPS to training on how to use monitoring tools such as HGDG;
Identify GAD activities using HGDG; update regularly the GAD Plan and identified projects to address the GAD issues and concerns in the Municipality; and
Ensure that at least five per cent of the municipal government’s total budget is appropriated for GAD.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System continue to develop and implement GAD-related programs/projects/activities that advocate women empowerment, promote gender equality, and improve the welfare of women, and ensure that planned GAD PPAs are all timely implemented based on its approved GAD Plan and Budget.

However, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant:
to closely monitor the timely and complete remittance of all withholding taxes;
prepare the necessary adjusting entry to correct the noted double recording of due to BIR, and
immediately remit the unremitted withholding taxes.
We recommended that the MA continue her effort to reconcile the records with GSIS for the remaining balance of ₱28,325.05, which may pertain to arrears for CYs 2010 and below.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Accountant and designated AAO to continue the reconciliation of previous years’ records with HDMF; and
Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to immediately refund or remit all the premium contributions from prior years after the reconciliation of records is made.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Accountant to reconcile previous years’ records with PHIC; and
Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to immediately remit all premium contributions from prior years after the reconciliation of records is made.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer conduct immediate reconciliation of the individual balance of the Cash Local Treasury and Cash in Bank – LCCA account to make the necessary adjustments thereon.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
Adhere to the existing laws, rules and regulations of proper recognition of inventories in the books of accounts;
Strictly observe the perpetual inventory method in accounting for the acquisition and issuance of supplies and materials; and
Record the issuance of supplies and materials to the related expense account/s based on the correct SSMI.
Likewise, the Management requires the Property and Supply Officer to:
Prepare and maintain SCs for inventories, which shall be regularly reconciled with the SLCs of the Office of the Municipal Accountant; and
Consolidate weekly the RIS for which supplies and materials were issued using the SSMI and submit the same, together with the original copies of RIS, to the Office of the Municipal Accountant; and
Conduct periodic physical inventory of supplies or property, which shall be conducted semestrally and reported in the RPCI and be submitted to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the heads of the Municipal Accounting Office, Property, and Supply Office and the Inventory Committee to step up their commitment and efforts to comply with the conduct of annual physical inventory of all its properties and its reconciliation with the books and records of the Accounting and Property Office, to ensure a reliable balance of PPE accounts in the FS.
We further recommended that the concerned offices update the PPELCs, PCs, and the related AREs and reconcile the contents and balances to facilitate monitoring and verification.

We recommended that Management:
Recompute the depreciation of PPE considering the residual value of five per cent of the cost, or a justified estimate where applicable; and
Adjust the accounting records accordingly to reflect the correct depreciation and asset values.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor:
Require all AOs to liquidate/settle their outstanding cash advances without delay by submitting the duly accomplished liquidation reports on or before the expiration of the reglementary period;
Direct the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) to strictly ensure that no additional cash advances will be granted to accountable/disbursing officers with outstanding balances, following Section 89 of PD 1445; and
Consider appointing/designating additional DOs to have better management of funds and to avoid overlapping cash advances.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to:
conduct a comprehensive review of all aged items recorded in Accounts Payable; and
identify and revert to Accumulated Surplus those items that no longer qualify as valid liabilities, in compliance with applicable accounting standards and regulations.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to identify the status and establish the correct balance of the Due to NGA’s account for each SA, and thereafter:
Return/refund to the concerned SA the unutilized balance of the fund transfer, wherein the agreement requires the return thereof;
Revert to the Government Equity the balances of the fund transfers in the nature of financial assistance and subsidy wherein the agreement does not mention the disposition of the unused balance, and that future utilization thereof will be subject to the usual budgeting, accounting, and auditing laws, rules and regulations; and
Retain special- purpose funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare the necessary adjusting entry to reclassify the aforementioned transactions to their proper accounts; and
Thoroughly review transactions and use the appropriate account in recording.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to submit promptly the required monthly and quarterly financial reports on or before the due dates in compliance with Item 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010.

We recommended that Management require the employee in charge and the General Services Officer to submit the copies of Contracts/POs to the AT, together with the required supporting documents, within five days after perfection thereof, and the related Notification of Delivery of Services/Supplies and Materials, within 24 hours after acceptance of such deliveries of services/supplies and materials, in compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular 96-010.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer, together with the Municipal Accountant, to undertake a comprehensive and careful analysis of source of funds and ensure that funds are actually available prior to certifying the Statement if Revenue/Income/Savings as fund source of supplemental budgets.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to properly record the fund transfers in the Receivable account, while the Expense account shall be recognized only upon receipt of liquidation reports, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the LDRRM Council (LDRRMC):
Monitor the utilization of the LDRRMF unexpended balance and donations, and incorporate all available funds in the LDRRMFIP;
Refrain from incurring and charging expenses unrelated to disaster risk reduction and management activities to the LDRRMF pursuant to RA No. 10121; and
Ensure that QRF is used for the intended purpose in accordance with the abovementioned guidelines.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System continue to develop and implement GAD-related programs/projects/activities that advocate women empowerment, promote gender equality, improve the welfare of women, and ensure that planned GAD PPAs are all timely implemented based on its approved GAD Plan and Budget.
We recommended that the LSB implement fully all PPAs targeted during the year within the timeframe to ensure optimal utilization of the SEF.
We recommended that the MGG require the LSB to submit the aforementioned reports within the timelines set under Section 6.1 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended that Management:
Carefully carry out a feasibility/preliminary engineering study and detailed engineering to ensure that issues such as technical viability of the project, road right of way, and coordination with other government agencies relative to the issuance of required permits/clearances are properly addressed to ensure smooth implementation of projects;
Thoroughly review and evaluate the Program of Work to ensure that project designs and estimates are properly prepared and that all phases of the project are covered to minimize variation orders and time extensions, which often result in increased cost and delayed project completion; and
Submit a request for inspection, together with supporting documents, to the AT to facilitate a timely auditorial review and inspection of the project.

We recommended that the Office of the Vice-Mayor ensure adherence to mandated functions defined under Article II, Section 445 of RA No. 7160 and refrain from initiating or funding projects outside its authority and functions.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant stop the practice of reverting outstanding checks in the Cash in Bank – LCCA accounts and prepare only journal entry vouchers for valid reconciling items that require adjustment and correction in the General Ledger.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant, and Municipal Treasurer to exercise strict control over the disposition of MGJ's funds and limit expenditures to available appropriations and cash.

We recommended that MGJ require the Municipal Accountant to:
Ensure that prior cash advance was liquidated before issuing another cash advance by strictly monitoring the submission of the liquidation reports;
consider appointing/designating an additional Disbursing Officer to have better management of funds and to avoid overlapping cash advances; and
strictly comply with the provisions of the above-mentioned circulars.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
Adhere to the existing laws, rules and regulations of proper recognition of inventories in the books of accounts;
Strictly observe the perpetual inventory method in accounting for the acquisition and issuance of supplies and materials;
Prepare and maintain SLCs for each commodity/stock; and
Record the issuance of supplies and materials to the related expense account/s based on the SSMI.
Likewise, the Management requires the Property and Supply Officer to:
Prepare and maintain SCs for inventories, which shall be regularly reconciled with the SLCs of the Office of the Municipal Accountant; and
Consolidate weekly the RIS for which supplies and materials were issued using the SSMI and submit the same, together with the original copies of RIS, to the Office of the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require periodic physical inventory of supplies or property, which shall be conducted semestrally and reported in the RPCI and be submitted to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.

We recommended that management:
Recompute the depreciation of PPE considering the residual value of ten per cent of the cost, or a justified estimate where applicable; and
Adjust the accounting records accordingly to reflect the corrected depreciation and asset values.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to identify the status and establish the correct balance of the Due to NGA’s account for each SA, and thereafter:
Return/refund to the concerned SA the unutilized balance of the fund transfer, wherein the agreement requires the return thereof;
Revert to the Government Equity the balances of the fund transfers in the nature of financial assistance and subsidy wherein the agreement does not mention the disposition of the unused balance and that future utilization thereof will be subject to the usual budgeting, accounting, and auditing laws, rules and regulations; and
Retain special- purpose funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare the necessary adjusting entry to reclassify the aforementioned transactions to its proper accounts; and
Thoroughly review transactions and use the appropriate account in recording.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to submit promptly the required monthly and quarterly financial reports on or before the due dates in compliance with Item 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010.

We recommended that Management require the employee in charge and the General Services Officer to submit the copies of Contracts/POs to the AT, together with the required supporting documents, within five days after perfection thereof, and the related Notification of Delivery of Services/Supplies and Materials, within 24 hours after acceptance of such deliveries of services/supplies and materials, in compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular 96-010.

We recommended that Management require:
All requesting units to accomplish the PRs properly and include the specific purpose of the requisition;
The BAC to review the RFQs and Abstract of Quotations and Awards for completeness of vital information; and
Include all the prescribed basic data in preparing the POs.

We recommended that Management require the JO workers to apply for Pag-IBIG MID and immediately inform the accounting office for remittance purposes.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Accountant and designated Authorized Agency Officer (AAO) to make a reconciliation of previous years’ records with HDMF; and
Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to immediately remit all unremitted premium contributions after the reconciliation of records is made.

We recommended that the LDRRMC ensure full utilization of the LDRRMF unexpended balance by: (a) closely monitoring its usage, and (b) developing additional PPAs to incorporate all available funds into the LDRRMFIP.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System continue to develop and implement GAD -related programs/projects/activities that advocate women empowerment, promote gender equality, and improve the welfare of women, and ensure that planned GAD PPAs are all timely implemented based on its approved GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that the Local School Board (LSB) review and approve the subsequent year's budget through an LSB Resolution no later than October 31 of the current year, as stipulated under Item 5.5 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017, to prevent the need for advance procurement.
We recommended that the MGJ strengthen the budget controls to ensure that transactions align with the approved SEF Budget.
We recommended that the MGJ require the LSB to submit the aforementioned reports within the timelines set under Section 6.1 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to:
Ensure that payment of financial assistance to Barangays and SK shall be made in the name of the barangay/SK concerned; and
Require that the recipient barangay/SK issue an Official Receipt (AF51) to acknowledge the receipt of financial assistance.

We commended the Management’s active efforts in the conduct of actual
physical inventory of the Municipality’s PPE and ongoing reconciliation of the
Municipality’s property records.
However, we recommended that the Management:
a. Direct the GSO to fast-track the reconciliation of inventory records with
Accounting records, which is aligned with the guidelines of COA Circular No.
2020-006 for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to establish PPE
balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition, and accountability;
b. Instruct the GSO and Accounting Office to exhaust all efforts to determine the
details of the PPEs with unidentified lump sum balances in order that the items
and balances appearing in the RPCPPE and Accounting records may be
ascertained;
c. Instruct the Inventory Committee to prepare and submit the final RPCPPE
and other relevant forms;
d. Require the GSO and Accounting Office to maintain complete Property Cards
and PPELCs based on the final RPCPPE and in the form prescribed by the NGAS
Manual; and
e. Augment the personnel charged with reconciliation of property records, if
feasible, and enjoin the concerned department heads to attend relevant
trainings, if there’s any, on the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to
further to capacitate them and to be able to adopt and follow the procedures set
forth therein and eventual compliance with the requirements thereof on
reporting as prescribed under COA Circular 2020-006.

We recommended that Management consider hiring additional staff in the
Municipal Treasurer’s Office to lessen the workload of the accountable officer in-
charge of recording the daily collections and remittances, to enable the timely and
accurate recording of transactions, and ensure that the cashbook is reconciled
regularly.
We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer’s Office and the
Municipal Accounting Office conduct a quarterly reconciliation of the cashbook and
accounting balances to ensure the fair presentation of the Cash in Local Treasury
account in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to
regularly follow- up with the AGDB to obtain copies of bank statements promptly.
Once received, the Municipal Treasurer ensure that these bank statements are
immediately forwarded to the Municipal Accountant for the timely preparation of
BRS.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit
the BRS within ten days after the receipt of bank statements.

We recommended that Management:
a) Require the immediate liquidation of the outstanding cash advances and submit
the liquidation report or proof of refund/s, if there is any, to the Audit Team for
post-audit;
b) Send Demand Letters to the concerned Accountable Officer(s), Agency
Officials, and employees with unliquidated cash advances and withhold the
salaries and/or any amount due to those Accountable Officers who were unable
to comply with the pertinent rules and regulations on cash advances.

We recommended that management fast-track the cleansing of the dormant
receivables covered by COA Circular 2023-008, which are maintained in the agency’s
books, to ensure the fair presentation of the receivable accounts in the financial
statements.
We further recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant prepare and
submit the quarterly SDA in the prescribed format to the Audit Team, and ensure that
receivables less than ten years old are properly documented and promptly acted upon
to avoid becoming dormant.

We reiterated the prior year’s recommendations that Management:
a. Consider hiring additional staff to assist the Municipal General Services Office
(MGSO) in effectively carrying out its functions, specifically in the LGU’s
supply and property management;
b. Require the Inventory Committee to conduct a semestral physical inventory of
all undistributed and unconsumed inventories of the Municipality and to
prepare and submit the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of
Inventories (RPCI) to the Audit Team for verification;
c. Require the MGSO or the designated stock custodian to prepare and maintain
stock cards that record the receipt and issuance of inventories under their
custody. These records should include complete and accurate information to
facilitate verification of receipts, issuances, and balances of inventories on hand.
Custody of the inventory items shall remain with the MGSO until their official
issuance to the requesting party or department;
d. Require the MGSO to consolidate the RIS and prepare the SSMI on a weekly
basis, and submit the same to the Municipal Accountant. The Municipal
Accountant shall then compute the cost of supplies issued and the ending
inventory, and prepare the corresponding journal entries to record the
expenditures; and
e. Require the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) to prepare and maintain
complete and updated Supplies Ledger Cards (SLCs), and to conduct regular
reconciliations with the RPCI and the Stock Cards (SCs) maintained by the
MGSO at least quarterly. This will ensure the accuracy of inventory account
balances. The Municipal Accountant should take up the necessary adjustments
to ensure that the Inventory accounts are properly stated in the financial
statements.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to
recognize the accrued interest expenses at each year-end prior to the closing of the
books. If the books have already been closed, the necessary adjusting entry should be
made by debiting Prior Period Adjustment (Interest Expense) and crediting Interest
Payable in the amount of ₱1,110,126.45, or based on the accrued interest reflected in
the amortization schedule provided by the bank. Furthermore, it is recommended that
the modified accrual basis of accounting be strictly observed in recording such
expenses to ensure the accuracy of account balances at the end of each reporting
period.
Henceforth, we recommended that Management proactively secure updated
amortization schedules from the bank whenever changes in interest are noted in the
Municipality’s bank account.

We recommended that Management:
a. Direct the Municipal Accounting Office to immediately transmit to the Audit
Team the unsubmitted check disbursements and cash disbursement
transactions for CY 2024; and
b. Direct the Municipal Treasurer and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to strictly
comply with the timelines on the recording and submission of accounts to the
Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.

We commended the Management for optimally utilizing its 20 per cent EDF,
thus attaining its commitment to its constituents to deliver the benefits that can be
derived from the projects that were implemented.
However, we recommended that Management continue to pursue improved
practices in programming, prioritizing, and implementing programs and projects to
maximize the utilization of the Economic Development Fund in attaining desirable
socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes for the
benefit of the community.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Require the LDRRMC to include a separate section in the LDRRMFIP that
lists the projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of the
previous years, for review and approval of the Sangguniang Panlungsod; and
b. Require the MDRRMO to submit the LDRRMFIP and monthly Report on
Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF to the OCD and the DILG-LGOO.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management strictly
adhere to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and 2016-01 on gender
mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD
monitoring and preparation of GAD AR. Moreover, to revisit their programs/projects –
giving more attention on maximizing of the GAD funds through the implementation
of GAD PPAs to attain the objective for which the funds were provided; and to stop
inclusion of programs, projects and activities that are not GAD related such as
disbursement of utility bills, wages of JOs and travelling expenses that are regular
activity of the LGU.
Furthermore, we recommended that Management strictly adhere to the
approved budgets in implementing GAD PPAs to prevent overcharging of actual
expenditures and ensure efficient and compliant utilization of the GAD Fund.

We recommended that Management, through its GFPS/TWG, instruct the
GAD FPS to strictly observe the timeline in the preparation and submission of GAD
PB and GAD AR, along with the complete documentary requirements, pursuant to
PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-001 and COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated
March 18, 2024. Likewise, for the GFPS/TWG to review and monitor these reports,
ensuring the accuracy of the amounts to maintain the integrity of the data presented.

We recommended that the Management to create or assign a Responsibility
Center (RC) for their GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), or maintain a subsidiary ledger
for GAD for each account and assign SL code, to facilitate, properly account, and
monitor GAD related expenses and other GAD-related financial transactions for the
Municipality’s GAD funds.

We commended the Municipal Government for fully utilizing the LGSF-SBDP-
NTF-ELCAC, thereby addressing the existing issues and development gaps at the
barangay level, which greatly benefited their constituents.
However, we recommended that Management require the LFC to post the
quarterly report in at least three conspicuous public places within the locality.
Following the posting, a written notice should be sent to the Secretary of DBM,
Secretary of DILG, Chairperson of the NTF-ELCAC, coursed through the NTF-
ELCAC Vice-Chairperson and RTF-ELCAC concerned. Speaker of the House of
Representatives, President of the Senate of the Philippines, and the Chairpersons of
the House Committee on Appropriations and Senate Committee on Finance in
consonance with Sections 5.3 and 5.4 of LBC No. 151.

We recommended that Management:
a. Secure copies of documents supporting the acquisition of the RoW and submit
to the Audit Team for verification; and
b. Strictly comply with Section 17.6 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 and ensure
that the acquisition of the right of way for future infrastructure projects not
under the design and build scheme is properly carried out.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Treasurer, and other concerned Department Heads to prepare and submit to the
Sangguniang Bayan for approval, a consolidated proposed adjustment on tax rates to
fully exercise the LGU’s taxing power, pursuant to the above-cited provisions of the
Code, to increase tax revenues and receipts through updating of the rates in the
existing tax code, which will augment the budget provision for a better delivery of
government service.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Municipal Treasurer’s Office and other Departments involved in
generating revenues to meet its targets by conducting periodic review of
collection performance to ascertain whether collection targets are attained and
to analyze the reasons for the shortfall in collections and find alternative
solutions to minimize, reduce, or avoid the incurrence of a shortfall in the
future;
b. Require the Municipal Budget Office to provide targets for revenue accounts
without projected revenues by setting targets based on statistics, prior years’
experiences, and future expectations; and
c. Require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to accomplish the
Quarterly Report of Income (LBAc Form No. 1) periodically to monitor the
status of revenue generation and at the same time adopt measures that will
likely improve collection for accounts not reaching the targets, thus assure that
more plans and programs will be fully implemented.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to devise and implement strategies
to enhance the collection efficiency of the municipality’s revenue sources;
b. Direct the concerned offices to expedite the repair or replacement of the
municipality’s submersible pumps to restore adequate water supply, sustain
public trust, and improve constituents’ willingness to settle their water bills;
and
c. Conduct a periodic review of accomplishments to have an assurance that all
revenue sources are tapped to maximize its revenue- generating powers as
mandated by law.

We recommended that Management require the Bids and Awards Committee
to post the APP and the PMR in three conspicuous places within the LGU.
We further recommended that Management submit an electronic copy of the
APP to the GPPB on or before January 31 of the current year and the PMR on or
before the 14th day after the end of each semester, including a certification confirming
that the APP and PMR has been posted on the agency's website or in a conspicuous
place within the LGU.

We recommended that Management consider the use of the ATM facilities
available at the AGSBs in paying the salaries and wages of the Job Order Personnel as
well as the bonuses to be received by the permanent/regular employees of the
Municipality, to safeguard government funds against unnecessary risk of loss through
theft, misuse or misappropriation, and improve cash management to ensure efficient
and economical use of government funds.
We also recommended that Management request the Municipal Accountant,
Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Human Resource and Management Officer to
coordinate with the Agency’s AGSBs for the use of their ATM service facilities in
paying the payroll of the LGU.

We recommended that Management:
a. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to strictly comply with the requirement of the
DPWH letter dated February 24, 2025, and henceforth remit the five per cent
share derived from the collection of building permit fees and other charges to
the account of the DPWH Secretary under Special Account No. 154 through the
Bureau of the Treasury, as required under the National Building Code of the
Philippines; and
b. Immediately notify the DPWH Secretary through the NBCDO upon remittance
of the five per cent DPWH share.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to
reconcile the Due to BIR account, identify the taxes withheld due for remittance, and
immediately remit the same in compliance with the related laws and regulations.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to
make an analysis and reconciliation of the Due to GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and PhilHealth
accounts and cause the immediate remittances of the determined unremitted
contributions and loan amortization due to GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and PhilHealth in
compliance with the related laws to avoid unnecessary accumulation of interest and
penalties.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to adjust the books to accurately reflect the issuance of semi-expendable inventories in compliance with COA Circular No. 2022-004 and IPSAS 12;
Require the GSO and MAO to use and maintain the prescribed forms, ledgers, registries, and reports to properly account the semi-expendable items; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to record the succeeding issuance of semi-expendable inventories to the respective offices as expenses based on the RSPI prepared by the GSO.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to properly account for unreleased checks at year-end to ensure the fair presentation of the Cash in Bank – LCCA in the financial statements;
Assess the dormant trust fund account for GBPB and enforce the reversion of the LGU’s Counterpart to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund for reprogramming; and
Strengthen internal control and implement procedures for regular review, reconciliation, and updating of all bank accounts and related records to ensure compliance with IPSAS 1 on the fair presentation of account balances.

We recommended that Management:
Re-evaluate whether the income items totaling ₱206,500.00 entail actual obligations of the LGU. If no corresponding liability exists, require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify these amounts as revenues of the General Fund in accordance with IPSAS 1; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to record only funds with specific legal or contractual purposes under the Trust Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the necessary journal entries in the Trust Fund books to account the transfer of the unspent DRRMF and to reclassify donations received for DRRM purposes to the Trust Liabilities – DRRMF account.
We also recommended that the Municipal DRRM Officer (MDRRMO) incorporate in the CY 2025 LDRRMF Investment Plan and/or its supplemental plan, under a separate caption, the proposed PPAs to be funded from the CY 2024 DRRMF balance and unaccounted DRRM donations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the complete set of financial statements promptly to facilitate a timely audit and enable the Management to timely asses the financial information needed in decision-making activities.

We reiterated our prior-year recommendation that Management:
Impose firm policies requiring the officials/employees to liquidate cash advances within the reglementary period in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to issue a written reminder within ten days before the expiration of the 30-day liquidation period, duly signed by the head of the agency or authorized representative, enjoining the official/employee to liquidate their travel cash advance. Otherwise, withhold the salary of the official/employee until such time that the cash advance has been fully liquidated, pursuant to Item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Item 3.3.2(b) of COA Circular No. 96-004.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Designate a Secretariat and/or additional members to support the Inventory/Disposal Committee;
Provide a designated storage facility to temporarily accommodate the unserviceable items pending disposal; and
Instruct the Inventory/Disposal Committee to conduct inspection, appraisal, and disposal of unserviceable properties in compliance with the procedures outlined in the Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct:
The Municipal Accountant and the HRMA to conduct the analysis and reconciliation of the Due to Pag-IBIG account to determine the correct amount due and validate entries pertaining to employees without MID numbers, and facilitate the immediate registration of said employees with the Fund; and
The Municipal Treasurer to remit the full amount of contributions withheld from employees’ salaries, together with the corresponding government share, in accordance with the prescribed schedule under HDMF Circular No. 275.

We recommended that Management:
Expedite the execution of MOAs with the concerned barangays to facilitate the transfer of funds for the implementation of augmentation projects;
Instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Engineer to evaluate the project readiness, including verification of legal site clearance, ownership documentation, completeness of required POW, prior to its inclusion in the Annual Investment Program (AIP); and
Ensure that all proposed priority development projects included in the AIP are procurement- and implementation- ready.

We recommended that Management:
Require the MDRRMO to plan the utilization of unexpended LDRRMF balance from CY 2021 and the cash donations, and incorporate the identified PPAs in the LDRRMFIP for implementation; and
Require the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer, and MDRRMO to strengthen coordination to ensure timely monitoring, planning, and utilization of the LDRRMF.

We recommended that the LCE direct the MDRRMO to ensure that the use of prior years’ unexpended LDRRMF and any realignment in the current year appropriations are properly covered by a supplemental LDRRMP/LDRRMFIP, supplemental AIP, and supplemental budgets, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-002 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the Municipal Accountant and MDRRMO to establish a regular coordination mechanism to ensure the preparation of monthly utilization reports; and
Require the MDRRMO to submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF to the Audit Team and OCD on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management direct the GAD Focal Person and require the Municipal Planning and Development Office to spearhead the collection of data/information necessary to establish a comprehensive GAD database. We also recommended that Management include an adequate budget in the GPB for this activity and the additional manpower to handle GAD-related duties.

We recommended that the Management ensure full compliance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 by allocating at least five per cent of the total LGU budget for GAD activities and to include the Local Economic Enterprises budget in the GAD budget computation.

We recommended that Management, through the GFPS Executive Committee, in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Office (MPDO) and Municipal Budget Office (MBO):
Conduct a thorough review and proper costing of proposed PPAs to align resource requirements with the Municipality’s capacity and ensure the efficient utilization of GAD funds; and
Ensure timely implementation of GAD PPAs by improving coordination among responsible offices, setting implementation timelines, and assigning accountable personnel for each program.

We recommended that Management and the Local School Board ensure that the prescribed planning and budgeting activities are conducted within the required timeline for the preparation, review, and approval of the proposed SEF Budget to fully comply with the pertinent provisions of DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended that Management direct the MPMIU to:
Implement strategies to encourage greater participation and commitment from the member-beneficiaries;
Actively search marketing networks and channels and assist the Enterprise in accessing the same; and
Prepare the Enterprise Operations Monitoring Report.
We also recommended that Management pass an ordinance providing for the funds, facilities, services, and other resources required for the operations and maintenance of the Enterprise for the remaining six years of the ten-year period after subproject completion and furnish the DA-RFO with copies of the Appropriation Ordinance.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Submit the required supporting documents for the claims recorded under the Accounts Payable account, such as Delivery Receipts, Inspection and Acceptance Reports, and Billing Statements, to establish their validity and propriety; and
Strengthen internal controls and ensure complete and proper documentation in the recording of payables, in compliance with IPSAS 19.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the discrepancy of ₱156,460.00 between the reported balance in the financial statements and subsidiary ledgers, and prepare the necessary adjusting entries. We also recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office establish a regular reconciliation schedule and strengthen internal controls to ensure the integrity and accuracy of financial accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Inquire in writing from the Source Agencies, if known, the intended purpose of the fund transfers and whether the unutilized funds should be returned to the Source Agency; and
b. Exert effort in tracing relevant information such as the date of receipt of funds, Source Agencies, the intended purpose of the funds, and the status of implementation of the PPAs funded by the transfers.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the complete set of financial statements promptly to facilitate a timely audit and enable the Management to timely assess the financial information needed in decision-making activities.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer:
Strictly require the collecting officer/s to turn over their collections daily in accordance with the guidelines prescribed in the NGAS Manual for LGUs;
Verify the collections and accountable forms of each collecting officer as reported in the RCD; and
Deposit intact all the collections turned over to her with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer require the Collecting Officers to prepare and submit the individual Monthly RAAFs not later than the fifth day of the ensuing month for consolidation by the local treasurer in accordance with the NGAS Manual.

We reiterated our prior-year recommendation that Management impose strict policies and require the officers and employees to liquidate within the reglementary period in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002. Otherwise, withhold the salary of the official/employee until such time that the travel cash advance has been fully liquidated, pursuant to Item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Item 3.3.2(b) of COA Circular No. 96-004.

We recommended that Management:
Enforce strict compliance in the proper completion of all required fields in procurement documents – particularly the “Date of receipt by supplier” and “Delivery Terms” in the Notice of Award, Purchase Order, and Notice to Proceed; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to verify delivery compliance during the processing of disbursement vouchers and impose liquidated damages, if warranted, in accordance with the terms and conditions of the contract and the provision of Item 3.1 of Annex D of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the remaining balance of ₱71,432.28 under the Due to BIR account and immediately remit the amount to avoid penalties.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the balance of ₱12,264.00 under the Due to Pag-IBIG account and ensure the timely remittance of Pag-IBIG premiums and loans.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the balance of ₱135,390.39 under the Due to PhilHealth account and ensure the timely remittance of PhilHealth contributions.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Engineering Office to expedite the preparation of Detailed Engineering Designs (DEDs), and, if necessary, engage additional technical personnel through hiring or a contract of service, to ensure the timely procurement and implementation of PPAs under the 20 per cent DF; and
Instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Engineer to evaluate and reassess the unimplemented projects under continuing appropriations to determine whether these are still responsive to the existing needs of its constituents; otherwise, initiate reprogramming toward more viable and significant projects aligned with the socio-economic needs of the Municipality.
We also recommended that the Municipal Development Council:
Evaluate and plan thoroughly the proposed priority development projects to be included in the AIP and ensure that these are procurement- and implementation- ready within the appropriation year; and
Monitor and evaluate the implementation of projects so that any issues can be promptly identified and addressed.

We recommended that Management:
Revise the Contracts of Service to clearly define the scope of work, expected outputs, performance indicators, and require the submission of accomplishment reports and DTRs or equivalent documents to validate actual services rendered; and
Review the existing COS engagements in the Offices of the Mayor and the Vice Mayor, and evaluate whether they are necessary.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant and MDRRMO to submit the necessary documents such as the subsidiary ledger of Trust Liability – DRRMF account, pertinent registry, MDRRMC resolutions, approved supplemental AIP, and corresponding supplemental budgets, and relevant sanggunian ordinances;
Direct the MDRRMO to prepare the LDRRMFIP, incorporating the PPAs to be charged against the unexpended balances of LDRRMF from previous years; and
Ensure that, henceforth, the use of prior years’ unexpended LDRRMF, including funds received from other sources, is properly covered by relevant resolutions, supplemental LDRRMP/LDRRMFIP, supplemental AIP, and supplemental budgets, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-002 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Acting-Municipal Treasurer to undertake the actual cash transfer of the unexpended CY 2024 balance of the LDRRMF in the General Fund to the Trust Fund books, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the actual cash transfer in CY 2025 using the appropriate accounts and, henceforth, ensure the completeness of entries in recording the transfer of unspent DRRMF at year-end.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal DRRM Officer to establish a regular coordination mechanism to ensure the preparation of monthly utilization reports; and
The MDRRMO submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF to the Audit Team and OCD on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management direct the GAD Focal Person and Municipal Planning and Development Office to spearhead the collection of data/information necessary to establish a comprehensive GAD database. We also recommended that Management allocate an adequate budget in the GPB for this activity and the additional manpower to handle GAD-related duties.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the LSB, to prepare and submit the required quarterly and annual SEF utilization reports to the oversight agencies within the prescribed timeline; and
Evaluate the possibility of designating additional staff and implementing internal controls to guarantee the timely preparation and submission of required reports.

However, we recommended that:
a) The Property Custodian Designee comply with the required format in the
preparation of RPCPPE and incorporate the essential details in the said report as a result
of the actual physical count; and
b) Management carefully plan the procedures and timelines for the “One-Time
Cleansing” of PPE account balances and initiate compliance with the requirements set
forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that:
a) The Property Custodian Designee prepare the IIRUP, WMR, and PTR or
equivalent document, and accomplish the pertinent portions to be submitted to the
Disposal Committee;
b) Management constitute a Disposal Committee that will facilitate the disposal of
unserviceable properties in accordance with the COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2024-001;
