c) Management assess at each reporting date whether there is any indication that
the unserviceable PPE items may be impaired. If any such indication exists, the
recoverable service amount of these assets shall be estimated; and
d) the Municipal Accountant recognize Impairment Loss and provide Allowance
for Impairment for unserviceable properties at year-end, with corresponding disclosure in
the Notes to FS of the amount and details of impairment losses recognized during the
period in order to fairly present the proper valuation of the affected PPE accounts.

Also, we recommended that the Property Custodian Designee ensure that all the
necessary details of each Motor Vehicle such as Vehicle Make/Year/Model, Dimensions,
Color, Chassis Number, Engine Number/Motor Number, Conduction Number or Plate
Number and assigned Accountable Office/r are presented in the RPCPPE to facilitate
timely and accurate monitoring, thereby ensuring completeness of insurance coverage of
the LGU’s properties.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, through the Local Chief
Executive, prepare a request for approval of COA to write-off the dormant receivable
accounts amounting to ₱1,448,421.34, following the procedures outlined in Sections 7
and 8 of COA Circular 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer and
Municipal Accountant to ensure that cash advances are supported with complete
documentary requirements. Henceforth, strictly comply with the requirements of Section
4(6) of PD No. 1445, Section 4.1.2 of COA Circular 97-002, and COA Circular 2012-001
dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to require
the submission of identification documents of the payee or authorized claimant as
supporting documents to the payroll.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that liquidations of
cash advances are taken up only when supported with complete documentary
requirements, in compliance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular 2012-
001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that Management strictly enforce the provisions on the grant
and liquidation of cash advances, including the sanctions thereof, as prescribed in
pertinent Sections of COA Circular 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that:
a) the Municipal Accountant and her staff continue their efforts in the
submission of the BRS, to provide a reliable basis for establishing the accuracy of the
Cash in Bank accounts in the financial statements;
b) the Municipal Accountant immediately record all valid reconciling items in the
books, by preparing Journal Entry Vouchers duly supported with proper documentation,
to be submitted to the Audit Team; and
c) The Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant reconcile their
Cashbooks and Subsidiary Ledgers to come up with accurate records.

We recommended that:
a) The Municipal Treasurer inquire with the concerned AGDBs about the
relevant information and/or documents in connection  with the time deposits, including its
nature/type and terms; 
b) Thereafter, the Municipal Accountant provide adequate, necessary, and
relevant disclosures for the Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Time Deposits in the Notes
to FS, to enhance the fair presentation and usefulness of the financial statements; and
c) Re-assess the propriety of maintaining these time deposits taking into account the
current financial standing and obligations of the Municipality as reflected in their financial
statements.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee post the Request for
Quotation (RFQ) on the procurements of goods and services through the Alternative
Modes of Procurement in the PhilGEPS website in compliance with the provisions of
Section 54.2 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. 9184.

We recommended that Management direct the LGU personnel involved in the
preparation of DVs and supporting documents to ensure that the documents are properly
accomplished as to dates and signatures, and that all required supporting documents are
complied with before processing payment.

We recommended that:
a) The Requesting office/End user ensure that complete details of the specification
of items being requested are properly indicated in the Purchase Order;
b) The Preparer of the PO indicate the complete specifications of the item/s
procured; and require the Suppliers to indicate the brand names of the item/s they are
quoting, which should be indicated in the PO, to have a reliable basis in checking the
conformity of delivered goods.

We recommended that Management:
a. Submit the ORs for the payment of registration fees to the International
Mathematical Olympiad and Philippine Elementary School Principals Association Inc.;
b. Secure the acceptability of evidence of receipt of payments for all
disbursements and ensure that reimbursements are properly supported with complete
documentation. Henceforth, strictly comply with the requirements of COA Circular 2004-
006 dated September 9, 2004, and COA Circular 2021-001 dated June 24, 2021.

We recommended that Management:
a. Submit the plane tickets, boarding pass, certificate of appearance or its
equivalent, narrative report, and certificate of travel completed by the nine participants
to the 2024 Thailand Mathematical Olympiad;
b. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that
liquidations of cash advances are taken up only when it is supported with complete
documentary requirements. Henceforth, strictly comply with the requirements of Section
4(6) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445 and COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14,
2012.

The Audit Team commended the Office of the Municipal Accountant for the efforts made in submitting all the Monthly Trial Balances and Quarterly FS within the timeline.

Furthermore, we recommended that Management continue its timely submission
of the Trial Balances and the Financial Statements.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue the timely
submission of the year-end financial statements, together with its supporting schedules,
to enable the timely verification by the Auditor.

We recommended that Management further improve its performance in the
submission of financial reports and accounts by:

a. Requiring the concerned Accountable Officer to submit the accounts and
financial reports to the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to the timelines of the
abovementioned existing laws, rules and regulations;

b. Directing the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting
Office all the disbursement vouchers, payrolls, and official receipts, including the
corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month or
pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of rules and regulations, as
applicable; and

c. Directing the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the timelines on the
recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.

We recommended that Management prepare and submit the BRS together with the bank statements and attachments within the prescribed period, and promptly
prepare the adjusting entries for the reconciling items to arrive at a correct balance for
the Cash in Bank account.

We commended Management for their commitment and efforts in consistently
conducting the annual physical inventory of all properties and reconciliation of property
records maintained by the Municipal Accounting Office and Property Office, resulting in
reconciled balances of PPE accounts in the Financial Statements.

However, we recommended that that Management submit to the Audit Team the
RPCPPE every January 31 of the succeeding year.

However, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant
include the necessary disclosures on the total road network system of the LGU in the Notes
to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that Management require the accounting department to strictly
comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997, on the
prescribed period within which to liquidate cash advances.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to require
the submission of identification documents of the payee or authorized claimant as
supporting documents to the payroll.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure
that cash advances are supported with certification from the accountant that the previous
cash advance has been liquidated and accounted for in the books. Henceforth, strictly
comply with the requirements of COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that the management:
a. Ensure that claims for reimbursement of actual accommodation expenses in
excess of the accommodation component of the DTE are supported with a certification by
the agency head or authorized representative as absolutely necessary in the performance
of an assignment (Sample shown in Annex B); and
b. Ensure strict compliance with the laws, rules and regulations on the claims for
traveling expenses to avoid possible misuse of government funds.

We recommended that the Management:
a) Require the end-users to promptly submit to the Accounting Unit all bills due
for payment;
b) Reassess and optimize the processing time from receipt of the bill by the end-
users up to disbursement of payment;
c) Direct the Accountant to monitor and initiate reconciliation with electricity
biller(s) on recurring arrears; and
d) Discontinue the practice of charging against government funds the payment of
penalties/ surcharges, as appearing on the official receipts of billers; instead, render the
officer/employee directly responsible for the late payment, and make them personally
liable.

We commended Management for its adequate planning of the activities related to
project implementation. We recommend that Management continue to improve its
implementation and proper monitoring of the I-BUILD sub-project to ensure that the
target date of completion is met and to achieve timely delivery of optimal benefits to the
project beneficiaries.

We recommended that, henceforth, Management ensure strict compliance with the
provisions of PD 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001, and the PRDP FMOG and comply
with the noted deficiencies in the identified transactions to ensure the propriety of the
transactions.

We recommended that Management improve its performance in the submission of
financial reports required in Annex S of the PRDP Financial Management Operations
Guidelines within the prescribed periods.

We recommended that Management further improve its performance in the
submission of financial reports and accounts by:
a. Requiring the concerned Accountable Officer to submit the accounts and
financial reports to the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to the timelines of the
abovementioned existing laws, rules and regulations;
b. Directing the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting
Office all the disbursement vouchers, payrolls, and official receipts, including the
corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month or
pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of rules and regulations, as
applicable; and
c. Directing the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the timelines on the
recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.

We recommended that Management further improve its performance in the
submission of the Trial Balances and the Financial Statements.

We commended the Local Chief Executive, the Municipal Accountant, and staff
for the early submission of the CY 2024 Year-End Financial Statements and other
financial reports prepared in conformity with IPSAS.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue the timely
submission of the year-end financial statements, together with its supporting schedules,
to enable the timely verification by the Auditor.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Accountant
prepare and submit the BRS within the prescribed timeframe in accordance with existing
rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the accountable officers, especially those with long overdue cash
advances, to immediately settle their accounts or refund their unliquidated cash
advances at once, or else criminal or administrative cases may be filed against
them pursuant to CSC Memorandum Circular No. 23, series of 2019;
b. Stop the practice of granting cash advances with no specific purpose or with a
vaguely described purpose in the Disbursement Voucher (DV), particularly for
labor payroll. Henceforth, all cash advances for special purposes shall be clearly
described in the disbursement voucher and payroll by indicating the project
name, duration, and/or any other material information that would clearly state
the specific purpose;
c. Direct the accountable officers to fully substantiate with complete supporting
attachments both the grant and liquidation of cash advances;
d. Instruct the Accounting Office to ensure that all transactions of the LGU are
well accounted for, recorded, and posted to their respective GLs to ensure
accuracy of the financial reports, which may affect decision making of the
Management; and
e. Strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 in the granting,
utilization, and liquidation of cash advances.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the Municipal
Accountant:

a. Prepare and submit the complete Bank Reconciliation Statements for the
accounts for all funds for Calendar Year 2024 for the preparation of the
correcting/adjusting entries;
b. Review the book reconciling items before recording any adjustments in the
financial statements to ensure that only properly supported items are included;
c. Take up the necessary adjustments in the books to reflect the correct balance of
the accounts in the financial statements, noting that a receivable from the AO
should be taken up for the service charge arising from DAUD; and
d. Maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for each bank account to serve as a basis
in the preparation of the BRS. The total balances of the subsidiary ledgers
should tally with the General Ledger.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal
Accountant reconcile their records regularly, at least on a quarterly basis, and
adjustments, if any, shall be promptly recorded in the appropriate records.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer revisit the dormant TF
bank accounts to check the proper disposition with regard to their specific purpose and
prevent the depletion of the same due to unnecessary bank charges.

We recommended that Management:

a. Instruct the Accounting Office to maintain complete, updated, and properly
accomplished PPELCs for each class of PPE, listing therein the data as to its
acquisition, description, custody, useful life, and other necessary
information;
b. Follow the provisions of COA Circular 2020-006 on the one-time cleansing of
PPE account balances and direct the Inventory Committee to complete the
inventory taking of all PPE of the Municipal Government, prepare the
Inventory Count Form, and upon completion of the physical count, prepare
the RPCPPE for submission to the COA Audit Team.

We recommended that Management strengthen its cash flow planning and
monitoring mechanisms to ensure that obligations are incurred only when sufficient
funds are available. Moreover, regular reconciliation and forecasting of cash
positions should be conducted to maintain sound financial management and avoid
future deficits that may affect service delivery and expose the LGU to legal and
financial risks.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management instruct the
Property Officer and concerned accountable officers/end-user offices/departments to
conduct the inventory of all supplies and materials in their custody at the end of the
year and to prepare the corresponding report certified correct by the Inventory
Committee.

We also recommended that the Property Officer maintain supply stock cards
and that the Accounting and Property Offices reconcile their records and take up the
necessary adjustments to ensure that the Inventory accounts are properly stated in
50
the financial statements.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendations that the Local Chief Executive
(LCE) require:

a. The Inventory Committee to conduct an inventory of the local roads under the
LGU’s jurisdiction and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Road
Network System;
b. The Municipal Treasurer/General Services Officer to render a report on the
Local Road Network at the end of the accounting period, to maintain a Local
Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network
Property Card for all roads and its components;
c. The Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant with the
complete description and cost segregation of road components for road
projects;
d. The Municipal Accountant to keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads
and road components for every road network and reconcile the same to the
result of the physical inventory taking at the end of each year; and
e. The Municipal Accountant to include the necessary disclosure on the total road
network system in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Management:

a. Instruct the Municipal Accounting Office to monitor its liabilities to recognize
all its obligations, following the recognition criteria set forth in the NGAS
Manual for LGUs and the International Public Sector Accounting Standards;
b. Conduct account analysis to determine the status of the unrecorded claims and
thereafter, timely settle all its payables and obligations, to prevent the filing of
possible legal actions by suppliers against the Municipality; and
c. Strictly adhere to the proper sequence for accounting check disbursements as
prescribed in the NGAS Manual to ensure accuracy and accountability in
financial reporting. Specifically, the JEV should be prepared only after the
actual issuance of the check by the Municipal Treasurer to prevent premature
recording of disbursements.

We recommended that the Management direct:

a. The Municipal Accountant to formulate a system for the necessary analysis of
the accounts and periodic reconciliation of their records to establish the balance
of the Inter-Agency Payable accounts;
b. The Municipal Accountant to implement an effective tracking mechanism for
the contributions and loans withheld and remitted to ensure that all the amounts
withheld for the month are remitted promptly and in the exact amount in the
succeeding month within the reglementary period; and
c. The Municipal Accountant and the Head of the Human Resource Management
Office to delineate and define the scope of their respective functions and
responsibilities to ensure that all tasks and obligations are properly addressed
and no work remains unperformed.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

a. Conduct analysis of the accounts to establish the correct balance of the ‘Due to
PhilHealth’ account, remit the PhilHealth premiums due accordingly, and
prepare adjusting entries, if necessary;
b. Perform reconciliation between the payroll generated by the Human Resource
Management Office and the Electronic Payroll Remittance System of
PhilHealth prior to the processing of remittance to prevent the occurrence of
64
discrepancies in the withheld and remitted amounts; and
c. Regularly update the Payroll System used in the Human Resource
Management Office so that the amounts generated therein would tally with the
amounts generated by the EPRS of PhilHealth.

We recommended that Management exert its best effort to implement all
programmed projects under the 20 percent Development Fund during the current year
to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental
management outcomes essential to the promotion of the general welfare of its
constituents.

We also recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Engineer to
regularly submit the Quarterly Report on Status of Government
Program/Projects/Activities (PPAs), properly accomplished with all the required data,
particularly the contract amount of each project, to ensure comprehensive and timely
monitoring of the status of their implementation.

We recommended that Management:

a. Strictly enforce compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 by ensuring that
all disbursements for the payment of JO workers comply with the mandatory
requirements, such as duly executed Job Order Contracts, properly
accomplished and signed DTRs, and verified Accomplishment Reports;
b. Provide a written justification for the inclusion of the five names that appeared
multiple times either within the same payroll or across different payrolls
covering the same pay period, as detailed in Table 34. In addition, explain the
noted discrepancies in payees’ signatures across various payrolls, particularly
where the signatures significantly differed from those in previous periods and
where the handwriting appeared highly similar across multiple DTRs for
different individuals;
c. Establish and implement stringent internal control measures and
comprehensive pre-audit procedures to ensure consistency between the number
of workdays reflected in the DTRs and those reported in the payrolls.
Discrepancies must be promptly investigated and addressed, with appropriate
corrective actions to prevent recurrence. A thorough review and validation
process during payroll preparation should also be institutionalized to detect and
eliminate any duplicate, inaccurate, or unsupported entries;
d. Develop and maintain a centralized and updated database of all JO and
Contract of Service personnel to streamline payroll processing, prevent
redundancy, and support the accuracy, reliability, and integrity of personnel
and payroll records;
e. Ensure the timely granting and liquidation of cash advances for the payment of
JO workers by improving fund planning and management, and require
complete and properly documented liquidation reports within the prescribed
period, in accordance with existing rules and regulations; and
f. Exercise due diligence by thoroughly reviewing all supporting documents prior
to signing payrolls and disbursement vouchers to ensure the validity, accuracy,
and regularity of claims being charged against public funds.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the HRMO to formulate a Human Resource Plan and issue a uniform
policy guideline on the hiring of JO personnel to determine the necessity of the
hiring as well as to strengthen the internal controls pertaining thereto; and
b. Categorically stipulate in the Job Order Contract or Contract of Services the
fund charging, the nature of work assignments and/or duties and
responsibilities, quantity of work to be accomplished, and the desired work
outputs to facilitate comparison and evaluation of actual work accomplishments
of hired job order personnel.

We recommended that Management:

a. Appropriate not less than five per cent of the total estimated revenues from
regular sources, including estimated revenues from local economic enterprises,
for MDRRMF, as required in Section 21 of the Philippines Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Act;
b. Assign and train accounting personnel who can handle the recording of
transactions and thereafter prepare and submit a revised utilization report as
of December 2024, taking into consideration the continuity of the previous
month’s report, and submit the same to the concerned agencies/offices, a copy
furnished the Audit Team for verification;
c. Maintain a subsidiary ledger for MDRRMF and provide a detailed schedule of
balances, showing its year of appropriation and allotment, to provide an
accurate monitoring of funds and aging of balances;
d. Reconcile the variance in the obligated amount of QRF between the SAAOB
provided by the Budget Office and the Utilization Report prepared by the
Accounting Office;
e. Require the Municipal Engineer to prepare Program of Works (POW) and
Detailed Estimates, conduct inspection of projects, and prepare the Inspection
Reports thereof, for attachment to the Disbursement Vouchers as supporting
documents of the claim; and
f. Instruct the concerned officers and employees to submit the lacking supporting
documents listed in Table 41. Otherwise, a Notice of Suspension/Disallowance
shall be issued if warranted.

We recommended that Management:

a. Allocate not less than five per cent of the total budget of the Municipality for
Gender and Development, as stipulated in the General Appropriations Act
(GAA) and PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular 2016-01
dated January 12, 2016;
b. Implement PPAs based on the GAD performance indicators to achieve gender-
sensitive governance and promote gender equality and sensitivity, not only in
the various departments/offices of the Municipality but also among the
constituents who were identified as beneficiaries of the PPAs;
c. Require the MPDO to prepare HGDG using the FIMME/PIMME checklist to
support the various PPAs attributed to the GAD budget;

We recommended that:

a. The Local School Board strictly adhere to the laws, rules and regulations on the
formulation, preparation, and approval of a SEF annual budget to provide funds
for the supplementary budgetary needs for the operation and maintenance of
public schools within the Municipality and submit the same to the Audit Team
for verification and examination;
b. The Municipal Budget Officer immediately submit the RAAO/ALOBS of SEF
for CY 2024 to the Audit Team for verification;
c. The Local Chief Executive instruct the concerned official/employee to cause
the immediate submission of the lacking documents stated in the Remarks
column of Table 51, amounting to ₱2,734,181.19. Otherwise, a Notice of
Suspension/Disallowance shall be issued, if warranted;
d. The Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant reconcile their
reports, specifically the SAAOB and Utilization Reports, in order to present
84
accurate data in the reports;
e. The Management regularly coordinate with the concerned DepEd District
Supervisor/Superintendent of the Municipality for proper utilization of SEF.
Relatively, all disbursements charged against SEF be properly
recommended/approved by the PSDS; and
f. The LSB and the Municipal Accountant regularly and timely prepare the
required SEF reports, for posting on the website of the LGU and/or in at least
three conspicuous public places and for submission to the oversight agencies.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that Management coordinate
with the Sangguniang Bayan on the update for the proposed adjustments on tax rates
to maximize the scope of the LGU's taxing power, pursuant to the provisions of the
Local Government Code, to increase tax revenues and receipts through updating of
the rates in the existing tax code, which will augment the budget provision for a
better delivery of government services.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management propose to
update during the ensuing year the rates imposed on water consumption operated by
the Municipal Waterworks System in accordance with Section 8 of Ordinance No. 06-
2008 to meet the current operational demands thereof.

We recommended that Management:

a. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to exercise due diligence in verifying
the documents submitted by claimants, particularly those claimed through
authorized representatives, and ensure that these documents are properly
attached to the liquidation report for audit verification;
b. Conduct a thorough review of the list of qualified senior citizens and provide
monthly updates to the DSWD to ensure equal and timely opportunities for
eligible individuals to receive the assistance; and
c. Strictly abide by the terms and conditions set forth under the duly executed
Memorandum of Agreement between the Municipality and DSWD, specifically
on the proper disposition and disbursement of funds.

We recommended that Management consider hiring a Financial Analyst to reconcile prior years’ records and reconstruct the subsidiary ledgers.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare and maintain the subsidiary ledgers for succeeding years to ensure proper monitoring and reconciliation with the general ledger; and
Secure and maintain copies of MOA for all funds received by the LGU from various agencies to properly substantiate the fund transfers recognized in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Restore the cash equivalent of all unreleased checks and recognize the related liability/payable account at year-end to ensure the fair presentation of Cash in Bank – LCCA account in the financial statements;
Prepare the necessary journal entries to record all the reconciling items; and
Strengthen internal controls by ensuring accurate recording, regular monitoring, and timely reconciliation of all cash accounts.

We reiterated our prior-year recommendation that the Special Disbursing Officer expedite the submission of valid identification documents to substantiate the proper payout of cash prizes. Henceforth, the SDO is likewise recommended to ensure that valid IDs are secured at the time of disbursement for all future transactions involving awards or prizes, to facilitate timely and complete liquidation.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare the necessary journal entries to record the transfer of the unexpended LDRRMF balances from prior years to the Special Trust Fund books with actual cash backing;
Maintain the subsidiary ledgers for transfer of unutilized LDRRMF to the special trust fund by year of transfer, receipts of NDRRMF by transferring agency, DRRMF from other LGUs by LGU, and other sources or donors; and
Henceforth, ensure the transfer of all unutilized balances of LDRRMF for QRF and MOOE to the Special Trust Fund at year-end to support disaster risk reduction and management activities within the next five years.

We reiterated our prior-year recommendation that Management enforce policies requiring officers and employees to liquidate cash advances within the prescribed period and/or by year-end, in compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant monitor the liquidation of travel cash advances and issue a written reminder within ten days before the expiration of the 30-day liquidation period, duly signed by the head of the agency or authorized representative, enjoining the official/employee to liquidate their travel cash advance. Otherwise, withhold the salary of the official/employee until such time that the travel cash advance has been fully liquidated, pursuant to Item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Item 3.3.2(b) of COA Circular No. 96-004.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Engineer to issue the Certificate of Final Acceptance only after the completion of the one-year defects liability period, ensuring that all identified defects or deficiencies, if any, have been satisfactorily corrected by the Contractor, and upon submission of the Warranty Certificate by the Contractor, in accordance with Section 62.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that Management:
Conduct a thorough review of the office's workload /staffing requirements and consider augmenting the manpower of the BAC Secretariat, as it is the main support unit of the BAC; and
A tracking system, whether manual or hybrid (e.g., Excel, spreadsheets with task logs and deadlines), may be implemented to improve the efficiency of the procurement process and ensure adherence to the objectives of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly comply with the procurement procedures prescribed under RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR;
Pay directly to the supplier/creditor for purchases made and ensure withholding of taxes due to the government; and
Ensure the completeness of supporting documents pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately remit the amount of ₱407,728.79 to avoid penalties and reconcile the remaining balance of ₱52,413.76 under Due to BIR account.
We recommended that the Municipality reconcile the balance of ₱92,295.67 under Due to GSIS account and ensure the timely and intact remittances of GSIS premiums and loans.
We recommended that the Municipality reconcile the balance of ₱133,875.71 under the Due to Pag-IBIG account and ensure the timely remittance of Pag-IBIG premiums and loans.

We recommended that Management expedite the completion of the projects under the LGSF-SBDP of the NTF-ELCAC and revert any excess funds to the National Treasury, in accordance with the DBM Local Budget Circular No. 151.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Engineering Office to expedite the preparation of POWs and DEDs based on the allocated budget to ensure their readiness for procurement and implementation; and
Instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Engineer to evaluate and reassess the projects under continuing appropriations to determine whether these are still responsive to the existing needs of its constituents; otherwise, initiate reprogramming toward more viable and significant projects aligned with the socio-economic needs of the Municipality.
We also recommended that the Municipal Development Council:
Evaluate and plan thoroughly the proposed priority development projects to be included in the AIP and ensure that these are procurement- and implementation- ready;
Monitor and evaluate the implementation of projects so that any issues can be promptly identified and addressed; and
Plan the appropriate use of savings from completed projects ensuring that these are implementable within the year.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require:
The Municipal Accountant to maintain the prescribed separate registries for LDRRMF to facilitate the timely preparation of the required monthly utilization reports;
The Municipal Accountant and Municipal DRRM Officer to establish a regular coordination mechanism to ensure the preparation of monthly utilization reports; and
The MDRRMO to submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF to the Audit Team and OCD on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.

We recommended that the Local School Board:
Strengthen planning and coordination among responsible offices to properly specify the priority programs for inclusion in the Annual SEF Budget;
Require the concerned officers to timely prepare and submit the activity proposals; and
Regularly monitor the implementation of each PPA to ensure efficient execution and the timely achievement of project goals.

We recommended that Management, through the GFPS:
Strengthen coordination with implementing officers to ensure the timely preparation and submission of activity proposals; and
Ensure future PPAs identified in the GPB are supported with activity proposals, aligned with the identified gender issues of the Municipality, and do not duplicate other government projects or services.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and GAD Focal Person to spearhead the collection of data/information necessary to establish a comprehensive GAD database.
We also recommended that Management include an adequate budget in the GPB for this activity and the additional manpower to handle GAD-related duties.

1.5 We recommended that the City Accountant make the following correcting
entry to properly close the carrying amount of properties classified as semi-
expendable items to Prior Period Adjustment and remove the recorded
inventories:
Account Debit Credit
Prior Period Adjustment 46,781,906.67
Other Supplies and Materials Inventory 25,654,129.33
Office Supplies Inventory 20,014,411.95
Other Property Equipment 1,113,365.39
1.6 We further recommended that the City Accountant conduct a comprehensive
evaluation of its recorded properties and retrospectively adjust them to
accurately classify semi-expendable items. This entails restating the amounts
for prior periods in the comparative financial statements to reflect the change
in accounting policy, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2022-004, as
supplemented by COA Circular No. 2024-006.
1.7 Lastly, we recommended that the City Accountant henceforth adhere to the
guidelines prescribed in COA Circular No. 2024-006 and make the
appropriate entries for semi-expendable items.
1.8 The City Accountant assured the Audit Team that his office would make the
necessary correcting or adjusting entries in CY 2025. His office also confirmed
that they would conduct a thorough review and evaluation of its semi-
expendable properties, following COA guidelines.

2.7 We recommended that Management install internal control mechanisms by
using the RIS to request for supplies and material from stock and to
consolidate the same in the SSMI, which shall be submitted to the City
Accountant for recording in the appropriate expense accounts for the supplies
consumed.
2.8 We also recommended that the City Accountant stop the practice of recording
purchases of supplies and materials directly to expense accounts and
henceforth, record purchases and utilization of supplies and materials in
accordance with Section 114 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I and
Section IV of COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014, to fairly
present the expense and inventory accounts in the financial statements.

3.10 We recommended that the City Accountant establish internal accounting
policies in identifying whether a particular expenditure pertains to training,
travel, extraordinary and miscellaneous expenses, or other materials and
supplies expenses.
3.11 We also recommended that the Office of the Accountant prepare the
necessary journal entry vouchers to reclassify the affected cash advance
accounts, and henceforth, record all cash advances pertaining to travel in the
Advances to Officers and Employees account and all cash advances pertaining
to intelligence or confidential purposes to the Advances to Special Disbursing
Officer account.
3.12 Lastly, we recommended that the City Accountant, henceforth, strictly follow
the Revised Chart of Accounts, as required in COA Circular No. 2015-009.

4.9 We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to:
4.9.1 Make the necessary adjustments to correct the erroneous recording of
prior period expenditures to reflect the actual financial performance of
the City Government of Bayawan, in conformity with Section 119 of PD
1445 and IPSAS 1; and.
4.9.2 Direct the Accounting Clerk assigned to record the expenditures to,
henceforth, review those that pertain to the prior period and journalize
these items as adjustments to equity and corresponding real or accrued
accounts rather than to current period expenditures or nominal accounts.

5.14 We recommended that Management strictly implement the guidelines and
procedures established under COA Circular No. 2007-001 in the granting,
utilization, accounting, and auditing of all funds released to non-governmental
organizations and/or people’s organizations.
5.15 We further recommended that Management demand liquidation of these
funds in accordance with the aforementioned issuance.
5.16 Lastly, we recommended that, henceforth, Management re-evaluate their true
intentions in granting financial assistance to organizations and individuals,
ensuring compliance with the law so that proper accounting and recording
can be made.

6.13 We recommended that the City Accountant and the City Budget Officer
reconcile their reports and determine the actual funds available for ongoing
and/or unimplemented projects under the 20 percent DF.
6.14 We further recommended that Management, upon the authorization of the
Sanggunian, open a separate bank account in the General Fund for the 20
percent DF, to which the City Accountant and City Treasurer transfer the
determined or reconciled balance of the said account.

7.6 We recommended that the City Accountant, henceforth, include the required
disclosures on the Road Network account as required under COA Circular
No. 2015-008.

8.9 We recommended that the City Engineer, in coordination with the City
Planning and Development Coordinator, evaluate the feasibility and closely
monitor the implementation of the various PPAs in accordance with the
approved development plan as required under Section 476 (4) of RA No. 7160.
In addition, we recommended that Management consider all possible
limitations, including the accessibility of the project location and availability
of equipment and materials, during the planning stage.

9.12 We recommended that Management transfer the funds from the time
deposit accounts to the City’s regular savings or current account upon
maturity to ensure cash availability when required for project
implementation in compliance with COA Circular No. 92-382.
9.13 We further recommended that Management review the funds intended for
capital outlay projects that are placed in time deposit accounts and assess
whether these projects are still subject to implementation or not. If not, such
funds shall be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund
and realigned to appropriate capital expenditures.

10.8 We recommended that meals and snacks served during regular and
administrative meetings of local officials and employees be charged to the
appropriations for EME and representation allowances of officials concerned.
10.9 We further recommended that Management refrain from conducting regular
and administrative meetings outside the City Hall to avoid additional and
unnecessary expenses.

11.4 We recommended and the BAC agreed to strictly observe the prescribed bid
validity period or, if necessary, secure the bidder’s written concurrence for an
extension before the bid validity expires, in accordance with the New
Government Procurement Act.
11.5 We also recommended and BAC also agreed to henceforth ensure that the
conduct of bidding, specifically the Negotiated Procurement – Two Failed
Biddings, is within the prescribed period under Sec. 4.1.1 of the GPPB
Circular No. 2016-05, otherwise, request an extension of 30 days upon
approval of the HoPE.
11.6 Lastly, we recommended and BAC agreed to evaluate and address bottlenecks
that cause delays in the procurement process, including the posting of NOAs
and NTPs, to prevent the recurrence of expired bid validity issues and
enhance transparency in government procurement, in accordance with the
provisions of RA No. 9184, as amended by RA No. 12009.

12.6 We recommended that the City Accountant ensure that no additional cash
advances are granted unless previous ones have been fully liquidated in
accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002.
12.7 We further recommended that Management, in coordination with the City
Accountant and City Treasurer, implement stricter monitoring and approval
for cash advances and liquidations, including regular reconciliation of
accounts.

13.14 We recommended that Management exercise prudence when charging
expenses to Advertising Expenses by limiting activities relating only to the
issuance of the agency’s guidelines, rules, and regulations, conducting
public bidding, and disseminating important public announcements in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2024-005 dated March 14, 2024.
13.15 Furthermore, we recommended that Management evaluate whether all
these expenditures outside the definition of Advertising Expenses are indeed
necessary and meet the documentary requirements mandated in the same
Circular.

14.10 We recommended that the City Accountant issue final demand letters
directing the persons concerned to immediately liquidate all outstanding
cash advances.
14.11 And if no liquidation is effected, we recommended that the City Accountant
impose the sanction of withholding the salaries of those who still fail to settle
their accounts after issuing notice.
14.12 We further recommended that Management pursue aggressive measures to
compel all accountable officers to settle their accounts, including the filing of
appropriate administrative proceedings, pursuant to CSC Memorandum
Circular No. 12, s. 2012.

15.7 We recommended that, henceforth, Management ensure that the cash
advance voucher for the release of funds for Confidential Expenses is
submitted to the concerned Audit Team Leader within seven days from the
release of the check.
15.8 Further, we recommended that the specific duration of implementation for
the said cash advance and purpose thereof, shall be indicated on the face of
the voucher.
15.9 Lastly, we recommended that the Certification of the Accountant shall be on
the face of the cash advance voucher, in accordance with Section 6.1.5 of COA-
DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01, dated January 8, 2015.

16.5 We recommended that, henceforth, Management shall only draw cash
advances for Confidential Expenses to cover projects to be implemented
within a three-month period. If the implementation is more than three
months, an additional cash advance shall be drawn, after the liquidation of
the prior cash advance.
16.6 We further recommended that Management prepare a Physical and Financial
Plan, indicating physical targets and corresponding budgets quarterly, in
accordance with COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01,
dated January 8, 2015.

17.6 We recommended and Management agreed to, henceforth, secure the
Certificate of Availability of Funds (CAF) from the City Accountant, prior to
entering into any contract and before making any disbursement or obligations
charged to any authorized appropriation, in accordance with Section 86 of PD
No. 1445.

18.10 We recommended and the City Treasurer agreed to develop an effective tax
collection campaign to enhance the collection of RPT/SET Receivables and
enforce remedies in the collection of real property such as the issuance of a
warrant on or before, or simultaneously with, the institution of civil action
for the collection of the delinquent tax.

19.9 We recommended and Management agreed to assess the necessity of hiring
consultants, especially when their functions are available in the LGU, and
review the terms and contracts of each consultant to ensure that these are in
accordance with existing rules and regulations.
19.10 We further recommended and Management also agreed to cease entering
into consultancy services that overlap with the functions of its personnel to
prevent unnecessary expenditures.

1.6. We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. Require the GSO and the Inventory Committee to conduct the one-time
cleansing of the PPE accounts for CY 2025 as prescribed in COA
Circular 2020-006.
b. Require the GSO to submit the documents for the PPE totaling
₱264,096,409.02 which was derecognized in CY 2018 in order to
identify the PPEs for proper disposition.
c. Direct the GSO and City Accounting Office to coordinate and conduct
the reconciliation of the results of the physical inventory and
accounting records, identify and address the nature and causes of the
variances and hold responsible the designated supply
officers/custodians and keep up reconciled reports at all times.
d. Require the City Accountant Designate to:
1. Review the PPE balances together with the corresponding
depreciation and prepare a JEV for the necessary adjustments.
2. Record accounting entries for the recognition of PPE found at
station, if any, derecognition of lost/non-existing/missing PPEs,
writing off/dropping from the books of accounts of the
remaining amounts lumped under the unreconciled subsidiary
ledgers of PPEs, when authorized, and present the necessary
disclosures in the Notes to the Financial Statements.
3. Prepare the JEV for the derecognition of all tangible properties
that fall below the P50,000.00 threshold and reclassify them
under the semi-expendable account.
4. Prepare the JEV to drop from the books all infrastructure
projects which were duly transferred and acknowledged
received by the recipients and end-user Barangays and ensure
that these are properly recorded in the Barangay books.
5. Drop from the LGU books the carrying amount of all
unserviceable properties, as soon as the authority to derecognize
is granted by appropriate office to bring the reported balance of
the PPE account in the financial statements in order.

2.11. We recommended that the City Accountant:
a. prepare the pro-forma adjusting journal entry to correct the balances
reported in the Construction in Progress account to its appropriate
asset account;
b. record the completed infrastructure “City Health Complex Computed
Tomography (CT) Scan” to its asset account; and
c. recognize the related accumulated depreciation expense of the
completed infrastructure assets.

2.12. We also recommended that the City Engineer maintain regular coordination
with the City Accountant to ensure the timely recognition and accurate
reporting of completed infrastructure projects at year-end.

3.11. We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to undertake
the transfer of the unexpended/unobligated balance of CY 2024 Quick
Response Fund (QRF) and DRRMF-MOOE totaling P25,182,873.01 to the
LDRRMF bank account.

3.12. We also recommended that the City Accountant take-up the transfer and
receipt of the unexpended balance of the QRF and MF-MMOE of the
LDRRMF in the General Fund and Trust Fund Books at the end of the year
when actual transfer of funds has been made for fair presentation of accounts
in the financial statements.

4.9. We recommended that the City Accountant ensure the accurate recording,
disclosure and reporting of LDRRMF receipts and utilization to provide
complete, transparent and reliable information in the Financial Statements.

5.13. We recommended that the City Accountant ensure all transactions are
accurately recorded using the appropriate accounts specified in the Revised
Chart of Accounts for LGUs. This will enhance the reliability and accuracy of
financial statements, ensuring they faithfully represent the City's transactions,
while providing reliable information to users and ensuring proper
accountability of funds.

5.14. We also recommended strengthening controls over the release and utilization
of financial assistance funds, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2007-001
to promote transparency and accountability, including monitoring of the
implementation of projects funded out of the funds granted.

6.8. We recommended that the City Accountant accurately record all transactions
using the appropriate accounts specified in the Revised Chart of Accounts for
LGUs. This will ensure that financial statements faithfully and correctly
present the City's transactions, providing reliable information to users and
ensuring proper accountability of funds.

7.13. Henceforth, we recommended that the City Government thru its GFPS/TWG
continue to design and implement PPAs that recognize fundamental equality
before the law of women and men and those which contribute to poverty
alleviations and economic empowerment, especially to marginalized women;
the protection, promotion and fulfillment of women’s human rights; and the
practice of gender-responsive governance.

8.13. For uncompleted PPAs, we reiterated our previous year’s audit
recommendation that the City Mayor instruct the concerned departments to
expedite the implementation of the on-going and unimplemented PPAs funded
by various NGAs;

8.14. We also recommended that the City Accountant prepare the Disbursement
Voucher for the return of the unutilized amount totaling ₱3,182,875.00 from
completed projects to the Source Agencies, and to submit the required
Liquidation Reports to the concerned funding agencies. Alternatively, request
the transfer of these funds to the General Fund of the City, if the disposition
of the unexpended balances is not specified in the MOA.

8.15. We further recommended that the Local Finance Committee and other
concerned City personnel update and submit the Liquidation Report/Report
of Disbursement on a monthly basis for all NGA-funded projects.

9.15. We reiterated our prior recommendation that the City Development Council,
through the City Mayor as Chairman:
a. Evaluate the projects from prior years’ AIP together with the current
year’s identified projects and undertake those of utmost priority taking
into account the available technical personnel and the number of
projects funded by national government agencies; and
b. Review the unimplemented projects in prior years funded from the
Continuing Appropriations of 20% DF during budget preparation to
determine its viability and necessity for inclusion in the AIP,
otherwise, recommend reversion of the funds if no longer needed.

9.16. We also recommended that the City Engineer:
a. Conduct a detailed engineering of all projects prior to its inclusion in
the AIP which includes the preparation of the Program of Work with
the Detailed Estimates, specific date and timetable and
plans/specifications;
b. Fast-track the implementation of priority projects identified for CY
2024 and in the subsequent years; and
c. Submit to the Bids and Awards Committee the Program of Work with
the Detailed Estimates, plans/specifications and Purchase Request
within the timetable indicated in the AIP, taking into consideration the
period within which the procurement process and project
implementation will be completed.

10.5. We recommended that the Local Finance Committee, in coordination with
Local Accounting and Budget Office, identify and validate the actual savings
from completed and/or discontinued projects.

10.6. Also, we recommended that, upon confirmation of actual savings, revert the
same to the General Fund for reappropriation, subject to the approval of the
Local Sanggunian.

11.14. We recommended that:
a. The BAC and all personnel concerned submit written
explanation/justification for failure to provide the necessary proper
contract, the Notice to Proceed, and Warranty Security in the
implementation of infrastructure projects procured through Small
Value Procurement.
b. The City government strictly adhere to the provisions of the
procurement law to prevent similar deficiencies and mitigate risks to
government funds.

12.14. We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the City Mayor, as
Chairman of the CDRRM Council, through the CDRRM Officer undertake
the following actions:
a. Conduct a quarterly monitoring on the implemented projects and
activities with reference to the LDRRMFIP;
b. Re-examine and evaluate the PPAs of prior years, and determine the
issues/concerns for the non-implementation of certain projects, and
consider them in the preparation of the Annual LDRRMFIP.
c. Prepare the LDRRMFIP every year and include in the budgeting for
the Programs, Projects and Activities, the unspent balance of the
LDRRMF which have been transferred to the Special Trust Fund
including all cash donations received.
d. Cause the timely implementation of all programmed projects under
DRRM program, and other PPAs, in accordance with the respective
appropriation ordinance, LDRRMP and AIP.

12.15. We further recommended that Management optimize the utilization of its
LDRRMF to lessen the socio-economic and environmental impacts of disasters in
the community and disaster risks, including projected climate risks, and to enhance
disaster preparedness and response capabilities of the community.

13.11. We recommended that Management, in coordination with the CDRRM
Officer, take the following actions to strengthen the planning, monitoring, and
implementation of the LDRRMF:
a) Clearly define the PPAs, including their specific titles, locations,
estimated costs, and expected outputs to facilitate effective monitoring
and evaluation;
b) Provide a detailed budget breakdown for each project or activity to
promote transparency and efficient fund utilization;
c) Establish a clear and realistic implementation timeline to enable timely
monitoring, evaluation, and completion of projects; and
d) Include all unimplemented PPAs from prior years into the current
year’s LDRRMFIP to ensure continuity and prevent the lapsing of
appropriations.

13.12. We further recommended that all PPAs included in the LDRRMFIP be
thoroughly reviewed to ensure they are implementation-ready within the year
of planning, supported by complete project documentation, approved
Programs of Work and necessary authorizations to avoid delays and ensure
successful execution.

14.7. We recommended providing the LSB with complete and accurate reports,
along with all necessary information on SEF budget preparation, in
compliance with DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JC) No. 1,
s. 2017. This will enable the LSB to optimize SEF utilization, ensuring to
effectively supplement the annual education budget, particularly for the
operation, maintenance and priority programs of public schools.

15.16. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the following
measures to ensure full compliance with existing laws, rules, and regulations:
 Prohibit the use of the reimbursement scheme by all City Officials and
Employees for the payment of permits and the procurement of goods
and services. This practice circumvents the established procurement
process and exposes the City Government to potential risks and
irregularities. All procurement activities must strictly adhere to the
procedures outlined in the procurement law.
 Direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to strictly enforce and
fully utilize the appropriate alternative methods of procurement, as
allowed under the procurement law, in instances where public bidding
is not practicable. All such transactions must be properly justified,
thoroughly documented, and compliant with the principles of
transparency, economy, and efficiency; and
 Instruct the City Accountant and City Treasurer to enforce strict
compliance with disbursement procedures by ensuring that all
payments are made through checks issued directly to the suppliers and
contractors concerned. The use of the Petty Cash Fund (PCF) shall be
strictly limited to authorized petty expenses and emergency purchases,
in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002 and other relevant
guidelines.

16.10. We recommended that the City Treasurer direct the Petty Cash Custodian to
ensure that all petty cash disbursements are properly documented with duly
accomplished Petty Cash Vouchers (PCVs), approved by the authorized
officer, and supported by official receipts and other necessary documents, in
strict compliance with COA Circular No. 2002-003 dated June 20, 2002, to
prevent audit suspensions and ensure accountability in fund disbursements.

17.15. We recommended for management to intensify its efforts in filling-up the
vacant Plantilla positions through hiring and/or promotion/regularization of
its existing casual and job order workers who are qualified for the positions to
enhance the quality of the City’s services and to provide job opportunities to
potential external talents and security of tenure to deserving in-house
personnel.

17.16. We also recommended for the Human Resource and Management Officer to:
a. Plan for additional Plantilla positions to be put up to complement for
the additional work brought about by the expansion of the City’s
medical and technical facilities.
b. Review the staffing pattern and service requirements in the
organizational structure of the city, assess the necessity of hiring
consultants as Service Providers and render a report thereon to the
City Mayor.

18.7. We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee and/or General
Services Officer submit perfected contracts, Purchase Orders (POs), and all
related documents to the Audit Team within the prescribed period, as required
by COA Circular No. 2009-001.

18.8. We also recommended that the General Services Officer ensure the prompt
submission of the Acceptance and Inspection Reports (AIRs) to the Audit
Team within 24 hours upon receipt and acceptance of delivered items, in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2009-002.

19.13. We recommended that the City Mayor, through the City Treasurer to initiate
the transfer of the GOP counterpart from the DA to the LGU as agreed in the
IMA.

19.14. We also recommended that the City Accountant prepare and submit the
required reports and financial statements in accordance with the relevant
provisions of the IMA and PRDP Guidelines.

20.7. We recommended that the City Mayor direct the GSO to prepare and submit
the PIF to the GSIS as a basis for the assessment of general insurance coverage
of all insurable assets, properties and interests of the City with the GIF of the
GSIS and submit the same to the Audit Team not later than April 30 of each
year as required under COA Circular 2018-002.

20.8. It is further recommended that the city should insure against any insurable
risk their properties, assets and interests with the General Insurance Fund as
administered by the GSIS.

21.10. We also recommended that the City Accountant-Designate and City DRRM
Officer submit the lacking supporting documents together with the detailed
computation of the honoraria given to the resource persons. This is without
prejudice to the issuance of Notice(s) of Suspension and Disallowance, if
warranted.

21.11. We further recommended that the City Accountant-Designate conduct a
thorough review to verify the completeness and accuracy of the supporting
documents attached to disbursement vouchers before processing.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:
Reclassify the erroneously recorded infrastructure projects and the two completed projects to their appropriate accounts;
Record the cost of the Lot owned by the City that was not included in the Land account;
Prepare the necessary journal entry to derecognize the demolished City Veterinary Office Building in Barangay Hamorawon; and
Be guided with the descriptions of the accounts provided under COA Circular No. 2015-009, so that the appropriate accounts will be used for each completed infrastructure project, and prevent the misstatement of PPE accounts.
The City Accountant commented that that the misclassification of certain Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) accounts has already been adjusted.
The Representative from the City Mayor’s Office clarified that the land was already titled under the name of the City, and was acquired through purchase.
The COA Audit Team emphasized that the projects should be recorded according to their nature and classification, which should be matched with the account descriptions as outlined in the COA Circular.  The account descriptions serve as the guide for selecting the appropriate account.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to prepare the necessary JEV to correct the erroneous balances of the Due to GSIS account.
The City Accountant commented that the adjustment in the books had already been made.

We reiterated and management agreed that the City Mayor direct:
The Local Finance Committee to:
prepare the Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Program Implementation within 20 days after the end of the quarter; and
post the same report on the Full Disclosure Policy Bulletin Board;
BAC Chairman to submit the contract within five working days from the time the contract is perfected; and
City Engineer to monitor and inspect the implementation of the LGSF-SBDP, and make sure that the project will be completed on the target date of completion.
The City Treasurer stated that she initially thought the City Accountant was responsible for posting the report.  However, for the current quarter and onwards, her office will handle the posting.
The BAC Chairman explained that the delay was due to the non-availability of the Head of the Procuring Entity (HoPE) to sign the documents, and issues related to the performance bond.
The City Engineer assured that all projects will be implemented as soon as possible, in accordance with the approved timeline and available resources.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Mayor instruct the BAC Chairman and BAC Secretariat to:
Determine the eligibility of all bidders by ensuring that all SLCCs are supported with a Certificate of Final Acceptance and/or CPES Final Rating, among others; and
Conduct post-qualification thoroughly and award contracts only to qualified bidders, to confirm strict compliance with the provisions of the procurement law.
The City Engineer (BAC Member) stated that the initially attached SLCCs were not updated; however, the contractor has since submitted the revised version. He further added that the Certificate of Completion, Acceptance, and Turnover served as the LGU’s basis for evaluating the contractor’s SLCC.
The COA Audit Team emphasized that the BAC failure to detect that the contractors submitted incorrect Statements of All Ongoing Government and Private Contracts in the post-qualification, that requires immediate action by the BAC, such as the establishment of database of awarded contracts to various contractors.

We recommended and management agreed that City Mayor direct the:
City Budget Officer to include in the City’s annual budget a sufficient amount of insurance premium for the general insurance covering all insurable properties and other assets;
City Treasurer and Accountant to ensure the payment of insurance premium to the GSIS;
Inventory Committee to reconcile records with the Property Officer, prepare, and submit the required Property Inventory Form to the Audit Team Leader and the GIF of GSIS on or before April 30 of each year.
The City Budget Officer suggested that a list of properties, along with their corresponding values, intended for insurance coverage should be submitted to their Office.  This is to ensure that these items will be properly included and accounted for in the Annual Budget.
The representative from the General Services Office (GSO) stated that it would be more appropriate if the insured amount is based on the depreciated value of the properties, rather than their original cost.
The City Accountant raised a query regarding the composition of the Inventory Committee.  He also added that the properties to be included in the Inventory Form shall be based on the physical count.
The COA Audit Team Leader emphasized that the insurance premium will be computed based on the data provided in the submitted form, such as the acquisition cost, market value, or appraised value.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the City Human Resource Management Officer to strengthen the internal control system in hiring additional workforce through job order/contract of service.   Limit the hiring of JOs per Office or to the actual number needed for normal operations, taking into consideration the volume of transactions per Office;
Direct all JOs and COS personnel to adhere strictly to Sections 1 to 5 of CSC Memorandum Circular No. 01, s. 2017 and to report for work eight hours daily, or as specified in their contract, using the biometrics machine, face-recognition-based, or other attendance monitoring systems for their “time in/ time out” as a control measure to ensure that payment of wages made by the government is based on the services they provided.
Provide a written justification for the 913 Job Orders that were absent during the headcount along with a valid document and identification proving their existence; and
Direct the City Accountant to ensure the validity and propriety of the supporting documents attached to the payroll.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to:
Have the GAD Focal Point System optimally utilize the allocated GAD Fund by prioritizing the implementation of the programs and projects in the GAD Plan and Budget;
Appropriately use the HGDG tool for attributing programs and projects to the GAD Fund to ensure its gender responsiveness; and
Establish and develop the GAD Database and GAD Agenda and monitor their implementation.
The GAD Focal Person stated that the Accomplishment Report was submitted and that the HGDG tool was used for Public Infrastructure Projects.  He also added that he will secure a duly signed copy and submit it to the COA Audit Team.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Mayor direct:
The City Budget Officer to:
reconcile with the City Accountant regarding the proper use of account titles and codes
refrain from issuing an Obligation Request without the necessary supporting documents, such as Purchase Request, Purchase Order, and Activity Design with Budgetary Requirements.
The City Accountant to coordinate with the City Budget Officer to reconcile the use of account titles and codes for proper expense classification and financial reporting.
The City GSO to conduct thorough inspections of actual items received and compare them with the details specified in the PR, PO, and PPMP.
The City Treasurer to:
require the concerned personnel to submit the lacking documents such as Activity Design with budgetary requirements, Purchase Request, Purchase Order, and Official Receipts, among others;
refrain from resorting to cash advances and reimbursement in the procurement of goods and services;
The BAC Chairman to procure goods and services in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9184, specifically the Competitive Bidding, Small Value Procurement, and Negotiated Procurement, where appropriate; and
We also recommended all City Officials concerned to conduct a thorough review to determine the legality and propriety of the aforementioned disbursements, their necessity in achieving the City’s desired development goals and promoting the general welfare and progress of the community, and take the necessary corrective actions to prevent any further unwarranted expenditures.
The City Treasurer explained that she proceeded on the understanding that procurement for emergency cases, such as the presidential visit, was acceptable. She added that they could not conduct bidding for such transactions because they did not anticipate this event.
The COA Audit Team emphasized that in the case of events conducted annually, the City should be able to conduct a bidding, or other alternative modes of procurement, as appropriate.
The management noted and will comply.

We recommended that Management ensure that the:
Municipal Accountant review and validate thoroughly the unreconciled balance of the CIP account by tracing prior year’s records and transactions, to fully identify/document the composition of the account; and
Municipal Engineer to submit Status RGPPAs to the Municipal Accountant together with the Certificate of Completion to transfer the whole amount of the completed infrastructure project from the CIP account to the appropriate PPE accounts and require the concerned contractors to submit the necessary supporting documents for the processing of their claims on the unbilled portion of the completed projects.
Management’s Comment:
The Office of the Municipal Engineer will comply with the audit recommendation by submitting to the Office of the Municipal Accountant the copies of Certificate of Completion and Copy of Turn-over and Acceptance.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management conduct the disposal proceedings necessary for these PPE items that are both fully depreciated and unserviceable, to allow fair presentation of balances in the FS and take heed and observe the guidelines on the disposal of government properties as prescribed in Joint Circular No. 2024-1 dated January 30, 2024 to facilitate systematic and timely disposal proceedings which are essential to avoid further deterioration of the properties and consequent depreciation in their value.
Management’s Comment:
The management has not yet undergone the one-time cleansing procedures for PPE, hence, all PPE items stated in the FS at year end still carry the cost of all unserviceable, burned, demolished and fully depreciated PPEs.

Recommendation:
We recommended, and Management agreed to ensure that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct balance of the loans payable and to furnish the Auditor a copy of the JEV taking up the adjustments. Henceforth, present the current and non-current portion of the liabilities as separate classifications on the FS as provided in IPSAS 1.

Recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation, and Management agreed to observe and implement the guidelines and procedures on the write-off of dormant receivables accounts provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005, in order to attain fair presentation of the Other Receivables account in the FS.

Recommendations:
We recommended, and Management agreed to:
Monitor the issuance of checks and send communications to the concerned payees or creditors informing them of any issued but unclaimed checks which have become stale. If the concerned payees or creditors appeared to claim the checks, the Municipal Treasurer to issue a new check to the claimant; and
After exhaustion of the communication phase and there are still unclaimed checks which have become stale in the possession of the Municipal Treasurer, the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments to restore the cash in bank amount and set up the corresponding payable/ liability account.

Recommendation:
We recommended, and Management agreed to ensure that the Municipal Accountant comply with the reporting requirement and prepare the schedules pertaining to the receivable and payable accounts indicating the DV/JEV/check number, date, debtor/creditor, nature, and status of the transactions to support the balances presented in the FS and to facilitate the writing-off of long outstanding balances.

Recommendations:
We recommended that the Management:
Refrain from incurring expenditures in case of revenue shortfall and officials may take the following courses of action:
Practice prudence so that government funds are safeguarded and that excessive expenditures are prevented;
Prioritize expenditures or spend only what is necessary; and
Intensify revenue collections.
Exert efforts to conduct activities that will contribute to the positive increase of revenue as anticipated in the prepared annual budgets.
Management’s Comment:
The Management commented that the decrease of business tax collection was because of the pending civil case of a mining company, hence, there will be a reduction of the targeted income for CY 2025. The Municipal Budget Officer reasoned that included in the CY 2024 PS and MOOE expenditures of ₱3.736 million and ₱44.404 million, were sourced from CY 2023 income surplus under Supplemental Budget Nos. 1 and 2. Moving forward, the management will adhere accordingly to the audit recommendations.

Recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed through the Municipal Treasurer, ensure to develop an effective tax collection campaign program and strategy and adopt collection remedies to enhance the collection of RPT/SET Receivables.

Recommendation:
We recommended the Management to stop the practice of granting new/additional cash advances to the DO unless her previous cash advance has been accounted for/liquidated and strictly grant cash advance to the DO within the limit of her cash accountability based on approved bonds pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2019 dated April 25, 2019, to ensure protection and indemnification in case of loss of public funds.
Management’s Comment:
The Municipal Treasurer reasoned out that there were some cash advances of the Special DO which remained unsettled in the books of accounts at year end due to some claimants/recipients of the funds especially for the special purpose like (TUPAD, SENIOR CITIZENS) are living in the remote barangays of the Municipality and sometimes the schedule of cash advance and pay-out falls on the last week of the month. However, the Municipal Treasurer and the Management assured during the exit conference that the COA recommendations will be addressed especially the designation of additional DO to cater the special cash advances.

Recommendations:
We recommended to Management to ensure that the:
Municipal Accountant to (i) assign a staff who will maintain supplies ledger cards containing the details for each receipt, issuances and remaining balances of all supplies; and
General Services Officer to (i) properly maintain and update the stock cards of all inventories;(ii) submit the SSMI regularly to the Accounting Office for proper accounting thereof; and (iii) submit to the Office of the Auditor the RPCI as required in Sec. 124 of Chapter 7 of NGAS Volume I.
Management’s Comment:
The Management assures that the COA recommendations will be comply and observed in the CY 2025 operation.

Recommendation:
We recommended to Management through the Municipal Agriculturist to inform and/or report to the GSO and Municipal Accountant all the items received from the DA and PhilRice and the distribution of these items to the intended beneficiaries as bases of the Municipal Accountant for recording in the books of accounts.
Management’s Comment:
The OIC - Municipal Agriculturist explained that the goods/farm inputs coming from the DA and PhilRice were not recorded in the book of accounts at year end, since there were already direct recipients. However, management assures that they will coordinate with the GSO and Municipal Accountant whenever there are donations received.

Recommendation:
We recommended, and Management agreed to strictly comply with COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 by submitting to the Audit Team the infrastructure/non-infrastructure contracts and POs within the prescribed period of five working days from its execution to prevent undue delay in the review thereof.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require the Accountable Officer and Liquidating Officer to perform thorough verification of the AFs and check the accuracy of the statement of AFs in the RCDs. Also, issue an IRAFs detailing the transfer of AFs from AF custodian, such as issuance of AFs stubs and/or RTS stubs from previous CO, duly acknowledged by the accountable officers.
Management’s Comment:
The Municipal Treasurer commented that there are instances where RCDs were not reviewed thoroughly that errors were overlooked. Management will comply with the recommendation.

Recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to require the CO to prepare and submit monthly RAAF for the former to consolidate as basis to prepare and submit the monthly consolidated RAAF.

Recommendation:
We recommended, and Management agreed to ensure that all accountable officers faithfully adhere to the existing rules and regulations on the receipt and remittance/deposit of all collections handled by them to prevent loss and/or misuse of government funds and avoid possible shortage in audit, which have penal sanctions.

Recommendation:
We recommended that Management ensure that the LDRRM Council, thru the LDRRMO, identify and incorporate the prior years’ PPAs in the formulation of the LDRRMFIP and report the utilization of the same in the monthly Report on Utilization of DRRMF to ensure accurate monitoring and maximum utilization of such funds.
Management’s Comment:
The LDRRMO committed to revise the LDRRMFIP for CY 2025 and submit monthly utilization report in order to comply fully with the COA recommendation.

Recommendation:
