observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer enforce more stringent measures and avail of other remedies in order to collect delinquent taxes pursuant to Sections 256 and 247 of R.A.7160, to facilitate implementation of programs and projects of the LGU which shall be financed out of collections from tax delinquencies.

We recommended that management prioritize and consider filling up the vacant positions by hiring new employees or expedite promotion of deserving employees to enhance its services to attain full implementation of programs, plans and activities of the local government unit for the optimal benefit of the constituents as well as to provide equal job opportunities to the community.

We recommended that management revisit the current regular plantilla items of the LGU and propose/create another plantilla position when necessary to avoid hiring of contract of service and job order personnel to perform the duties and responsibilities of a regular employee, in compliance with Section 2, Rule XI of CSC MC No. 40, series of 1998 and Sections 7.1 and 7.2 of CSC-COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 1 series of 2017.

We recommended that management consider adopting the procedures in reconciling and cleaning the dormant receivable and liability accounts, unliquidated cash advances, and fund transfers per COA Circular No.2016-009. Also, we recommend that management send confirmation letters to its creditors such as BIR, PAGIBIG, Philhealth and GSIS to validate outstanding payables. Confirmation letters and adjusting journal entry vouchers, if any, shall be furnished the audit team.

13. We recommended that management revise/update its Revenue Code of 2009 pursuant to Section 191 of RA 7160 to increase the revenue collection of the municipality so as to provide additional funds which can be used to finance beneficial programs and projects.

We recommended that management exhaust all possible remedies to collect these receivables, deduct the outstanding balances from the salaries or any amount due from the employees who are still connected with the municipality, if any and review, analyze and reconcile all overdue accounts to determine proper disposition of reconciled and validated accounts.

17. In view of the SC’s decision on the unconstitutionality of the PDAF, we recommend that the local government unit return the unutilized PDAF balance to the Bureau of Treasury for reversion to the unappropriated surplus in the general fund of the national government in accordance with the Supreme Court decision dated November 19, 2013.

21. We recommended that Management submit all the original copies of the necessary supporting documents as enumerated in COA Circular 2012-001 and observe the provisions of the said circular on the submission of copy of government contracts, purchase orders and their supporting documents and of Section 4 (6) of PD 1445. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly review the supporting documents attached to the disbursement vouchers to ensure that these consist of all the necessary documents required to be submitted and that these are correctly and completely filled up to serve as valid documents in support of the transactions involving financial claims.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the offices of the Municipal Assessor and Municipal Treasurer and exhaust all efforts in order to establish the RPT/SET receivables accruing to the municipality duly supported with certified list of taxpayers or RPTAR and the amount of tax receivable.
3 We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant properly prepare the accounting entries on the collection and discount of real property taxes.

We recommend that management ensure the full implementation of the One-time cleansing of PPE accounts as provided for in the same COA Circular 2020-006 in order to present fairly the PPE account balances.

We recommend that management intensify the implementation and monitoring of the above projects in order to deliver good and timely services which the public deserves. We also recommend to demand liquidated damages against the contractors of these delayed projects.

We recommend that management effect the proper liquidation of all implemented and completed projects and return the remaining balances of the funds to the source agencies.
10 We also recommend that shares to government agencies be properly remitted in order to present the correct balances of the Due to NGAs and due to LGU accounts.

We recommend that management expedite the hiring of personnel to fill-up these vacant positions and/or fast track the promotion of deserving workers among the present pool of manpower resources in the municipality.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the Human Resource Management in the Office and the Internal Audit Services to conduct personnel audit, review, assessment and evaluation of the existing manpower complement to prepare an updated organizational structure/pattern, remove excessive and unnecessary JOs/COS workers who are performing redundant functions with other workers and regular plantilla personnel and other activities not related nor aligned with the mandates of the municipality and their assigned department/offices.

We recommend that management comply on the documentary requirement for the filing of the request for relief from accountability on the damaged/lost properties in compliance to Section 73 of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 93-404. Otherwise, the accountable officer shall not be relieved from accountability nor be allowed credit for the loss during the settlement of his account.

We recommend that management require the Municipal Accountant to trace the cause of the abnormal balances and effect the necessary adjustments in the books of the agency in order to present fairly the financial statements of the municipality.

We recommend to the Municipal General Services Officer to update the list of properties that needs to be insured and submit the same to GSIS and the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate funding for the insurance coverage of all its insurable assets as required under Republic Act No. 656.

We recommend that Management create an Internal Audit Service (IAS) Unit that would handle the internal control functions of the agency pursuant to the above-mentioned rules and regulations. Refer to the Philippine Government Internal Audit Manual and the National Guidelines on Internal Control System as guide in strengthening the internal control system of the LGU.

We recommend that management use the prescribed format and provide the complete data of the Annual Procurement Plan in compliance with GPPB Circular 08-2015 to in order to properly guide the municipality on its procurement process.

We recommend that management require the Municipal Accountant to Submit a Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF in the prescribed format to the COA Auditor on or before the 15th day of the month, through the LDRRMC and Local Development Councils (LCDs) as required under Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

In view hereof, we recommend that management require the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the required financial information in prescribed in COA Circular 2012-02 and IPSAS 1 Section 127(c).
Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for the transfers of the agency’s unutilized DRRMF to the special trust fund pursuant to Section 5.1.12 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommend that management require the concerned municipal officials to comply the accounting and reporting guidelines prescribed by COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 14, 2014 to properly monitor the receipt and distribution of relief goods as well as accounting of all donated PPEs.

We recommend that management install adequate control measures to safeguard government funds and to avoid other similar instances. We also recommend that management require the supplier to refund the ₱107,520.00 which was paid twice due to inadequate control measures on the disbursements.

The Local Chief Executive in the absence of General Services Office (GSO) require the Municipal Treasurer's Office/concerned personnel to conduct physical inventory taking of all LGU's properties and identify all unserviceable units/items thru the preparation of the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and submit to COA for review and validation
27 Upon completion of physical count of the Inventory Committee, proper disposal of all obsolete and unserviceable properties shall be undertaken pursuant to the instructions of Section 79 of PD 1445 and procedures cited in COA Circular No. 89-296.
28 Require the Accounting Unit to drop from the books all items in the IIRUP duly supported with Journal entry vouchers (JEVs). Reflect the accurate amount of PPE in the financial statement at year-end.

Recommend that the Municipal Accountant to disclose the required financial information in the Notes to Financial Statement to assists the users in understanding the financial statements and to provide them with additional information value.
We recommend that management demand immediate liquidation/ settlement of cash advances from concerned employees and comply COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommend that management provide a venue to discuss with the municipal employees the role of the LGU in ensuring that its employees are able to receive a minimum of P 4,000.00 net take home pay per month for them to maintain self-respect and dignity as employees of the government, in pursuance of Section 47 of the 2017 GAA.
39 Likewise, we recommend that the Municipal Accountant and HRMO monitor payroll deductions and refrain to certify for another /renewal of loan application of an employee with net pay below P4,000.00.

We recommended that the Designated CGSO as head of the inventory committee, perform the following actions, to wit:
1. Conduct a complete physical count of all inventory items per semester, prepare and submit the Report on Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to the City Accountant for reconciliation and submit the RPCI to the Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year per Section 124 Chapter 7 Volume 1 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual.
2. Cause the preparation of the Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) upon distribution/issuance of the stocks to the end users and summarize all RIS in the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued and submit the same to the City Accountant for recording purposes.
3. We also recommended for the City Accountant to stop the practice of recording immediately to the expense account the inventories received by the City. Recognize the corresponding expense account based on the monthly Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued, supported by duly approved RIS.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Development Council, through the City Mayor as Chairman:
a. Evaluate the projects from prior years’ AIP together with the current year’s identified projects and undertake only those of utmost priority taking into account the available technical personnel and the number of projects funded by national government agencies.
b. Include in the AIP only those priority projects which are ready for implementation in the current budget year to ensure optimal utilization of the 20% DF; and
c. Review the unimplemented projects in prior years funded from the Continuing Appropriations of 20% DF during budget preparation to determine its viability and necessity for inclusion in the AIP, otherwise, recommend reversion of the funds if no longer needed.
We also recommended that the City Engineer:
a. Conduct a detailed engineering of all projects prior to its inclusion in the AIP which includes the preparation of the Program of Work with the Detailed Estimates, specific date and timetable and plans/specifications;
b. Fast-track the implementation of priority projects identified for CY 2023 and in the subsequent years; and
c. Submit to the Bids and Awards Committee the Program of Work with the Detailed Estimates, plans/specifications and Purchase Request within the timetable indicated in the AIP, taking into consideration the period within which the procurement process and project implementation will be completed.
We further recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee schedule the procurement of projects in accordance with the timeframe specified in the AIP to ensure completion of the projects before the end of the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant revisit the Infrastructure Assets Accounts particularly Road Network, Sewer System and Water Supply Systems, ascertain the estimated useful life used for each component of the asset, and compute the depreciation to arrive at the correct book value of each component as of December 31, 2022.
observations.reason: The accounting unit has yet to record the component of Road Network. Moreover, subsidiary ledgers for each road network components were not yet maintained by the latter; thus, depreciation expense cannot be determined yet.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:

a) 	The Local Chief Executive to enjoin the department head’s compliance with the requirements of the circular and direct the Municipal Engineer to provide the local accountant and the general services officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;

b) 	That thereafter, the Municipal Accountant and General Services officer to prepare and maintain all required reports, records and ledgers as mentioned in COA Circular No. 2015-008; and

c) 	Lastly, the Municipal Accountant to revisit the Infrastructure Assets Accounts, ascertain the estimated useful life used for each component of the asset, determine the proper valuation of the local road assets and compute depreciation to arrive at the correct book value of each component as of December 31, 2022.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
provide the details or breakdown of Other MOOE, Other Receipts and Other
Expenses as of December 31, 2023 and ensure that supporting information for
items presented on the face of the statements of financial position, statement of
financial performance, statement of changes in net assets/equity, or cash flow
statement were adequately disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to reclassify the 30 completed infrastructure projects
totaling P10,147,175.19 to their appropriate PPE accounts and provide the same
with corresponding depreciation expense;
b. Inventory Committee to validate the existence and conditions of the above-
mentioned projects and correctly report the same in the Report on Physical
Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE); and
c. Municipal Engineer to furnish the Municipal Accountant with the list of
completed and on-going projects as year-end as basis in the transfer of
completed projects to its proper PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to derecognize the
above-mentioned properties using the following entry:
Debit Credit
Prior Period Adjustment P 820,346.44
Accumulated Depreciation 1,455,586.56
Property, Plant and P2,275,933.00
Equipment
b. the MGSO to remove the noted 69 semi-expendable properties from the
RPCPPE; and
c. the MGSO and the Municipal Accountant to validate the existence of the
recorded other infrastructure asset totaling P198,540.96. In addition, to
continue the periodic reconciliation of their records and make the necessary
corrections or adjustments to the discrepancies noted between their records.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal General Services
Officer (MGSO) to ensure that perfected contracts and copies of approved POs,
together with its supporting documents, are properly accomplished and submitted
to the Office of the Auditor within five working days upon execution or issuance in
compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to:
a. ensure that all the procurement is done through competitive public bidding
except in those cases allowed under the alternative mode as provided for in RA
No. 9184 to obtain the most advantageous prices to the government; and
b. require the suppliers to submit the noted lacking documents such as
mayor/business' permit and proof of registration with PhilGEPS. Also ensure
that all the transactions were duly supported with BAC Resolution
recommending and justifying to the HOPE the use of alternative mode of
procurement.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that succeeding
payments for procurements are supported with complete documentary
requirements as prescribed under the provisions RA No. 9184 to avoid suspension
or disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the management prioritize and consider filling-up the
vacant positions by promoting deserving employees among the present pool of
manpower resources with exemplary performance and hiring new employees for
lower-level positions to further improve its services, provide equal job
opportunities to the community and attain the full implementation of programs,
plans and activities which will in turn benefit the Municipality as a whole.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned officials to:
a. exercise tighter control over the use of government vehicles through properly
accomplished and duly approved serially numbered Driver’s Trip Tickets, a
summary of which shall be made at the end of the month in a Monthly Report of
Official Travels for audit purposes;
b. ensure that the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and Monthly Report of
Official Travels are submitted to the Office of the Auditor for verification
purposes to determine the reasonableness of fuel consumed during the period;
and
We also recommended the submission of justification or explanation on why fuel
was given to privately owned vehicles out of the funds of the Municipality and
ensure that only government-owned motor vehicles are issued with gasoline or
official business.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and
other concerned officials to submit immediately the lacking documents for the
transactions with aggregate amount of P10,899,894.39 to the Office of the Auditor
and ensure that succeeding payments for infrastructure transactions are
supported with complete documentary requirements as prescribed under the
provisions of Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 to avoid
suspension or disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MDRRMO and the
Municipal Budget Officer to submit to the Audit Team valid justification and/ or
legal basis for charging the noted expenditures to the LDRRMF and refrain from
obligating and charging LDRRMF for expenditures which are not directly related
to the implementation of disaster risk reduction and management activities,
otherwise the expenses shall be disallowed in audit for being contrary to NDRRMC,
DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the HRMO to coordinate with the
GSIS to update the payroll of the employees in the GSIS system.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer remit the outstanding
balance of P97,081.79 and ensure that all the amount withheld are remitted intact
within the first 10 days of the calendar month following the month to which the
contributions apply.

However, we reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Accountant prepare and submit the Monthly Trial Balance, Quarterly FS together with the complete set of schedules and annexes on or before the prescribed timelines in accordance with the abovementioned rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management:
Require the concerned Accountable Officers to submit the accounts and financial reports to the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to the timelines of the abovementioned existing laws, rules and regulations;
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting Office all the disbursements vouchers, payrolls, official receipts, including the corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month or pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of rules and regulations, as applicable; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the timelines on the recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to evaluate and determine the cause/s of the discrepancy and record the necessary adjustments in their books. Henceforth, both officials shall reconcile their records regularly, at least on a quarterly basis, and adjustments, if any, shall be promptly and appropriately recorded;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to promptly take up necessary adjusting entries for the noted book reconciling items to reflect the correct balances of pertinent accounts in the financial statements; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to immediately monitor, review and retrieve the records of the four non-moving/dormant accounts for possible return to source agencies or closure and transfer of balances to active accounts and other possible remedies to avoid unnecessary depletion of the funds due to bank-imposed charges, if any.
We also recommended the Municipal Treasurer to properly record transactions in the cashbook and to reflect all adjustments, instead of replacing the balances without the proper disclosure on the changes made.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management require both the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to identify the specific sources of funds and establish whether these were essentially idle funds in accordance with Section 21 and 22 of COA Circular No. 92-382.  In cases when there are idle funds identified, investing such should have prior authority of the Sangguniang Bayan and the approval of the Local Chief Executive.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Municipal Collecting Officers strictly adhere to the rules and regulations to turn over all their collections intact to the Local Treasurer at the close of business day together with the duplicates of official receipts issued and the cash collected for submission to the to the Local Treasurer/Cashier.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer check the Statement of Accountable Forms as to initial balances on hand, receipts, issues and the ending balances on hand, make physical count of accountable forms remaining in the custody of collectors and check the same against new balances on hand column. Henceforth, accountable forms shall be issued only to those accountable officers who have religiously and completely remitted their collections in compliance with the provisions of Section 30 of the Manual on NGAs Volume I.
We likewise recommended the Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact and in full amount all her collections and/or collections remitted to her by the collectors with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day in compliance with existing regulations.
We further recommended the Municipal Treasurer to submit all the duplicates of Official Receipts to be attached to the RCDs and submitted to the Accounting Office for recording in the books and henceforth be submitted to the Audit Team on or before the 10th day of the following month for verification.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer  to maintain a permanent printed/hard copy of cashbook to record daily all her collections and deposits and balances to facilitate tracking, monitoring, evaluation and reconciliation with the Accounting Records.
Further, we recommended the Management to instruct concerned Accountable Officers to maintain a cashbook to record daily their collections and remittances and balances.
Henceforth, at the end of each month, the MT and the collectors shall rule and foot the cashbook and show the ending balance their accountability.  Further, they shall certify on the correctness and completeness of the entries in the cashbook

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to submit the Monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms to the Auditor, in accordance with Section 98 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual, Volume I.

We recommended that Management:
Prepare and issue demand letters to concerned officers and employees with outstanding cash advances requiring them to immediately settle their unliquidated cash advances. Henceforth, strictly enforce rules and regulations on the liquidation of cash advances as required in COA Circular No. 97-002 and P.D. No. 1445; and
Prepare a Request for Authority to Write-off the dormant receivable accounts amounting to ₱90,310.26 following the procedures provided in Sections 8.2 and 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management instruct the GSO/Property Officer and concerned accountable officers/end-user offices/departments to conduct the inventory of all supplies and materials in their custody at the end of the year and to prepare the corresponding report certified correct by the Inventory Committee.
We also recommended that the GSO/Property Officer maintain supplies stock cards and for the Accounting and Property Offices to reconcile their records and take up the necessary adjustments to ensure that the Inventory accounts are properly stated in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management direct the Accountant to:
Undertake collaborative procedures with the Property Officer in the reconciliation of accounting and property records; and
Maintain complete, updated and properly accomplished PPELCs for each class of PPE, listing therein the data as to its acquisition, description, custody, useful life and other necessary information.
Furthermore, we recommended that Management follow the provisions of COA Circular 2020-006 on the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances and direct the Inventory Committee to complete the inventory taking of all PPE of the Municipal Government, prepare the Inventory Count Form, and upon completion of the physical count, prepare the RPCPPE.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Assessor and Property Officer to facilitate the titling of the 40 parcels of land. Relative thereto, require the Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate funds for the payment of transfer taxes and other fees so that issuance of the titles could be facilitated.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Engineering Office in determining the actual status of all reported on-going projects and to effect the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts based on the result of validation in order to reflect the accurate balance of the CIP accounts;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all completed and paid projects are immediately classified to their appropriate PPE accounts and to compute for the required depreciation thereof; and
Observe proper planning and coordination to avoid complications during the project implementation and ensure that infrastructure projects are completed on time.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Property Officer to immediately dispose of the unserviceable properties in accordance with existing regulations.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to determine the validity of the unliquidated obligations or Accounts Payable amounting to ₱4,263,899.01 which are outstanding for more than two years and if found to have no legitimate claim against the Municipality, cause the reversion of the same to the unappropriated surplus.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Planning Officer to prepare a separate budgetary allocation for the local economic enterprises (LEE) of the Municipality
Further, we reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Accountant prepare a complete set of FS for each of the Municipality’s economic enterprises which shall be attached to the consolidated FS of the General Fund in order to provide adequate information on the financial position and performance of the enterprises.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned offices to hasten the implementation of PPAs funded by the current and prior years’ continuing appropriations of 20 percent Local Development Fund to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes essential to the promotion of the general welfare of its constituents.

We recommended that Management, through MDRRMO, ensure the efficient and effective utilization of the DRRMF thru implementation of programs and projects designed to avoid, lessen or limit the potential adverse impact of disasters, such as projects pertaining to disaster prevention, mitigation and preparedness.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant include in the Notes to Financial Statement a disclosure of the amount and details of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF as required by Section 5.1.16 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended the LCE direct:
The GFPS, TWG to coordinate with the MPDO in preparation of GAD Plan and Budget in relation to the approved PPAs with the correspoding budget alocation and  inform the concerned DILG Office in writing if there are changes in the DILG endorsed GPB and/or if there is a need to implement additional PPAs relevant to current gender issues or GAD related undertakings;
The Local Finance Committee to incorporate in the Annual Budget, the approved and reviewed GPB, with its detailed PPAs and its corresponding budget; and
The Budget Officer to present in the SAAOB any additional or decuction in the original approved GAD Plan and Budget that resulted from supplemental/realignment, if any.

We recommended the GFPS and MPDO to coordinate with the departments in setting up budget for activities that are allowed to be charged against GAD budget to avoid inconsistensies. We also recommend that in computing for the GAD budget the LGU use its total approved annual budget to ensure that not less that 5% of which would be allocated to GAD.
Further, we recommended that the GFPS prepare the GAD accomplishment report based on the approved GAD Plan and budget for proper evaluation of the implementation of the PPAs for GAD.

We recommended that Management, thru the GFPS, to maximize the utilization of the identified GAD program, projects and activities to achieve the objectives of the government regarding the institutionalization of Gender and Development in the communities.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management direct that GFPS Profile Form for the newly organized GFPS be accomplished to facilitate determination/evaluation on who among the members need training and what GAD capacity building programs needs to be undertaken. Henceforth, require that GFPS to attend/undergo trainings to advance the GAD advocacy of the Municipality. The annual GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipality should consistently include capacity building for the GFPS members.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommentation that Management direct the Municipal Accounting Office to maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for GAD, the Municipal Budget Office to provide the necessary data/information in the OBR and require the regular reconciliation of balances between the two offices to ensure consistency, accuracy, and completeness of financial records.

We recommended that Management and the LSB stop the practice of disbursing claims that do not fall under the categories enumerated in DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2017 dated January 19, 2017 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2020 dated August 27, 2020 and strictly adhere to COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012, to avoid disallowance.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the LSB prepare and submit to the Audit Team the detailed annual accomplishment report of the Special Education Fund for CY 2023 for validation and verification of the reported utilization of the said fund.

We recommended that Management and the LSB strictly comply with the required posting of SEF Utilization Report in Municipality’s Full Disclosure Policy Bulletin Board for transparency.
We also recommended that oversight agencies be furnished with the required reports in compliance with DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 2017-01 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2019-149 and furnish a copy to the Auditor’s Office for verification.

We recommended that Management direct all concerned to furnish the Office of the Auditor with a copy of perfected contracts or purchase orders within five (5) working days upon approval together with the supporting documents for review.
We also recommended the management require the supplier to properly fill up and provide date of received/confirmation of Purchase Order (PO) upon deliver/serve by the procuring agency.

We recommended that Management together with the Sangguniang Bayan give priority the revision and updating of the 2013 Revenue Code of the Municipality, pursuant to the provisions of the Local Government Code, in order to increase tax revenues and receipts through updating of the rates in the existing tax code and provision for new revenue sources, which will augment their budget for a better delivery of government services.

Require the Municipal Budget Office to render a systematic and realistic revenue forecasting taking into account prior years’ experiences and statistics as aid in setting targets; and
Require the Municipal Treasury and other Departments involved in generating revenues to meet its targets by conducting periodic review of collection performance by the concerned officials to ascertain whether collection targets are attained and to analyze the reasons for the shortfall in collections and find alternative solutions to minimize, reduce or avoid incurrence of shortfall in the future.

We commended Management for achieving a high collection efficiency on locally sourced revenues, having significant gains in the revenue generation during the year; however, we recommended that management conduct periodic review of accomplishments in order to have assurance that all revenue sources are tapped to maximize its revenue generating powers as mandated by law.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management adhere to the provisions stipulated in RA No. 9003 by requiring concerned officials/employees and the SWM Board the preparation of an annual SWM plan/budget and accomplishment report on the strategies implemented at the of the year for submission to the NSWC copy furnished the Audit Team.
We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive spearhead the creation of the plantilla position of MENRO position and its staff.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that Management require the Municipal Accounting Office to maintain separate subsidiary ledger for the transactions under SWMP and conduct periodic reconciliation of the expenses and balances with the records maintained by the Municipal Budget Office.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Human Resource Management Officer to attach the job order contracts together with the monthly accomplishment reports duly verified and signed by their immediate supervisors and submit these as attachment to their payroll in accordance to Section 1.2.1 of COA Circular 2012-001.
We also recommended the Human Resource Management Officer to provide all personnel under the job contract status, a copy of their job contract for their reference and being as a party involved in the contract.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant to process only those payrolls with complete attachments in accordance to Section 1.2.1 of COA Circular 2012-001.

We recommended that Management:
Require the HRMO to formulate a Human Resource Plan and issue a uniform policy guideline on the hiring of JO personnel to appropriately determine the necessity of the hiring thereof and as well as to strengthen the internal controls pertaining to; and
Require the HRMO to adopt a schedule of standard rates for JOs per position that is based on qualification and the nature of work/assignment taking into consideration the prevailing minimum wage in the locality, in order not to create inequality in the workforce of the LGU.
Henceforth, we recommended that Management categorically stipulate in the Job Order Contract or Contract of Services the fund charging and the specific nature of work assignments and/or specific duties and responsibilities, office assignments and quantity of work to be accomplished and the desired work outputs to facilitate comparison and evaluation of actual work accomplishments of hired job orders.
Further, we recommended that Management require the HRMO to observe proper monitoring of attendance of personnel since their contract was on daily/monthly wage.

We recommended that Management submit a list of all on-going government projects/programs/activities and those that are to be implemented in CY 2023; and hereafter, submit the same at the beginning of each year.

We recommended that Management inform the Audit Team within ten days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity of any postings of project signboards and/or public notice for validation by the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to conduct the necessary analysis of the accounts and periodic reconciliation of their records in order to establish the balance of the Inter-agency accounts and remit the BIR taxes, GSIS, Pab-Ibig premiums due;
Require the Human Resource Management Office, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, to ensure that the contractual employees of the Municipality secure their Pag-Ibig account numbers, as pre-requisite in the acceptance of remittances, in order to avoid further accumulation of the unremitted balances of Due to Pag-Ibig account; and
Require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to ensure the timely remittance of the mandatory deductions to the concerned government agencies at the end of each month.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the MT to consistently monitor the release of checks issued by the Municipality and perform her task of informing the payees of the existence of checks due them before the said checks become stale.  Likewise, adjust the cashbook records of the SEF for the cancelled check identified.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive to instruct the MA to:
Continue the preparation and submission of BRS within 10 days upon receipt of the BS;
Immediately prepare correcting/adjusting entries for discrepancies/errors or other reconciling items requiring corrections in order to reflect the correct balance of cash account in the financial statements; and
Immediately record all unrecorded unreleased checks as credit to the CIB, LCCA account.  Henceforth, all checks issued shall be recorded in the CkDJ on the date of the check.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue tracing and analyzing the transactions, and reconcile the remaining difference of ₱361,255.54 in the reciprocal accounts Due to/Due from Other Funds and prepare the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances of the accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds as of December 31, 2023.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the Municipal Inventory Team to initiate the process of disposing the unserviceable properties by preparing the IIRUP in accordance with Section 125 of the Manual on the NGAS, Volume I.
After the disposal, direct the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the derecognition of the unserviceable properties from the books of account.

We recommended that Management continue to comply strictly with the requirements of Section 89 of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002 and all related rules and regulations with regard to the granting, utilization and liquidation of CAs.

We recommended that the Management require the DO to immediately apply for an increase in bond, within the threshold of her total accountability, as required in Section 5.1 of the Bureau of Treasury Order No. 02-2009 dated August 6,2009.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to submit promptly the required monthly and quarterly financial reports on or before the due dates in compliance with Item 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010.
We recommended that Management continuously ensure the timely preparation and submission of the financial reports and accounts as required in the existing laws and regulations.

We recommended that Management require the General Services Officer to submit the copies of Contracts/PO’s to the AT, with the required supporting documents attached thereto, within five days after perfection thereof, and the related Notification of Delivery of Services/Supplies and Materials, within 24 hours after acceptance of such deliveries of services/supplies and materials, in compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular 96-010.

We recommended that the LCE inform the ATL within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity of any postings of project signboards and/or public notice for validation by the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer provide the Audit Team at the beginning of the year, a list of all on-going and to be implemented PPAs during the year with detailed information prescribed under COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.

We recommended that Management direct the BAC to:
Strictly comply to regularly prepare and submit an approved PMR in accordance with the pertinent provisions of RA No. 9184 and in the format prescribed in GPPB Circular No. 03-2017; and
Prepare the APP based on the prescribed format as provided in GPPB Circular No. 07-2015 and ensure that all PPMPs are properly consolidated and procurement are entirely incorporated to reflect the transparency of procurement activities within the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC and TWG to:
Scrutinize the sufficiency of the winning bidder’s manpower on all projects to be awarded and not on an individual basis only in cases where the latter is the lowest bidder in several projects which have similar or overlapping implementation period in order to oblige the winning contractor to supplement its manpower to be utilized in the projects awarded to it or to disqualify it from obtaining several awards or contracts in conformity with the provisions of section 34.1 and 34.3 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184;
Monitor contractors with on-going projects with other government agencies and to coordinate with them regarding infrastructure projects not disclosed in the Statement of On-going and Completed projects to avoid overlapping and delay in projects accomplishment and/or substandard quality of work; and
Ensure that pertinent provisions of RIRR of RA No. 9184 are strictly adhered to promote transparency and economy of its procurement process.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to monitor the simultaneous implementation of projects by the same contractor to guard against unwarranted delays or substandard quality of work.

We commended Management for the substantial implementation of the PPAs under the 20% DF through their systematic planning and strategic execution which achieved desired benefits to the community within the timeline.  Nevertheless, we recommend that the Municipal Development Council (MDC) and all other concerned Offices of the MGC implement fully all PPAs targeted during the year within the timeframe to ensure that anticipated local developments will be realized.
We also commended Management for appropriating and utilizing the 20% DF in PPAs that are consistent with the desirable socio-economic targets and environmental outcomes as mandated under Section 287 of R.A. No. 7160.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to review and analyze the unexpended balances of appropriations of completed projects for reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund in accordance with Section 322 of RA No. 7160.
We also recommended that Management revert to the General Fund for reappropriation all excess balances of projects already implemented.

We recommended that the LDRRM Council (LDRRMC) coordinate with the Municipal Accounting Office so that accurate figures are included in the supplemental LDRRMIF in order to comply with the prescribed guidelines.
Also, require the LDRRMC to prepare a resolution that will amend the appropriation for the capital outlay project - Establishment of Camalig Command Center (Municipal Emergency Operation Center).
We recommended that the LDRRMC refrain from incurring and charging expenses unrelated to disaster risk reduction and management activities to the LDRRMF pursuant to RA No. 10121.

We commended the MGC for prioritizing the utilization of the LDRRMF prior years’ unexpended balance.
Nonetheless, we recommended that the MGC continuously monitor the utilization of the LDRRMF unexpended balance and revert back to general fund, if any, the unutilized amount after five years to be programmed to other social service identified by the local sanggunian.

We recommended that the Management instruct:
the Municipal Engineer that SWA/Progress/Accomplishment Report of all ongoing projects shall be submitted to the Audit Team (AT) pursuant to Annex “C” of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 in order that the AT will be able to conduct inspection/validation during the stages of the implementation of the project; and
Municipal Accountant shall see to it that SWA/Progress/Accomplishment Report are duly received by the AT before facilitating payment of progress billings/final payment.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to require the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board (MSWMB) to:
re-evaluate the ten-year Solid Waste Management Plan especially the diverted waste projection if still attainable. Also, strictly implement the “No Segregation, No Collection Policy” to decrease the residual waste for disposal; and
conduct massive IEC on waste segregation at source, reuse and recycling of wastes.
We also recommended that the LCE require the MENRO to maintain the records relative to daily weights or volumes of waste received and processed and tracking of success of waste diversion goal.

We recommended that Management submit immediately the required certifications signed by the LCE or any authorized approving officials to support the travel expenses paid to JO personnel in CY 2023 amounting to ₱278,373.28, lest these will be suspended in audit.
Henceforth, the LCE direct the department heads, as immediate supervisors of JO workers, to stop the practice of allowing these personnel to attend conferences, trainings and other travel-related tasks, unless in compliance strictly with the criteria set forth in Section 3(a) of EO No. 77, series of 2019.  Also, issue certifications signed by the LCE or any authorized approving officials as required under paragraph B(5) of COA Resolution No. 2021-044 and attached the same to support future travel claims.

We recommended that Management seriously consider the adoption of ATM Payroll System instead of the cash payment system, which is a much safer scheme, precludes possible loss or misapplication and can promote operational efficiency.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require:
The Municipal Treasurer to provide the correct SET income estimate for the year based on the projected RPT collections;
The LSB, MAO and the MBO to coordinate properly so that accurate figures will be incorporated in the SEF annual and supplemental budgets.
The LSB to prepare the SEF Budget in accordance with the timeline set forth in DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 01, s. 2017.
We recommend that the LSB implement fully all PPAs targeted during the year within the timeframe to ensure optimal utilization of the SEF Budget.

We recommended that Management, through the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), expedite the full establishment of the GAD database in order to have a basis for planning and programming PPAs and formulating policies that are gender responsive and to be able to undertake thorough gender analysis based on relevant data to properly identify existing gender issues and ensure that the approved PPAs to be incorporated in the GAD Plan and Budget are gender responsive.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Person continue to develop and implement GAD related programs/projects/activities that advocate women empowerment, promote gender equality and improve the welfare of women, and ensure that planned GAD PPAs                          are all timely implemented based on its approved GAD Plan and Budget.

However, we recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Accountant and designated AAO make a reconciliation of previous years’ records with GSIS;
Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to immediately remit all the premium contributions from prior years after reconciliation of records is made; and
AAO to observe prompt submission of ARA to GSIS whenever there are changes in the member’s data in accordance with Section 4 of GSIS MC No. 3, Series of 2012, to ensure timely updating of the GSIS database and to prevent inaccuracies in the billing statements.

We recommended that Management require the job order workers to apply for Pag-IBIG MID and immediately inform the accounting office for remittance purposes.
We also recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Accountant and designated AAO make a reconciliation of previous years’ records with HDMF;
Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to immediately remit all the premium contributions from prior years after reconciliation of records is made; and
AAO to immediately update the HDMF whenever there are changes in the member’s data to ensure timely updating of the HFMD database and to prevent inaccuracies in the billing statements.

We commended the Management for ensuring that all insurable properties are insured in the GSIS consistent with Section 2 and 5 of the RA No, 656.
Also, we recommended that the Management assure the timely renewal of the insurance policy of the various properties.

We commended Management for preparing the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) for all of the unserviceable properties subject for disposal.
Further, that the Appraisal and Disposal Committee conducted the appraisal of the unserviceable properties to determine the modes of disposal and the appraised values of the properties to be disposed to facilitate its immediate disposal in accordance with Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 89-296.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant revert the remaining amount of stale checks to the Cash-in Bank and Accounts Payable accounts to ensure the proper presentation of the account balances.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned offices to hasten the implementation of the partially and unimplemented 20% Local Development Fund programs, projects and activities to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the general public.
We recommended that Management continue to improve its performance in the submission of the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund.

We recommended that Management maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF to cover all the four thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management in order to enhance the preparedness of the Municipality against disasters and avoid, lessen or limit their adverse impacts to the community.

We recommended to the Management to follow strictly the regulation in the utilization of the 30% Quick Response Fund (QRF) of the 5% Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund as provided in DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013
Moreover, we recommended that Management strictly observe the guidelines and documentary requirements in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and Section 5 (f) of the GAM Vol I.

We commended Management for appropriately transferring the unexpended balance of the 5% LDRRMF for CY 2023 to the Special Trust Fund amounting to ₱5,507,117.78 and properly reverting the unutilized balance aged more than five (5) years in the Special Trust Fund to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund amounting to ₱188,429.26 in compliance to Section 21 of RA 10121 and Items 5.1.10 and 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We therefore recommended that the focal point/person for the GAD Program of the agency to adhere strictly with proper recognition of Gender-Related Issues in considering programs ‘projects/and activities for the GAD Plan and Budget in the succeeding year.
We likewise reiterated our recommendation that the members of the GAD Focal Point System attend seminars on HGDG to enhance compliance with the proper preparation of the GAD PB/AR.

We commended the Management’s compliance in creating a Solid Waste Management Board in accordance with Section 12 of R.A. No. 9003 and Section 5, Rule VI of DENR Administrative Order No. 2001-34 or the Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. 9003.
We commended the Management and the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board for reviewing and updating the Solid Waste Management Plan every year pursuant to Section 16 of RA 9003.

We recommended that Management:
Apply or renew the contracts of insurance with the GSIS to ensure security and financial assurance in the event of unforeseen circumstances that could potentially impact the property adversely;
Properly monitor the insurance of all properties with the GSIS to avoid payment of unnecessary penalties, surcharges and damages to third party liabilities in case of vehicular accidents; and
Ensure that properties with insurance policies are existing properties of the Municipality and therefore be included in the RPCPPE.

We recommended that:
The Municipal Accountant ensure that payments for overtime services rendered be made only when such claims are supported with complete documentary requirements; and
Prospectively, the LCE ensure that the LGU submit to the DBM, copy furnished the CSC, the Report on Overtime Services with Pay using the template in Annex A of the Joint Circular on or before March 31 of every year.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the concerned officials and employees to immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents as provided under Annex B; and
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to check the completeness of the supporting documents before processing the claims for travel expenses.

We recommended that Management require the accountable officers to strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 on the prescribed period within which to liquidate cash advances.

We recommended that Management:
ensure that claims for reimbursement of actual accommodation expenses in excess of the accommodation component of the DTE must be supported with a certification by the agency head or authorized representative as absolutely necessary in the performance of an assignment (Sample shown in Annex C); and
ensure strict compliance on the laws, rules and regulations on the claims for traveling expenses to avoid possible misuse of government funds.

We recommended that Management submit comments and justification for why the aforementioned transaction should not be disallowed in audit, subject to evaluation by the Audit Team. Henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of EO No. 77 regarding the criteria for authorized official travel.

We recommended that Management submit comments and justification for why the aforementioned transaction should not be disallowed in audit, subject to evaluation by the Audit Team. Henceforth, strictly adhere to governing laws, rules and regulations to ensure regularity of transactions.

We commended Management for adhering to accounting and reporting guidelines on the receipt and utilization of donations in-kind from local and foreign sources for DRRM program which resulted to a comprehensive reports and availability of information on the receipt and utilization of disaster relief aids.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
The Municipal Treasurer and other officials concerned to exert more efforts to collect the rentals and business taxes from delinquent market stall occupants using the procedures provided under the Contract of Lease and those included in the internal policy of the Municipality and enforce the civil remedies provided by the present Revenue Code, if necessary, in order to generate additional income that will be used for the implementation of programs, projects and activities of the Municipality; and
The Municipal Treasurer to periodically provide the Market Administrator of the list of delinquent stallholders and the amount still due and demandable for monitoring purposes, and strictly enforce the terms and conditions stipulated in the market stall contracts relative to the automatic revocation or cancellation of the contract upon non-compliance of the terms and conditions stipulated in the Contract of Lease.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
a.	The Municipal Treasurer to provide the Municipal Accountant with the List of Delinquent Market Stallholders with amount due and penalties before the end of each year to facilitate timely recording of appropriate receivable and income accounts; and
b.	The Municipal Accountant to recognize the total unpaid rentals, business taxes, surcharges and interests as of December 31, 2023 in the books of the Municipality to the appropriate receivable and income/equity account, in compliance with the provisions of PPSAS 13 in order to present fairly the financial statements of the Municipality.

We recommended Management the following courses of action:
Consider taking appropriate legal action and/or blacklisting of the contractor involved as provided under Item 4.2 of GPPB Resolution No. 09-2004; and
Initiate take-over of contract pursuant to Item V, D (3) of Annex H of RA 9184, if necessary.

We recommended Management the following courses of action:
Demand for the immediate refund/return of the unrecouped advances;
Forfeit the corresponding bond posted by the contractor for the advance payment, if still applicable; and
Initiate legal action if the contractor refused to refund the unrecouped advances, if warranted.

We recommended that Management prepare and submit within the prescribed period the BRS together with the bank statements and attachments and to promptly prepare the adjusting entries for the reconciling items to arrive at a correct balance for the Cash in Bank account.

We recommended that the LCE:
Require the concerned Accountable Officer/s to submit the accounts and financial reports to the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to the timelines of the abovementioned existing laws, rules and regulations;
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting Office all the disbursements vouchers, payrolls, official receipts, including the corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month or pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of rules and regulations, as applicable; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to submit the remaining accounts, DVs and supporting documents and strictly comply with the timelines on the recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.
We recommended that Management improve its performance in the submission of the Trial Balances and the Financial Statements.

We recommended that Management submit henceforth, copies of all perfected government contracts involving acquisition of land including all supporting documents within five working days from execution pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 to facilitate timely auditorial and technical review thereof.
Further, for Management to provide a copy of the DENR Clearance and CAR to the Audit Team considering that considerable length of time has already passed from its payment. Also, to apprise the Audit Team of the status of transfer of title in the name of the LGU for this land acquisition.
We recommended that the Municipality expedite the securement of the title to the land so as to have an unimpeded exercise of all legal rights as the owner of the land.

(51) Require the Municipal Treasurer to provide the Licensing Office the list of all delinquent business owners before the ensuing year commences.
(52) Require the Licensing Office to closely monitor that all business establishments have paid all permits necessary to commence and continue its operations within the reglementary period; thereafter prepare a report to the Office of the Mayor on business establishments who are operating illegally after their conduct of inspection; and
(53) Enforce its police power by issuing a closure order for business establishments who are operating illegally.

(54) We recommended that Management direct the issuance of a Contract of Lease in CY 2023 to qualified lessees, incorporating therein the duties and responsibilities of the lessees as well as other necessary stipulations to safeguard the interest of the Municipality.
(55) Henceforth, the Municipality should see to it that contracts are properly prepared and issued in all its lease agreements.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer:
(56) With the help of the Municipal Assessor, create strategies to intensify tax collection campaign in order to increase local resources which would help finance the delivery of basic services and facilities;
(57) Regularly update the list of delinquent real property tax thus create impact on the total amount due for collection; and
(58) Coordinate or request the Provincial Office to send final demand letters to delinquent taxpayers and avail of the remedies through levy on real property or judicial actions provided under the Local Government Code of 1991.

We recommended that the Municipal Assessor henceforth request the concerned Registrar of Deeds to furnish their Office every year a copy of the abstract of the Registry of Property, accompanied by copies of corresponding deeds of sale, donation, or partition, or other forms of alienation pursuant to the above-cited Manual.

We recommended that Management:
(60) Require the Municipal Human Resource Management Office to assess the existing organizational structure so that the ideal number of plantilla personnel will be established and submit a report with recommendation thereon to the Local Chief Executive (LCE) for approval by appropriate bodies;
(61) Create policies and guidelines to address the necessity and propriety of hiring JOs taking into consideration the existing plantilla personnel; and
(62) Require applicants to submit primary personal identification, undergo an interview and evaluate the performance of each personnel that would give assurance that the risks identified above are addressed.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendations that Management:
(63) Stop the practice of giving different rates to JOs having the same position in the LGU in order not to create inequality in the workforce of the LGU; and
(64) Require the HRMO to adopt a schedule of standard rates for JOs per position that is based on qualification and the nature of work/assignment taking into consideration the prevailing minimum wage in the locality
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipality stop the hiring of street sweepers as they perform the same services already being rendered by the barangay street sweepers.
We recommended that the Local DRRM Office be institutionalized and organized according to law by assigning full-time permanent employees thereat to maximize its efficiency and effectiveness.

We recommended that the Municipality: (i) submit plausible explanations for all the deficiencies noted and lacking supporting documents to arrive at a decision in audit; (ii) strictly comply with the provisions of the RIRR of RA No. 9184 on the procurement of various infrastructure projects with regard to post-qualification, contract award and contract signing, among others, to enhance transparency and establish regularity of the procurement process and award of contracts.

The Audit Team recommended that the Municipality submit all lacking documents relating to the procurement of various infrastructure projects as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and in compliance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445.

The Audit Team recommended that the Municipality address this issue on ownership of the location of the project to protect the interest of the LGU. Henceforth, for the Municipality to secure a notarized deed of sale or deed of donation on the property where the project will be constructed prior to the award of the contract in accordance with Section 17.6 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct Ms. Morada to refund the honoraria received as BOT member and that she be relieved as BOT member in compliance with the requirement under CHED Memorandum Order No. 42 series of 2016. We further recommend that the LGU be circumspect in appointing individuals to positions in committees/boards to avoid payment of irregular expenditures.

We recommended that the Management establish the Municipal Internal Audit Office following the guidelines set forth under DBM Local Budget Circular (LBC) No. 110 in the organizational structure and staffing of office to further improve and strengthen the internal control system of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to review and recheck the validity and correctness of claims appearing in the trial balance and financial statements. Necessary adjustments may be effected in case of invalid or insufficient claims by the agency to reflect the correct amounts, thus, present reliable accurate financial reports of the agency. A request for write-off maybe filed with COA for those long outstanding accounts as long as there is a proof that the agency has exhausted all available legal and administrative remedies to enforce collections pursuant to existing COA Regulations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct:

The Municipal Accountant to:
hasten the preparation of the BRS and immediately submit the complete set of BRS to the AT for proper validation and audit; and
immediately prepare correcting/adjusting entries for discrepancies/ errors or other reconciling items requiring corrections in order to reflect the correct balance of the cash account in the financial statements.
The Municipal Treasurer to:
Coordinate with the Depository Banks for the prompt delivery of the monthly BS with the supporting documents, but in cases where the BS are not yet available, secure snapshot copy of the bank transactions to facilitate the timely reconciliation of its bank accounts; and/or
Enroll in online banking services of the depository banks.
We also recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile periodically their respective books/records so that discrepancies in their balances do not accumulate and errors are immediately detected and corrected.

We recommended that Management require:
The Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort in the retrieval of detailed information and documents on said receivables;
The Municipal Treasurer to send demand letters and to intensify efforts to require the principal borrowers as well as the co-makers, if any, of those involved to pay the unpaid balances of their loans. In case of continuous default in payment of loans, seek assistance for the enforcement of legal remedies in the collection of long outstanding receivables and for the filing of appropriate charges without prejudice to the negligent officers and delinquent borrowers; and
The Municipal Accountant to review and analyze the accounts to ascertain the collectability of the receivables and the probability of liquidation/refund from various accountable persons. Henceforth, consider requesting to the Commission on Audit authority to write-off dormant accounts which could no longer be collected despite diligent efforts, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Consider the creation of a new Inventory Committee that will perform diligently annual comprehensive physical inventory count of all MGG’s properties;
Require the MAO and GSO to maintain complete and upated PPELCs, PCs and the related AREs and conduct periodic reconciliation of PPE records and adopt the applicable procedures prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 for the one-time cleansing of MGD’s PPE accounts; and
Require the MAO to effect necessary adjustments.

We recommended that the LCE require the GSO/Inventory Committee to identify properties without tags/stickers and attach the Property Tags/Stickers on all properties for easier cross-examination with AREs, facilitate identification of the properties during inventory count, and to establish accountability and control in the custody or use of the properties.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the General Services Officer to submit to the Auditor the following documents for evaluation:
Inventory and Inspection Report for Unserviceable Property (IIRUP);
Certificate of disposal by the Municipal Engineer, if any; and
List of materials without value.

We commended the efforts of the Municipal Accountant and its staff to meet the legal deadline on the submission of the annual Financial Statements for CY 2023 as required by law and for their continuous hard work to making quality and reliable financial reports.

Also, we recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to submit promptly the required monthly and quarterly financial reports on or before the due dates in compliance with Item 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010.

We recommended that Management:
Require the concerned Accountable Officer to submit the accounts and financial reports to the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to the timeliness of the abovementioned existing law, rules and regulations;
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting Office all the disbursements vouchers, payrolls, official receipts, including the corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month or pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of rules and regulations, as applicable; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the timeliness on the recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Property Officer to submit copies of Contracts/PO’s to the Audit Team, with the required supporting documents attached thereto, within five days after perfection thereof, and the related Notification of Delivery of Services/Supplies and Materials, within 24 hours after acceptance of such deliveries of services/supplies and materials, in compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular 96-010.
Explain/ justify as to why the Notification of Delivery of Services/Supplies and Materials were not furnished the auditorwithin 24 hours from acceptance.

We recommended that the LCE inform the ATL within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity of any postings of project signboards and/or public notice for validation by the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer provide the Audit Team at the beginning of the year, a list of all on-going and to be implemented PPAs during the year with detailed information prescribed under COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.
We recommended that the MA scrutinized the transactions of the LDRRMF and ensure the accuracy of the amount booked in the Trust Liability – DRRM account.

We recommended that the Implementing Office to fast track the implementation of all projects so that the target beneficiaries could avail themselves of the benefits of the projects and improve the living and economic condition of the constituents of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management submit justification/reason for its inability to submit variation orders to the Office of the Auditor.
We also recommended that Management comply with the provisions of COA Circular 2009-001 on the timely submission of contracts and variation orders for review and audit.

We recommended that Management submit justification/reason for its inability to disburse the fund for the seven completed projects.
We also recommended that Management revert the excess funds to the National Treasury.
Henceforth, strict adherence to the rules and regulations on the utilization of trust funds is enjoined.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the requirements under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and to ensure keeping copies of the MOAs for proper evaluation of the implementation and timely verification of the veracity and completeness of the disbursements made in relation to these trust accounts.

We recommended that the Management:
Require concerned implementing offices to reconcile their records with the records of SA as to the unliquidated, unrefunded and/or unremitted balances as to date; and
Ensure prompt liquidation, remittance and/or refund of balances to the source or intended agency.

We recommended that Management make the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the said balances to their appropriate accounts to fairly present all affected accounts in the FS.
We also recommended that Management revert to the General Fund for re-appropriation excess balances of counterpart for projects already implemented.

We recommend that Management require, thru the offices-in-charge to:
Review operations to determine the bottleneck and the reasons why the said grants were not yet implemented and utilized as to date and identify measures that would address the reasons and act accordingly;
Ensure timely and efficient utilization of funds more importantly those coming from the NGAs as required in the MOA, existing laws, rules and regulations; and
Make certain that the unused funds be promptly returned to the Source Agency.
We also recommend that Management designate a focal person who would be coordinating and responsible for monitoring the status of project and/or programs implementation of funds transferred from SAs, be it from NGA, GOCC or LGU.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
LDRRM Council to use the unexpended and excess balances of the LDRRMF by incorporating these in the LDRRMFIP and submit the same, including supplemental Investment Programs, if any, to the AT within the prescribed period; and
LDRRMF Council, MPDO, Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to coordinate properly so that accurate figures will be incorporated in the LDRRMFIP.

We recommended that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management (LDRRM) Council to optimize the utilization of the LDRRMF of the LGU in order to strengthen its disaster preparedness and avoid potential damages during disasters.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a) The BAC and TWG
scrutinize the sufficiency of the winning bidder’s manpower on all projects to be awarded and not on an individual basis only in cases where the latter is the lowest bidder in several projects which have similar or overlapping implementation period in order to oblige the winning contractor to supplement its manpower to be utilized in the projects awarded to it or to disqualify it from obtaining several awards or contracts in conformity with the provisions of section 34.1 and 34.3 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184;
monitor contractors with on-going projects with other government agencies and to coordinate with them regarding infrastructure projects not disclosed in the Statement of On-going and Completed projects to avoid overlapping and delay in projects accomplishment and/or substandard quality of work; and
explain why the operational timeline of the project was not met, particularly the issuance of Notice to Proceed was beyond the allowed maximum calendar days as prescribed in the Annex C of the 2016 Revised IRR.
b) The Municipal Engineer
to monitor the simultaneous implementation of projects by the same contractor to guard against unwarranted delays or substandard quality of work; and
strictly comply to regularly prepare and submit timely the approved Procurement Monitoring Report (PMR) in accordance with the pertinent provisions of RA No. 9184 and in the format prescribed in GPPB Circular No. 03-2017.

We recommended that the Management submit a justification for the failure to disposed within the timeframe, moreover, to provide attainable solution to minimize the storing period of the MGG’s residual waste in the TRCA.

We recommended that the LCE:
Require the HRMU to assess the existing Organizational Structure, expedite filling up the vacant positions thru advertisement and assessment of qualified employees for promotion, and submit a report thereon to the LCE for approval by appropriate bodies;
Make written policy/guidelines to address the issue on the necessity and propriety of hiring JO workers, who should not perform tasks assigned to existing permanent and regular employees per their job descriptions; and
Limit the hiring/deployment to those offices that are actually extremely in need of their services; and
Incorporate in the JO contract the number of man hours, quantity and duration of the work to be performed by the JO personnel and require them to submit quantified accomplishment reports and signed copies of contracts to support payment of wages.

We recommended that Management seriously consider the adoption of ATM Payroll System instead of the cash payment system, which is a much safer scheme, precludes possible loss or misapplication and can promote operational efficiency.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require:
The Municipal Treasurer, LSB and the MBO to coordinate properly so that accurate figures are included in the SEF Budget computation;
