The LSB to prepare the SEF Budget in accordance with the timeline set forth in DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 01, s. 2017; and
The LSB to monitor the efficient implementation and immediate realization of identified priority education PPAs to achieve the optimum utilization of SEF and achievement of its objectives.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
the Local Finance Committee to see to it that at least five per cent of the municipal government’s total budget are appropriated for GAD; and/or
the GFPS to attribute for GAD related activities to complete at least five per cent of the total appropriation for the year.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) Executive Committee to ensure the full implementation of GAD Plan and Budget in compliance with JMC 2013-01.

We recommended that Management:
ensure that insurable properties are insured in the GSIS; and
have a monitoring of the lapsing of these insurance policies to prevent delay on the renewal of the policy.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive requires the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entry to reflect the valid reconciling items in the books of accounts.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive to instruct the MA to:
Continue the preparation and submission of BRS within 10 days upon receipt of the BS;
Immediately prepare correcting/adjusting entries for discrepancies/errors or other reconciling items requiring corrections in order to reflect the correct balance of cash account in the financial statements; and
Immediately record all unrecorded unreleased checks as credit to the CIB, LCCA account.  Henceforth, all checks issued shall be recorded in the CkDJ on the date of the check.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to conduct immediate reconciliation of the individual balances of the Cash-in Local Treasury account with the pertinent Cashbooks and to make the necessary adjustments thereon.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to record the DRRM Funds in the Special Trust Fund Books under the Trust Liability – DRRM account.

Also, we recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to submit promptly the required monthly and quarterly financial reports on or before the due dates in compliance with Item 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010.
We recommended that the management require the Municipal Accountant to submit the required reports to the audit team pursuant to the aforecited regulations.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the GSO to initiate the process of disposing the unserviceable properties by preparing the IIRUP in accordance with Section 125 of the Manual on the NGAS, Volume I.
After the disposal, direct the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the derecognition of the unserviceable properties from the books of account.

We recommend the following courses of action:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to strengthen the accounting and processing system being part of the accounting and fiscal control process and ensure that transactions processed, paid and recorded are properly approved and duly supported with complete documents and/or technical requirements and proper signatures; and
Require the concerned Municipal personnel to use the Checklist provided for under the Revised Documentary Requirements for Common Government Transactions prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and the Revised RIRR of RA No. 9184 as a useful guide in the processing of claims for payment.

We recommended that the LCE inform the ATL within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity of any postings of project signboards and/or public notice for validation by the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer provide the Audit Team at the  beginning of the year, a list of all on-going and to be implemented PPAs during the year  with detailed information prescribed under COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January  30, 2013.

We recommended that Management direct the BAC to Strictly comply to  regularly prepare and submit an approved PMR in accordance with the pertinent  provisions of  RA No. 9184 and in the format prescribed in GPPB Circular No. 03-2017.

We recommended that the Implementing Office to fast track the implementation of the on-going projects so that the target beneficiaries could avail of the benefits of the programs and improve the living and economic condition of the constituents of the Municipality.

We commended Management for the substantial implementation of the PPAs under the 20% DF through their systematic planning and strategic execution which achieved desired benefits to the community within the timeline.  Nevertheless, we recommended that the Municipal Development Council (MDC) and all other concerned Offices of the MGC implement fully all PPAs targeted during the year within the timeframe to ensure that anticipated local developments will be realized.
We also commended Management for appropriating and utilizing the 20% DF in PPAs that are consistent with the desirable socio-economic targets and environmental outcomes as mandated under Section 287 of R.A. No. 7160.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
MDRRM Council to monitor properly the utilization of LDDRM Fund;
MDRRM Council to use the unexpended and excess balances of the LDRRMF by incorporating these in the LDRRMFIP and submit the same, including supplemental Investment Programs, if any, to the AT within the prescribed period; and
LDRRMO and the MAO to coordinate properly so that accurate figures will be incorporated in the LDRRMFIP.

We recommended that the LDRRM Council (LDRRMC) coordinate with the Municipal Budget Office so that accurate figures are included in the supplemental LDRRMF Budget.
Also, we reiterated our recommendation that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management (LDRRM) Council to optimize the utilization of the LDRRMF of the LGU in order to strengthen its disaster preparedness and avoid potential damages during disasters.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board (MSWMB) to:
strictly implement the “No Segregation, No Collection Policy” to decrease the residual waste for disposal; and
conduct massive IEC on waste segregation at source, reuse and recycling of wastes.
We also recommended that the LCE require the MENRO to maintain the records relative to record of daily weights or volumes of waste received and processed and tracking of success of waste reduction programs.

We recommended that Management, through the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), expedite the full establishment of the GAD database in order to have a basis for planning and programming PPAs and formulating policies that are gender responsive and to be able to undertake thorough gender analysis based on relevant data to properly identify existing gender issues and ensure that the approved PPAs to be incorporated in the GAD Plan and Budget are gender responsive.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Person submit the CY 2023 GAD Plan and Budget as well as GAD Accomplishment Report to the Audit Team for evaluation and verification.

We reiterated prior year’s recommendations that Management:
Direct the Inventory Committee to continue with the actual physical count of PPEs and reconciliation of inventory count with Accounting records which is aligned with the guidelines of COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to determine the details of the PPEs with unidentified lump sum balances in order that the balances appearing in the Accounting records may be ascertained; and
Require the GSO to maintain complete Property Cards in the form prescribed by the NGAS Manual.

We recommended that Management reconcile the differences of the PPE balances between the Statement of Financial Position, Notes to Financial Statements and PPE Ledger Cards for fair presentation in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant trace and analyze the transactions, and reconcile the difference of ₱1,327,196.33in the reciprocal accounts Due to/Due from Other Funds and prepare the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances of the accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds as of December 31, 2023.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that Management:
Instruct the IOs to properly coordinate with the Accounting and Treasury Departments when a fund is about to be released by an NGA or other sources so that proper accounting, recording and reporting thereof can be made; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries after a thorough verification and analysis has been made thereof, and collating the necessary supporting documents to substantiate the same, for a fair presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the BAC, prepare and submit the required PMR to the GPPB in the prescribed formats within 14 days after the end of each semester to ensure that the Municipality abides by the standards set forth by RA 9184 and its IRR per Section 12.2 of its Revised IRR and GPPB Circular 03-2015 dated March 20, 2015 and Government Procurement Policy Board Circular No. 02-2020 dated May 20, 2020.

We recommended that the Local Executive Chief require:
The end users or implementing units of the procuring entity to formulate their PPMP for different PPAs in accordance with Section 7.3.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184;
The BAC Secretariat to prepare the Consolidated APP and submit the same to the BAC for final recommendation of the appropriate procurement modality; and
Submit a copy of the Consolidated APP to the Audit Team for verification.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting Office all the disbursements vouchers, payrolls, official receipts, including the corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month or pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of rules and regulations, as applicable; and
Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the timelines on the recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to immediately release the respective amount payable to the 44 barangays of the Municipal Government as well as to the Province of Albay as mandated under the existing regulations.
Further, require the Offices of the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to strictly adhere on the guidelines on the release of barangay shares from realty taxes as stated under Section 271 (d) of the Local Government Code and shares from CTCs collections in the ensuing years.

We reiterated that Management require all Accountable Officers to settle their respective cash advances at the end of the year to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts in the books of accounts of the Agency.  Henceforth, avoid granting subsequent cash advances to Accountable Officers with outstanding cash advances and withhold the salaries and/or any amount due to those Accountable Officers who were unable to comply with the pertinent rules and regulations on cash advances. Moreover, adhere strictly to the guidelines in the granting, utilization and settlement of cash advances as enunciated in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
We further recommended the immediate liquidation of the outstanding cash advances and submit the liquidation report or proof of refund/s, if there is any, to the Auditor for post-audit. Also, send Demand Letters to the concerned Accountable Officer(s), Agency Officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances for monitoring purposes.

We further recommended that Management ensure that any unreleased appropriations be reverted back to the National Treasury or if at any point before December 31, 2023, upon consultation with the Regional Task Force-ELCAC and the barangays concerned, determines that remaining funds can no longer be utilized.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to make an analysis and reconciliation of the Due to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth accounts and cause the immediate remittances of the determined unremitted contributions and loan amortization due to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth in compliance with the related laws to avoid unnecessary accumulation of interest and penalties.

We recommended that the LCE require the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to adjust the payment of the employer’s share to the Pag-IBIG contribution to only ₱100/month per covered employee in accordance with Rule VI, Section 1 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act 9679 otherwise known as the PAG-IBIG Fund Law.

We recommended that management require the contractors to have the materials tested to laboratories duly accredited by DOST/BRS and submit the results thereof to ensure the quality of the materials to be used for infrastructure projects, and the Municipal Engineer to ensure compliance thereof.

We recommended that the LCE provide justification why the mandatory requirement for a CPES is not observed.  Henceforth, we enjoin LCE to revisit the provisions as provided under Section 13 of the Annex “E” of the R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR on the evaluation of contractor’s performance.  Further, that concerned department representatives be convened to discuss on the subject matter to ascertain commitment/s in terms of agency resources towards conformance to the mandatory requirement for a CPES and its necessary impact to the operations and structure of the LGU.
We recommended that Management stop the practice of prematurely issuing certificate of acceptance and comply with the set timeline to maximize the one-year defects liability period.

We recommended the following courses of action be taken by the Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to strengthen the accounting and processing system being part of the accounting and fiscal control process and ensure that transactions processed, paid and recorded are properly approved and duly supported with complete documents and/or technical requirements and proper signatures; and
Utilize the checklist provided for under the Revised Documentary Requirements for Common Government Transactions prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and the Revised RIRR of RA No. 9184 as a useful guide in the processing of claims for payment.

We recommended that the Management:
Submit a justification as to the necessity of the attendance of the five (5) LGU employees in the aforementioned seminar/training; and
Confine the issuance of travel authority and grant of DTE and training expenses to employees whose travels will be essential to the effective performance his/her mandates or functions.

We commended Management for optimally utilizing its 20 percent Economic Development Fund, thus, attaining its commitment to its constituents to deliver the benefits that can be derived from the projects that were implemented.
Further, we recommended that Management continue to pursue improved practices in programming, prioritizing and implementing programs and projects to maximize the utilization of the Local Development Fund in attaining desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes for the benefit of the community.
Moreover, we recommended that Management expedite the implementation and completion of the six (6) on-going projects, during the ensuing CY 2024

We recommended to the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management (LDRRM) Officer to optimize the utilization of the LDRRMF of the LGU in order to strengthen its disaster preparedness and avoid potential damages during disasters.

We recommended that Management, through the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), expedite the full establishment of the GAD database in order to have a basis for planning and programming PPAs and formulating policies that are gender-responsive and to be able to undertake thorough gender analysis based on relevant data to properly identify existing gender issues and ensure that the approved PPAs to be incorporated in the GAD Plan and Budget are gender responsive.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and 2016-01 on gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD monitoring and preparation of GAD AR.
Henceforth, we recommended that the Management:
Revisit their programs/projects, give more attention and study on how to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds through the implementation of GAD related programs and projects in order to attain the objective for which the funds were provided;
Develop priority programs/projects that are in immediate needs to avoid various unimplemented program/projects and activities; and
Stop inclusion of program’s projects and activities that are not GAD related PPAs such as travelling and training expenses that are regular activity of the LGU.
We recommended that the Management strictly observe the approved budgets in the implementation of GAD PPAs to ensure the efficient utilization of the GAD fund.

We recommended that:
The VM and Members of the SB stop the practice of granting assistance to the constituent of the Municipality, charged against the respective funds as this is not among their mandated duties and functions as per laws and rules and regulations specified above; and
Management should adhere to the applicable laws, rules and regulations particularly Section 2 and 4 (1) of PD 1445.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the Due to BIR account and identify the taxes withheld which are due for remittance and make the immediate remittance of such in compliance with the related laws and regulations. Likewise, make adjusting entries to the Due to BIR account for all funds to reflect the correct balance of the account.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the complete set of financial statements on time for all funds to allow the timely audit thereof and the timely submission of the required report. We also recommended adequate internal controls on the maintenance and security of financial data.

We recommended that the Petty Cash Custodian ensure that the PCF balance is maintained at a level where replenishment could be made before the fund is fully used, such as the processing of replenishment as soon as the total disbursements thereof reaches 75 per cent.

We recommended Management to file the request for authority, to write-off the dormant unliquidated cash advances supported with the pertinent documents prescribed under the COA Circular No. 2016-005.
Henceforth, we recommended Management to require the Municipal Accountant to monitor and submit a complete report of cash advances and properly maintain adequate subsidiary records thereof.

We recommended Management to:
require Accountable Officers to refund immediately any excess/unutilized cash advance upon liquidation; and
require the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting additional cash advances to officers and employees without liquidating first their previous cash advances.

We recommended that the Management, through the Municipal Accounting Office, impose firm policies and require the officers and employees to liquidate within the reglementary period in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002. Otherwise, implement the aforementioned sanctions in PD No. 1445 and DBM MC No. 2023-1.
We also recommended the Municipal Accounting Office to:
ensure the verification, recording in the books and submission of the liquidation documents to the audit team within ten (10) days after receipt thereof from the officers/employees; and
ensure that the supporting documents for the liquidation of cash advances as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 date June 14, 2012 are intact and complete.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
GSO to (i) install and execute control procedures to monitor all inventories procured and received under their custody and (ii) ensure the preparation and timely submission of reports pertaining to the issuance of inventory items (RIS and weekly SSMI); and
Accounting Office to record the procurement of inventories as assets and consequently recognize these as expenses upon issuance to the end-users.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to require the Municipal Accounting Office to:
review thoroughly the long-outstanding payables and prepare appropriate adjusting entries for the reversion to surplus and/or to re-establish the valid claims; and
in case of trust fund, determine whether the project has been completed and coordinate with the source agencies for the appropriate disposition of the remaining funds.
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to review the accounting errors noted and prepare adjusting entries to correct the balance of the Accounts Payable account.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
inquire in writing from the Source Agencies, if known, the intended purpose of the fund transfers and whether the unutilized funds should be returned to the Source Agency; and
exert effort in tracing relevant information such as the date of receipt of funds, Source Agencies, the intended purpose of the funds, and the status of implementation of the PPAs funded by the transfers.

We recommended Management to require the concerned personnel to submit the government contracts, purchase orders and the supporting documents within five days after perfection pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to properly record the fund transfers to the recipient barangay for the implementation of various infrastructure projects and the liquidation thereof, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009.
We also recommended that Management require all fund transfers to Barangays for the implementation of projects and programs be covered by a Memorandum of Agreement and other documentary requirements to establish the proper accountability and monitoring of the proper utilization of funds.
We recommended that Management, through the Sangguniang Bayan, comply with the prescribed legislation as regards the realignment of funds to ensure the regularity of expenditures thereon.

We recommended that Management:
re-assess the DRRM plan and the period of implementation of programs, projects and activities in the LDRRMF Investment Plan to significantly improve the utilization of the fund;
in coordination with the MDRRM Officer, incorporate in the LDRRMFIP the projects, programs and activities to be sourced from the previous years’ unexpended balance and various grants/donations together with the current year’s allocation; and
require the MDRRMO and Municipal Accountant to closely coordinate in the monitoring and utilization of the LDRRMF.

We recommended that Management give more effort in the filling up of the other mandatory positions of the Municipality’s DRRM Office as required under NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC Circular 2014-01 dated April 4, 2014 and Rule 6 Section 2 of the IRR of RA No. 10121.

We recommended that Management/LSB ensure that the prescribed planning/ budgeting activities adhere to the required timeline in the preparation, review and approval of the proposed SEF Budget in compliance with the pertinent provisions of the DepEd/DBM/DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the preparation and submission of the required reports in its proper format (SEF Budget Accountability Form No. 1) and within the prescribed period (on or before February 15) to allow the Audit Team ample time in verifying the utilization of the Special Education Fund.

We recommended that Management revisit the GAD PPAs to consider prioritizing realizable programs adaptable to the current situation to optimally utilize allocated funds and commitments to gender equality and GAD goals.
We recommended that the Accounting Office reconcile and trace the negative balance in the Due to BIR account to correct said balance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
Municipal Accountant to conduct the necessary analysis and reconciliation to properly ascertain the correct valuation of the Due to Pag-IBIG account; and
Municipal Treasurer to remit the full amount withheld from the employee’s salaries together with the government’s share in the employees’ premiums.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the complete set of financial statements on time for all funds to allow the timely audit thereof.

We recommended that Management direct the Accounting Office to:
prepare and submit the Bank Reconciliation Statements for all the current accounts maintained by the Municipality in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 96-011;
conduct analysis of the dormant trust fund accounts to review the purpose of such funds and utilize it according to its intended purpose; and
cause the reversion thereof to the General Fund if warranted, in accordance with the requirements set by the LBP on dormant accounts.

We recommended that Management:
re-evaluate the placement of funds in a time deposit account and review the fund sources to establish its idleness; and
submit documents showing prior authority from the Sangguniang Bayan and approval of the Local Chief Executive authorizing the placement of the funds in the time deposit.
Otherwise, close the time deposit account and use the fund to prioritize implementation of projects for the general welfare of the constituents.
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to prepare subsidiary ledgers of the receivable and payable accounts of the LGU for proper monitoring of accounts.

We recommended Management to file the request for authority to write-off the dormant unliquidated cash advances supported with the pertinent documents prescribed under the COA Circular No. 2016-005. We also recommended the Municipal Accounting Office to properly maintain the accounts and records of the Municipality and monitor the cash advances.

We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Accounting Office, impose strict policies and require the officers and employees to liquidate within the reglementary period in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002. Otherwise, implement the aforementioned sanctions in PD No. 1445 and DBM MC No. 2023-1.
We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to stop the granting of additional cash advances to officers and employees unless the previous balance is liquidated to avoid the accumulation of outstanding cash advances.

We recommended that Management require the Accounting Office to apply generally accepted accounting practice in the recording of Inventories, that is credit the account upon issuance to end-users, transfers, write down or other disposal.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to record the cost of ongoing projects in the appropriate Construction in Progress accounts prescribed under the NGAS Manual for LGU and COA Circular No. 2015-09. Thereafter, reclassify the costs into proper PPE accounts upon completion.

We recommended that Management:
require the Municipal Accountant to review the accounting errors noted and prepare adjusting entries to correct the balance of the Accounts Payable account; and
ensure that the supporting documents of the transactions as well as the data input, specifically the dates, are complete in accordance with PD No. 1445, to substantiate the validity and propriety of balances reflected in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management:
submit the Municipality’s duly accomplished Property Inventory Form (PIF) to the Audit Team Leader and the GIF of GSIS on or before April 30 of each year to be used as basis for the assessment of the general insurance coverage; and
insure with the GIF of GSIS the Municipality’s insurable assets and properties for indemnification in case of damage or loss due to fire, earthquake, storm or other fortuitous events.

We recommended Management to require the Bids and Awards Committee to conduct pre-procurement conference for Public Bidding of Goods costing above ₱2,000,000.00, Infrastructure Projects costing above ₱5,000,000.00, and Consulting Services costing above ₱1,000,000.00 in accordance with Section 20 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that Management require the awarded contractors to post performance security that is valid until one year from project completion up to final acceptance to ensure that the projects are adequately covered.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
deduct the appropriate retention money from first progress billings for infrastructure projects in accordance with Section 6 of Annex E of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and
ensure that it shall only be released upon final acceptance (that is one year after completion) of the project or upon the request of the contractor to substitute the same with a valid financial instrument.

We recommended that Management require the personnel assigned in preparing the PR, PO and AIR to accomplish said forms completely and accurately not only to facilitate verification and monitoring but more significantly, to strengthen the internal control on procurement.

We recommended Management to require the supplier to post warranty security in the forms prescribed under Section 62.1 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and collect liquidated damages prior to the release or lapse of the performance security.

We recommended that Management submit a valid justification on the noted irregularities together with supporting documents to avoid disallowance in audit in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-003.
We also recommended that Management require the Bids and Awards Committee to strictly observe the prescribed procurement guidelines and procedures in the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended Management to require the Municipal Accounting Office to strictly comply with the prescribed documentary requirements on disbursements to ensure the validity thereof and avoid suspension/disallowance in audit.

We recommended that Management:
prioritize and consider filling up the remaining vacant department head positions; and
intensify/expedite the promotion/hiring of the remaining plantilla positions as well as provide equal opportunities to deserving individuals in the community, including the current job order and contract of service workers of the Municipality, in accordance with the existing guidelines, rules and regulations of the CSC.

We recommended that Management:
re-assess the DRRM plan and the period of implementation of programs, projects and activities in the LDRRMF Investment Plan to improve the utilization of the fund;
monitor the implementation of PPAs to facilitate its prompt completion within the planned timelines; and
submit a revised AIP or LDRRMFIP relative to the use of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF. Henceforth, incorporate in the LDRRMFIP the projects, programs and activities to be sourced from the previous years’ unexpended balance together with the current year’s allocation.

We recommended that management direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to undertake the actual cash transfer of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF in the General Fund to the Trust Liabilities – DRRMF account in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that Management/LSB ensure that the prescribed planning/ budgeting activities adhere to the required timeline in the preparation, review and approval of the proposed SEF Budget in compliance with the pertinent provisions of the DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended that Management/LSB:
observe the preparation and submission of the required reports in its proper format (SEF Budget Accountability Form No. 1) and within the prescribed period (on or before February 15) to give ample time in the verification of the allocation and utilization of the Special Education Fund; and
continue to maximize the utilization of its SEF in order to deliver its services to the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management direct the GAD Focal Person to:
submit the GAD PB and GAD AR to the PPDO of the Provincial Government for approval of the DILG; and
submit the GAD AR to the oversight bodies, including the complete accompanying reports such as: 1) brief summary of the reported program or project; (2) results of HGDG tests; and (3) actions taken by the LGU on the COA audit findings and recommendations, copy furnished the Office of the Auditor for verification.

We recommended that Management thru the GAD Focal Point System,  set up appropriate systems and mechanisms to ensure the generation, processing, review and updating of GAD Database to serve as basis in performance-based gender responsive planning.
We recommended that the Municipality include in the notes to financial statements, the description and nature of the non-moving balance for proper disclosure.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly observe the existing rules
and regulations on depositing the collections regularly and intact to safeguard
government funds.

We recommended that Management:
a. Expedite the recruitment and hiring of a full-time Property Custodian;
b. Require Property and Accounting Units to adhere to the procedures outlined in
COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, with emphasis on the derecognition
of non-existing/missing PPEs for which accountability could not be established despite
exhaustion, to establish the correct balance of the PPE and record the necessary
adjustments; and
c. Require the Inventory Committee to prepare the RPCPPE in accordance with
the prescribed format and signed by the members thereof.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
a. Cause the preparation of the Inventory and Inspection Report on Unserviceable
Property by the Property Officer/Unit to facilitate the disposal of unserviceable properties;
and
b. Direct the Disposal Committee to expedite the disposal of unserviceable
properties following the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 92-386 to prevent
further deterioration and to realize income therefrom if found to be saleable.

We recommended that Management properly accomplish the RPCPPE by
providing complete information of each motor vehicle to facilitate proper and timely
monitoring of insurance with the GSIS and to prevent the payment of unnecessary
penalties, surcharges, and damages in the event of vehicular accidents, thereby
safeguarding the Municipality's interests.

We recommended that Management:
a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to send another copy of the Report of
Disbursement and Liquidations to the source agencies and reconcile the balances in the
trust receipts against the books of accounts of the source agencies in order that the correct
amount of liquidation of the funds are properly recorded.
b. Refund the excess or unutilized amount of the fund transfer to DOH RO V
amounting to ₱10,770.54 and record its full liquidation in the books of accounts.
c. Make the adjusting entry to correct the journal entries made for the receipt the
funds from DOH RO V for the Salud Bikolnon Awards amounting to ₱93,600.00, as
follows:
Due to NGAs ₱93,600.00
Prior Period Adjustment ₱93,600.00

We recommended that Management direct the concerned offices to hasten the
implementation of the partially and unimplemented 20% Local Development Fund
programs and projects to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and
environmental management outcomes essential to the promotion of the general welfare of
the general public.

We recommended the Management to adhere strictly with the provision of Item
5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and submit regularly to the
COA Audit Team of the Municipality the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of
Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund on or before the 15th day after the end
of each month through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council and
the Municipal Development Council.

We recommended that Management maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF to
cover all the four thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management in order to
enhance the preparedness of the Municipality against disasters and avoid, lessen or limit
their adverse impacts to the community.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive (LCE)
institutionalize the PSCP by creating a working group that will perform the following
activities:
a. to conduct risk assessment;
b. to identify critical processes and functions;
c. to determine scenarios that may disrupt normal operation;
d. to conduct risk analysis and impact analysis;
e. to formulate the PSCP based on the identified risks on critical processes and
functions and its related steps to be followed to eliminate, if not mitigate, the
impact of the determined disruptions following the prescribed template and be
guided by the PSCP guidebook; and
f. to submit plan to the MDRRMO for review, to the LCE for approval, and to the
Sangguniang Bayan for the passage of a resolution to institutionalize the PSCP.
Further, we recommended that members of the working group be capacitated by
allowing them to attend seminars/trainings on the formulation of the PSCP.

We recommended that the focal point/person for the GAD Program of the agency
to adhere strictly with proper recognition of Gender-Related Issues in considering
programs ‘projects/and activities for the GAD Plan and Budget in the succeeding year.
We likewise reiterated our recommendation that the members of the GAD Focal
Point System attend seminars on HGDG to enhance compliance with the proper
preparation of the GAD PB/AR.
Lastly, we recommended that Management ensure that the GAD M & E System of
the LGU is in place and fully functional so as to assess the effectiveness and efficiency of
their gender mainstreaming efforts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop the practice of using RERs
as evidence of payment for disbursements amounting to more than ₱1,000.00. Henceforth,
strictly comply with the requirements of COA Circular 2004-006 dated September 9, 2004
and COA Circular 2021-001 dated June 24, 2021.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Solid Waste
Management Board to review and update the Solid Waste Management Plan every year
pursuant to Section 16 of RA 9003.

We recommended to Management the following courses of actions:
a. Settle the issue on ownership of the land where project site is located before
continuing the construction of the said project.
b. Henceforth, verify the ownership of land/lot of any project that will be
implemented before any award be made to avoid similar deficiency in the future.

We recommended that Management resolve all legal issues affecting the
implementation of the said project in order to avoid possible suspensions and/or
disallowances in audit.
Henceforth, Local Chief Executive seek the approval of the Municipal
Development Council when there is a change of location of a project prior its
implementation.
Likewise, the Local Chief Executive shall obtain an authorization from the
Sangguniang Bayan before entering into a contract in behalf of the Municipality,
pursuant to Section 22 (c) of RA 7160.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management require the Municipal
Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Human Resource and Management
Officer to discuss and make the necessary arrangement with the Agency's GSB for the use
of ATM in the payment of salaries, wages and other benefits of the Officials and
Employees of the LGU, so that government funds may be safeguarded against loss or
misuse and to ensure efficiency and economy in its Cash Management.

We recommended that Management:
a. Stop the practice of giving financial assistance or donation intended for local
and foreign travel of delegates whose participation or presence to the related activity is
not necessary and likewise, keep the participants being granted with such financial
assistance or donation to the barest minimum as provided under Sections 3 and 9 of EO
77 to avoid possible future audit disallowances;
b. Submit the plane tickets, boarding pass, Official Receipt for the payment of
hotel accommodation and Certificate of Participation or its equivalent of the nineteen (19)
participants/delegates to the 2023 HKIMO held last August 23 – 28, 2023; and
c. Submit the official receipt for the payment to GOPLACES TRAVEL AND
TOURS amounting to ₱133,400.00 for the registration fee of ten (10) participants of the
same activity.

We recommended that Management improve its performance in the submission of
financial reports and accounts by:
a. Requiring the concerned Accountable Officer to submit the accounts and
financial reports to the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to the timelines of the
abovementioned existing laws, rules and regulations;
b. Directing the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting
Office all the disbursements vouchers, payrolls, official receipts, including the
corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month or
pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of rules and regulations, as
applicable; and
c. Directing the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the timelines on the
recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.

Furthermore, we recommended that Management continue to improve its
performance in the submission of the Trial Balances and the Quarterly Financial
Statements.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue the timely
submission of the year-end financial statements, together with its supporting schedules,
to enable the timely verification thereof by the Auditor.

We recommended that Management prepare and submit the BRS together with the
bank statements and attachments within the prescribed period and to promptly prepare
the adjusting entries for the reconciling items to arrive at a correct balance for the Cash
in Bank account.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary correcting entries for the erroneous entries made in recording the 15% advance payment for mobilization by recognizing it in the Advances to Contractors account. The retention money for each progress payments shall also be recorded under Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable. Moreover, if after fifty percent (50%) completion, the work is satisfactorily done and on schedule, no additional retention shall be made; otherwise, the ten percent (10%) retention shall be imposed;
Direct the Municipal Accountant to henceforth close to appropriate PPE accounts the CIP balance of completed projects. Any unpaid amount due to the contractor shall be recognized as payable if it is still feasible under existing rules and regulations. Periodic depreciation from the date of completion shall likewise be provided to allow fair presentation of the affected accounts in the year-end financial statements; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the notes to financial statements under the Construction in Progress account the balance of the projects which were not completed and discontinued.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the concerned Implementing Offices (IOs) of the unimplemented projects to determine if the same will still be implemented. Otherwise, refund/return the unutilized funds to the respective SAs; 
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to conduct cash analysis of the trust accounts this CY 2023 to determine which of these trust receipts no longer have cash back-up and need to be adjusted; and 
c. To avail the one-time cleansing of dormant payables, if there is any, following the procedures under COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023.

We recommended that the BAC, prepare and submit the required PMR to the GPPB in the prescribed formats within 14 days after the end of each semester to ensure that the Municipality abides by the standards set forth by RA 9184 and its IRR per Section 12.2 of its Revised IRR and GPPB Circular 03-2015 dated March 20, 2015, and Government Procurement Policy Board Circular No. 02-2020 dated May 20, 2020.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to identify the specific sources of funds and establish whether these were essentially idle funds in accordance with Section 21 and 22 of COA Circular No. 92-382.

We recommended that Management implement additional security measures such as installing CCTV cameras within the vicinity of the Municipal Hall Building and prioritize the construction of a garage/motor pool to shelter its vehicles and heavy equipment and protect them against the elements of nature as well as to safeguard the same against damage due to vandalism and possible theft.

We recommended that Management submit the perfected contracts and purchase orders together with the supporting documents to the auditor after five days upon issuance/approval in consonance with COA Circular 2019-001 in order for the audit team to have a proper review of the contracts and inform management of any defects in the contract.

We recommended that Management, upon approval of the IAS plantilla by the Civil Service Commission, hire qualified personnel to man the Municipal Internal Audit Office following the guidelines set forth under DBM Local Budget Circular (LBC) No. 110 in the organizational structure and staffing of office to further improve and strengthen the internal control system of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Executive Chief require:
a. The end users or implementing units of the procuring entity to formulate their PPMP for different PPAs in accordance with Section 7.3.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184;
b. The BAC Secretariat to prepare the Consolidated APP and submit the same to the BAC for final recommendation of the appropriate procurement modality; and
c. Submit a copy of the Consolidated APP to the Audit Team for verification.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to indicate the date of all the transactions in the SL of the disbursing officer. Additionally, instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure complete supporting documents in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 before the issuance of cash advance to the disbursing officer.

We recommended that Management expedite the implementation of the unutilized balance of the LGSF-SBDP-NTF-ELCAC in the ensuing year amounting to ₱96,730,198.93 to address the existing issues and development gaps in their barangays.

We recommended that Management instruct the Property Officer Designate:
To prepare and submit the Property Inventory Form (PIF) to the GIF of the GSIS, and to the Auditor, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2018-002 and, in coordination with the Municipal Engineering Office, ensure that all the insurable properties and assets of the Municipality Government are included in the said listing;
To immediately secure insurance coverage for the Municipality’s insurable properties not yet insured from GIF; and
To always ensure that the insurance coverage of the insurable properties and assets of the Municipality Government are promptly renewed every year and that adequate insurance coverage are secured on the assets to provide the Municipality protection and indemnification for any damage caused the assets due to fire, earthquake, storm or other calamities.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant/ OIC-Human Resource Officer implement appropriate controls so that the salary deductions allowed in the GAA will not result on the net take home pay to fall below the required threshold of ₱5,000. Personnel with loan amortizations which cannot be allowed anymore to be deducted from their salary and benefits may opt to pay directly to their respective lenders.
We recommended that Management stop the practice of prematurely issuing certificate of acceptance and comply with the timeline set to maximize the one-year defects liability period.

We recommended that management require the contractors to have the materials tested to laboratories duly accredited by DOST/BRS and submit the results thereof to ensure the quality of the materials to be used for infrastructure projects, and the Municipal Engineer to ensure compliance thereof.

We recommended that the LCE provide justification why the mandatory requirement for a CPES is not observed.  
Henceforth, we enjoin LCE to revisit the provisions as provided under Section 13 of the Annex “E” of the R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR on the evaluation of contractor’s performance.  
Further, that concerned department representatives be convened to discuss on the subject matter to ascertain commitment/s in terms of agency resources towards conformance to the mandatory requirement for a CPES and its necessary impact to the operations and structure of the LGU.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) determine and recommend to the Local Chief Executive the appropriate mode of procurement or alternative method of procurement to be used based on the prevailing conditions as cited under the IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Municipality should strive to improve its efficiency on the utilization of the 20 percent EDF and to fully implement the priority PPAs, in order to attain the desired outcomes on the socio-economic development that will benefit the intended beneficiaries.
Further, we recommended that Management expedite the implementation/completion of the 20 on-going projects and the 23 unimplemented CY 2023 EDF projects for calendar year 2024.

We recommended that Management instruct the LDDRMO to incorporate in the LDRRMFIP the unspent prior years DRRM fund, and henceforth update the said plan as necessary so that transparency in the utilization of the said fund is achieved and to ensure that the expenditures charged to the said fund are included in the priority PPAs of the LGU for their disaster mitigation, prevention and preparedness activities/projects pursuant to the pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended to the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management (LDRRM) Officer to optimize the utilization of the LDRRMF of the LGU in order to strengthen its disaster preparedness and avoid potential damages during disasters.

We recommended that Management, through the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), expedite the full establishment of the GAD database in order to have a basis for planning and programming PPAs and formulating policies that are gender-responsive and to be able to undertake thorough gender analysis based on relevant data to properly identify existing gender issues and ensure that the approved PPAs to be incorporated in the GAD Plan and Budget are gender responsive.
We recommended that Management establish a Monitoring and Evaluation System to enable it to assess the efficiency and effectiveness of its gender mainstreaming efforts.
We recommended that the GAD Focal Person ensure that planned GAD PPAs are all timely implemented based on its approved GAD Plan and Budget; as well as improve the utilization rate of the GAD Fund.

We recommended that the Management:
Require the submission of liquidation report and complete supporting documents with pertinent details within the required period after completion of the travel prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001, otherwise, unsupported claims shall not be allowed. Also, we recommend that the Municipal Accountant verify and thoroughly review supporting documents of claims to ensure that only valid claims will be allowed; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to only allow for daily travelling expenses (DTE) in accordance with E.O. No. 77, to avoid overspending and incurrence of excessive and unnecessary expenses. Likewise, meal component of the DTE should not be claimed if the registration fee already includes for the full-board meals;
Restrict grant of DTE only to those days which are necessary in the conduct of business; and
Adhere to the guidelines of DBM NBC No. 563 dated April 22, 2016 for the allowable payment for registration fee for trainings/seminars organized by non-government organizations and private institutions.

We recommended the following courses of action be taken by the Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to strengthen the accounting and processing system being part of the accounting and fiscal control process and ensure that transactions processed, paid and recorded are properly approved and duly supported with complete documents and/or technical requirements and proper signatures; and
Utilize the checklist provided for under the Revised Documentary Requirements for Common Government Transactions prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and the Revised RIRR of RA No. 9184 as a useful guide in the processing of claims for payment.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require all of the end-users of the procured laptops and desktop the submission of the specific brands, models, justification on the use of the laptop and desktop computers and other documents to support the same for consolidation and submission to the Audit Team for further verification; and
b. Observe prudence in the incurrence of expenditures by giving due consideration of the actual needs and requirements and by always adhering with the economic measures in every procurement activity to be undertaken.

We recommended that Management require the concerned officials to reconcile the difference totaling ₱28,714,322.24 between the Treasurer’s and Accounting Offices recorded RPT and SET Receivables and henceforth make the necessary adjustments in their respective records.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant revert the total amount of stale checks outstanding to the Cash-in Bank and Accounts Payable accounts to ensure the proper presentation of the account balances.

We recommended that the Property and Accounting Units undertake the necessary reconciliation procedures as outlined in COA Circular No. 2020-006 to establish the correct balance of the PPE and record the necessary adjustments. Likewise, Management submit to the Audit Team the RPCPPE every January 31 of the succeeding year.

Further, that the Appraisal and Disposal Committee conduct the appraisal of the unserviceable properties to determine the modes of disposal and the appraised values of the properties to be disposed to facilitate its immediate disposal in accordance with Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 89-296.
Further, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant include the necessary disclosures on the total road network system of the LGU in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We commended Management for the substantial implementation of the projects under the 20% Development Fund and recommend that it continue to improve practices in programming, prioritizing and implementing programs and projects to maximize the utilization of the Fund in attaining desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes for the benefit of the community.

We commended Management for appropriately transferring the unexpended balance of the 5% LDRRMF for CY 2023 to the Special Trust Fund in compliance to Section 21 of RA 10121 and Items 5.1.10 and 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
We recommended the Management to adhere strictly with the provision of Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and submit regularly to the COA Audit Team of the Municipality the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council and the Municipal Development Council.
We commended the efforts exerted by the Local Chief Executive in coordination with the LDRRMO and other stakeholders in the improvement of utilization rate of 77.84 per cent compared to last year’s 12.88 per cent.
We also recommended that Management continue to improve and maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF to cover all the four thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management in order to enhance the preparedness of the Municipality against disasters and avoid, lessen or limit their adverse impacts to the community.

We recommended that the focal point/person for the GAD Program of the agency to adhere strictly with proper recognition of Gender-Related Issues in considering programs ‘projects/and activities for the GAD Plan and Budget in the succeeding year.
We likewise reiterated our recommendation that the members of the GAD Focal Point System attend seminars on HGDG to enhance compliance with the proper preparation of the GAD PB/AR.

We recommended that the Municipality and the Local School Board continue to improve their utilization of the SEF and to provide for equitable budget for all the priority programs, projects, and activities allowable as SEF Expenditures.

Management Comment:
Management is thankful for the commendation and commits to continue reviewing and updating the LGU’s Solid Waste Management Plan every year.

Management Comment:
Management appreciated the commendation of the Commission on Audit.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management strictly observe the submission of copies of contracts and supporting documents to the Audit Team within five days after perfection thereof pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.
We recommended that Management improve its performance in the submission of financial reports required in Annex S of the PRDP Financial Management Operations Guidelines within the prescribed periods.

We recommended that henceforth, Management ensure strict compliance with the provisions of PD 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001 and the PRDP FMOG and comply with the noted deficiencies in the identified transactions to ensure the propriety of the transactions.

We commended Management for its adequate planning of the activities related to project implementation. We recommend the Management continue to improve its implementation and proper monitoring of the I-BUILD sub-project to ensure that the target percentages of completion are met and to achieve timely delivery of optimal benefits to the project beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management require the concerned officials to reconcile the difference between the Treasurer’s and Accounting Offices recorded Receivables amounting ₱237,899.00, and henceforth make the necessary adjustments in their respective records.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the BRS within the prescribed timeframe in accordance with existing rules and regulations, together with the corresponding Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) for recording of the adjustments/reconciling items, for review and verification.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
require the concerned Accountable Officer to submit the accounts and financial reports to the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to the timelines of the abovementioned existing law, rules and regulations;
direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting Office all the disbursements vouchers, payrolls, official receipts, including the corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month or pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of rules and regulations, as applicable; and
direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the timelines on the recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.
We recommended that Management further improve its performance in the submission of the Trial Balances and the Financial Statements.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue the timely submission of the year-end financial statements, together with its supporting schedules, to enable the timely verification thereof by the Auditor.

Management Comment:
Management is committed to continued faithful compliance with tax laws and regulations, ensuring proper withholding and remittance of taxes to the Bureau of Internal Revenue.

Management Comment:
Management acknowledges compliance with RA No. 8291, ensuring timely deduction and remittance of GSIS premiums and loan amortizations.

Management Comment:
Management acknowledges compliance with RA No. 9679, ensuring timely deduction and remittance of HDMF contributions and loan amortizations.

Management Comment:
Management acknowledges compliance with RA No. 7875 as amended by RA Nos. 9241 and 10606, ensuring proper deduction and remittance of PhilHealth contributions within the required period.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to closely monitor the LGU’s expenditures to avoid cash insufficiency and require the Municipal Accountant to see to it that monies held in trust be used only for the intended purpose, pursuant to Section 305 (e) of RA 7160.

We strongly recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, to immediately transfer all the downloaded funds from different source agencies from the GF account to the TF account to reflect the correct balances of the liability accounts in the financial statements and to use the fund only for the fulfillment of the purpose for which it was established in accordance with Section 305 (c) of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160 or the Local Government Code of the Philippines
Henceforth, the Management require the Implementing Offices to coordinate with the Municipal Accounting Office and the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to ensure that all fund transfers are properly recorded in the books of the Agency in accordance with Section 4.2 of COA Circular 94-013.

We recommended that Management:
Fast track the implementation of the AICS program and submit copies of the liquidation reports to DSWD, as stipulated in the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA);
Limit the cash advance to the requirements for two months to prevent unnecessary idle cash on hand that is highly susceptible to theft and misappropriation, in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002; and
Liquidate the previous cash advance before granting another cash advance to minimize the balance of outstanding cash advances.

We recommended that Management immediately submit the financial transaction documents of the 171 issued checks to the Accounting Office for recording, who shall submit the same to the Audit Team for verification. If warranted, notice of suspension/disallowance may be issued for the non-submission of said documents.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit intact and in full amount all her collections or collections remitted to her by the collectors with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day in compliance with existing regulations.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management instruct the Property Officer and concerned accountable officers/end-user offices/departments to conduct the inventory of all supplies and materials in their custody at the end of the year and to prepare the corresponding report certified correct by the Inventory Committee.
We also recommended that the Property Officer maintain supplies stock cards and for the Accounting and Property Offices to reconcile their records and take up the necessary adjustments to ensure that the Inventory accounts are properly stated in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management instruct the Accounting Office to:
Immediately submit the lacking Aging Schedules to support the submitted Financial Statements; and
Carefully review the submitted financial statements taking into consideration the noted discrepancies, and submit a revised set of Financial Statements such that the figures stated therein tally with the corresponding items in the Notes to Financial Statements and the Aging Schedules.
Henceforth, we recommended for the Municipal Accountant to submit complete and accurate financial reports in order to facilitate review thereof.

We commended the Management for their continued efforts to improve their revenue forecasting system and collection effort that will ensure the realization of revenue targets, thus, funding for plans and program spelled out in the budget of the Municipality are properly provided for to ensure the delivery of public service.
Henceforth, the Management:
Require the Municipal Budget Office/LFC to render a systematic and realistic revenue forecasting taking into account prior years’ experiences and statistics as aid in setting targets; and
Require the Municipal Treasury and other Departments involved in generating revenues to meet its targets by conducting periodic review of collection performance by the concerned officials to ascertain whether collection targets are attained and to analyze the reasons for the shortfall in collections and find alternative solutions to minimize, reduce or avoid incurrence of shortfall in the future.

We commended Management for achieving a high collection efficiency on locally sourced revenues, having significant gains in the revenue generation during the year.
Henceforth, we recommended that management conduct periodic review of accomplishments in order to have assurance that all revenue sources are tapped to maximize its revenue generating powers as mandated by law.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that Management coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan on the update for the proposed adjustments on tax rates to fully tap the scope of the LGU's taxing power, pursuant to the provisions of the Local Government Code, in order to increase tax revenues and receipts through updating of the rates in the existing tax code, which will augment the budget provision for a better delivery of government services.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management propose to update during the ensuing year the rates imposed on water consumption operated by the Municipal Waterworks System in accordance with Section 8 of Ordinance No. 06-2008 to meet the current operational demands thereof.

We recommended that Management, through the MDRRMO ensure the efficient and effective utilization of the DRRMF by implementing programs and projects designed to avoid, lessen or limit the potential adverse impact of disasters, such as projects pertaining to disaster prevention, mitigation and preparedness.
We further recommended that Management require the Municipal Budget Office to conduct reconciliation of the SAAOB with the Report on Utilization of DRRMF and ensure that the actual amounts of appropriations and obligations are properly reflected therein.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned offices to hasten the implementation of the partially and unimplemented 20% LDF PPAs appropriated in CY 2023 to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes essential to the promotion of the general welfare of its constituents.

We reiterated our prior year recommendation that the Local School Board strictly adhere to the laws, rules and regulations on the formulation, preparation and approval of a SEF annual budget to provide funds for the supplementary annual budgetary needs for the operation and maintenance of public schools within the Municipality and submit the same to the Audit Team for verification and examination.
We also recommended the Management to Coordinate with the concerned DepEd District Supervisor/Superintendent of the Municipality for proper utilization of SEF.
We recommended the LCE to instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to immediately submit the RAAO/ALOBS/SAAOB of SEF for CY 2023 to the Audit Team for verification.

We strongly recommended that the Management comply with the disbursement procedures of the Special Education Fund in accordance with the Chapter 5, Sections 90 and 91 of New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGU) Volume 1. Non-compliance with the guidelines set forth for the disbursement of SEF is tantamount to illegal disbursement and will warrant the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

We reiterated our prior year recommendation that the LSB strictly observe the timely preparation and submission of the required reports to the concerned oversight bodies and posting of the same in the Municipality’s website and/or in at least three (3) conspicuous public places to promote transparency and accountability.
Further we recommended the Municipal Accountant to regularly and timely prepare quarterly utilization reports using SEF Budget Accountability Form No.1, and furnish copy to the LSB for posting to in the website of the LGU and/or in at least three (3) conspicuous public places and for submission to the oversight agencies.

We recommended the LSB to transfer the ₱345,261.34 under savings account to the current account of SEF and use the funds in accordance with the priority programs provided under Section 100 of RA 7160.
No specific recommendation in this introductory part other than commendation.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and 2016-01 on gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD monitoring and preparation of GAD AR.
Henceforth, we recommended that the Management:
Revisit their programs/projects, give more attention and study on how to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds through the implementation of GAD related programs and projects in order to attain the objective for which the funds were provided;
Develop priority programs/projects that are in immediate needs to avoid various unimplemented program/projects and activities; and
Stop inclusion of program’s projects and activities that are not GAD related PPAs such as travelling and training expenses that are regular activity of the LGU.
Lastly, we recommended that the lacking supporting documents for the payment under Check No. 3199932 and 3199932 in the amount of ₱13,500.00 and ₱10,000.00 both dated December 21, 2023 be submitted to the Audit Team, otherwise, a Notice of Suspension shall be issued.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the GAD AR and the required accompanying reports such as: 1) brief summary of the reported program or project; (2) complete results of HGDG tests; and (3) actions taken by the LGU on the COA audit findings and recommendations be submitted to the province for review and consolidation and the DILG Regional Office for the preparation of the annual GAD accomplishments and furnish a copy to the Auditor’s Office for verification.

We reiterated our prior year recommendation that the management maximize the utilization of GAD Funds on its identified programs and activities that contribute to poverty alleviation, economic empowerment especially of marginalized women, protection, promotion, and fulfillment of women’s human’s rights, and practice of gender-responsive governance.
We also recommended the management submit to the concerned DILG Office the changes in the DILG endorsed GPB for their information and evaluation and thereafter, submit a copy to the Audit Team for verification and audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office, Municipal Budget Office and ENRO reconcile their records pertaining to the actual cost/expenses incurred in the implementation of each PPAs of GAD for an effective monitoring and performance evaluation.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Budget and Accounting Offices maintain the RAAO and subsidiary ledger, respectively, for all GAD transactions. Henceforth, indicate in the RAAO/SL the approved annual budget/appropriation, record all transactions and present the balances by using the three-money columns for debit, credit and balances to ensure complete, consistent and comparable data for easier analysis and extraction of useful information by Management and other users.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management:
Continue gathering data to support the quantified existing beneficiaries and to be able to undertake thorough gender analysis based on the relevant data for proper identification of existing gender issues and ensure that approved PPAs to be incorporated in the GPB are timely gender responsive; and
Endure that the date of gathering data be indicated in the report to assure its effectivity and efficiency, and location/barangay of clients for verification purposes.

We recommended that Management direct the formulation/identification of specific PPAs that shall be incorporated in the annual budget based on the approved 10-Year ESWMP.
We also recommended the regular and timely preparation of accomplishment report for the efficient monitoring of program implementation of the PPAs for SWM.
Further, we recommended that the Budget and Accounting Office stop the practice of charging expenses appropriated to other program without an SB Resolution to authorize the same, to avoid issuance of disallowance.
Further, we reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management strictly monitor areas where indiscriminate dumping of waste is rampant and proper waste segregation is not practiced and enforce all regulations, including imposition of penalties for non-compliance by the residents.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain a subsidiary ledger for the SWM using the three-columns for debit, credit and balances, indicating therein the approved budget, supplemental/ realignment, if any, and record all transactions and balances.
Further, we recommended that the Budget and the Accounting Offices regularly reconcile their records on SWM transactions.
Lastly, we recommended the MENRO to coordinate with Budget and Accounting Office in the preparation of their accomplishment report pertaining to the SWM.

We recommended that Management:
Assign two different personnel as head of the Accounting and the Budget Office to prevent the internal control system of the LGU from being compromised and to strengthen the check and balance mechanisms of these two departments; and
Strengthen the control policies and procedures on the granting of cash advances by designating additional disbursing officer/s to diversify risk of managing huge amount of cash on hand.

We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant/Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that all cash advances are properly obligated and legally supported with budgetary provisions before these are processed/granted.
Henceforth, all cash advances shall be supported with estimates of expenditures, such as payroll, project proposals for special activities and program of works for infrastructure projects and repairs. Also, indicate the OBR Numbers in the Disbursement Vouchers of the cash advances which would serve as reference in the recording of obligations in the RAAO.

We recommended that Management require all concerned Departments/Offices of the Municipal Government to submit to the Supervising Auditor and the Audit Team Leader at the beginning of each year the report on all its on-going PPAs and those to be implemented during the current year.  Quarterly reports on the PPAs shall likewise be furnished the Auditor on or before the 10th day after the end of each quarter.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that the hiring and payment of job order personnel are supported with job order contracts, Daily Time Record (DTR) and Accomplishment Reports and submit these as attachment to their payroll in accordance to Section 1.2.1 of COA Circular 2012-001.
Require the HRMO to formulate a Human Resource Plan and issue a uniform policy guideline on the hiring of JO personnel to appropriately determine the necessity of the hiring thereof, as well as to strengthen the internal controls pertaining thereto; and
Require the HRMO to adopt a schedule of standard rates for JOs per position that is based on qualification and the nature of work/assignment taking into consideration the prevailing minimum wage in the locality, in order not to create inequality in the workforce of the LGU.
Henceforth, we recommended that Management categorically stipulate in the Job Order Contract or Contract of Services the fund charging and the specific nature of work assignments and/or specific duties and responsibilities and quantity of work to be accomplished and the desired work outputs to facilitate comparison and evaluation of actual work accomplishments of hired job orders.

We recommended that Management:
Require the concerned officials and employees to immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to check the completeness of the supporting documents before processing the claims for travel expenses.

We recommended that Management
Ensure that claims for reimbursement of actual accommodation expenses in excess of the accommodation component of the DTE must be supported with a certification by the agency head or authorized representative as absolutely necessary in the performance of an assignment (Sample shown in Annex Q); and
Ensure strict compliance on the laws, rules and regulations on the claims for traveling expenses to avoid possible misuse of government funds.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of the employees of hiring van services during their official travel unless properly justified as necessary and supported with the certification by the Agency Head, in accordance to Section 7 of E.O 77.

We recommended that the Management instruct the concerned employees to refund the excess Daily Travel Expenses (DTE) for hotel/lodging for their travels with separate claims for actual hotel accommodations, else, a Notice of Disallowance shall be issued.
We also strongly recommended that the Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to only allow DTE in accordance to EO 77 to avoid overspending and incurrence of excessive and unnecessary expenses.

We recommended that the Management instruct the concerned employees to refund the claims for travel expenses which were not authorized and deemed irregular, else, a Notice of Disallowance shall be issued.
We also strongly recommended that the Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to only allow claims for travel expenses which are authorized by the proper approving authority in order to avoid incurrence of irregular expenses.

We recommended that Management instruct the accountable officers to immediately submit the required supporting attachments to completely liquidate their outstanding cash advances. Non-submission of which would warrant the issuance of a Notice of Suspension.
Also, we recommended that Management desist from including personnel in seminars and conferences whose attendance thereof is not essential to the effective performance of their mandates or functions.
