Henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of EO No. 77 regarding the criteria for authorized official travel.

We recommended that Management direct the BAC and the BAC-TWG:
To include in their post-qualification a comparative evaluation showing the number of projects with the same equipment and key personnel pledged and assigned by the same lowest calculated bidder for different projects with simultaneous or overlapping implementation period; and
To consider formulating a policy on the sufficiency and availability of equipment pledged by the contractors with regard to projects with simultaneous or overlapping implementation period awarded to the same contractors to avoid delay in project implementation and setting the limit for the number of contracts handled by key personnel, to still reasonably perform its duties and functions, without affecting the quality and timeliness of the project implementation.

We recommended that Management instruct the BAC and the TWG to properly check and verify the veracity and truthfulness of the documents submitted by the bidders during the bidding and for post-qualification to accurately determine the ultimate Lowest Calculated Responsive Bid.
Also, for the BAC to strictly enforce the penalties provided under the Rules XXI and XXIII of this IRR in order that bidders will strictly comply with the requirements of the bidding.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that Management provide the Auditor copies of the awarded contracts for infrastructure projects and its supporting documents within five working days from execution thereof to allow for a systematic, timely and effective review process.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to conduct the necessary analysis of the accounts and periodic reconciliation of their records in order to establish the balance of the Inter-agency accounts and remit the BIR taxes, GSIS, Pab-Ibig premiums due; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the full and proper deduction and remittance of GSIS premium contributions of employees in compliance with Section 6 of R.A. No. 8291, in order to avoid further accumulation of interests, and the filing of appropriate legal action which may implicate the responsible officers of the LGU.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that the Local Chief Executive enforce the COE to settle the ND in the amount of ₱365,720.13 as of December 31, 2023 by requiring the withholding of salaries or other compensation due the persons liable in satisfaction of the said disallowance and exhaust all available remedies provided under existing laws, rules and regulations to enforce settlement of the audit disallowance.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant restore the cash equivalent of all unreleased checks and recognize the related liability/payable account to ensure the fairness of presentation of Cash-in-Bank accounts in the financial statements starting 2024 and thereafter.

We recommended that Management re-evaluate the placement of funds in the savings/time deposit accounts to prioritize the implementation of projects for the general welfare of the constituents. Otherwise, review the fund sources to establish its idleness and secure prior authority from the Sangguniang Bayan and approval of the Local Chief Executive should the LGU place the funds on time deposit accounts.

We recommended the Municipal Treasurer to include all Accountable forms in her custody in the RAAF (Official Receipts, checks, cash tickets, etc.) and submit the Monthly RAAFs for check not later than the fifth day of the ensuing month in accordance with the NGAS.

We recommended the Municipal Treasurer and Collecting Officers to properly verify their collections and actual accountable forms as reported in the RCD.
We also recommended the Municipal Treasurer to require the immediate remittance of the lacking forms from the collectors, deposit the same to the bank, and furnish the audit team copies of RCD and deposit slips. Finally, we recommend to add controls in the Treasury Information System to avoid duplicate usage of ORs and the complete reporting of the accountable forms in the custody of the Collecting Officers.
We recommended the Special Disbursing Officer to expedite the completion of documentary requirements to liquidate his cash advances.

We recommended and Management agreed that through the Municipal Accounting Office, they will impose strict policies to require the officers and employees to liquidate within the reglementary period in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002. Otherwise, implement the aforementioned sanctions in PD 1445 and DBM MC No. 2023-1.
We also recommended the concerned officers and employees to liquidate the outstanding cash advances as of March 31, 2024 amounting to ₱59,753.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
prepare the subsidiary ledgers for the receivables and payables of the LGU; and
cause the reconciliation of the subsidiary ledger balances of the accounts to tally with the balances appearing in the financial statements as of year-end.

We recommended that the Management secure an independent appraisal report from proper authorities, together with other documentary requirements, to ensure the reasonableness and fairness of the negotiated price and submit the same to the Audit Team for review.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the conduct of materials testing duly accredited by DOST/BRS laboratories to ensure the quality of the materials to be used for infrastructure projects and submit the results thereof as part of the supporting documents on the payments to contractors in 2024 and onwards.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Engineering Office to collect the liquidated damages from the securities or warranties posted by the contractor, and strictly monitor the implementation of projects and properly report the status thereof.
We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the completeness of the supporting documents prior the processing of disbursements pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001 and impose the liquidated damages on the delayed implementation of infrastructure projects.

We recommended and Management agreed, through the Bids and Awards Committee, to ensure that the performance security posted by the winning bidders are valid until final acceptance or one year after completion. Henceforth, require the contractor to post extension of the validity of performance security to cover the approved contract time extension.

We recommend that Local Chief Executive require the:
Municipal Accountant to properly monitor the utilization of fund transfers and the implementation of the projects funded by the same by preparing a schedule of utilization and liquidation; and
concerned offices to secure and provide copies of MOAs of the projects/ programs/ activities funded by fund transfers to ensure proper utilization of the funds and compliance with the terms and conditions of the MOA.

In view thereof, we recommended and Management agreed that:
the VM and Members of the SB stop the practice of granting assistance to the constituent of the Municipality, charged against the respective funds as this is not among their mandated duties and functions as per laws and rules and regulations specified above;
funds for financial assistance and donations should be transferred to the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office (MSWDO) subject to the usual procedures and documentary requirements; and
Management should adhere to the applicable laws, rules and regulations particularly Section 2 and 4 (1) of PD 1445.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
re-assess the DRRM plan and the period of implementation of programs, projects and activities in the LDRRMF Investment Plan to significantly improve the utilization of the fund;
monitor the implementation of PPAs to facilitate its prompt completion within the planned timelines; and
in coordination with the LDRRM Officer, incorporate in the LDRRMFIP the projects, programs and activities to be sourced from the previous years’ unexpended balance and various grants/donation.

We recommended that Management/LSB ensure that the prescribed planning/ budgeting activities adhere to the required timeline in the preparation, review and approval of the proposed SEF Budget in compliance with the pertinent provisions of the DepEd/DBM/DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive:
require the concerned personnel to strictly observe the timeline for submission of Financial Reports and Financial Statements in accordance with Item 3.4.1 of PRDP’s Financial Management Manual; and
instruct the Municipal Project Management and Implementation Unit to ensure accuracy of the progress report to avoid overpayment to the contractor.

We recommended that management direct the Municipal Accountant to fully disclose the required financial information in the Notes to Financial Statements to achieve fair presentation of the Financial Statements, as well as assist its users in analyzing and understanding the financial statements pursuant to the pertinent provisions of IPSAS

We recommended that Management revisit the GAD PPAs to consider prioritizing realizable programs adaptable to the current situation to optimally utilize allocated funds and commitments to gender equality and GAD goals.

We recommended that Management thru the GAD Focal Point System,  set up appropriate systems and mechanisms to ensure the generation, processing, review and updating of GAD Database to serve as basis in performance-based gender responsive planning.
We recommended that the Municipality immediately remit the balance of ₱40,989.78 pertaining to 2023 withheld taxes to avoid penalties.

We recommended that the Municipality immediately remit the balance of ₱337,523.92 pertaining to 2023 to avoid interests and to have an updated remittance posting to facilitate transactions of employees with the GSIS.
We recommended that the Municipality immediately remit the balance of ₱122,622.07 pertaining to 2023 in accordance with PHIC law.
We recommended that the Municipality immediately remit the balance of ₱264,267.42 pertaining to 2023 in accordance with the Pag-IBIG law.

1.18 We recommended that the City Accountant and the Inventory Committee
complete the reports necessary to establish the correct balances of PPE items
that should be reported in the financial statements.
1.19 We also recommended that the City Mayor file a request for authority to
derecognize non-existing/missing PPEs to the audit team and support its
request with the certified lists required in Section 8.3 of COA Circular No.
2020-006, to take full advantage of the one-time cleansing privilege afforded
by the Commission and establish accuracy of its PPE balances reported in
the financial statements.
1.20 Lastly, we recommended that the Inventory Committee adopt the uniform
numbering system for property numbers of PPE and require the Property
Unit to update the property stickers based on the prescribed format and to
include the required information under Sections 5.6 and 5.7 of the said COA
Circular.
1.21 Management assured the Audit Team that they will be verifying and reconciling
the discrepancies noted between the RPCPPE and the amounts reported in the
financial statements. Rest assured that the City Mayor would be requesting
authority to derecognize those confirmed as missing and to dispose those which
are already unserviceable.
1.22 Management also added that they cannot immediately implement the uniform
property identification in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006, because
they are using a different numbering system in monitoring its properties.
However, they informed the Audit Team that they will slowly be adopting the
required property identification; they just need more implementation time
because their eNGAS already follows their current numbering system.

2.8 We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to direct the Accounting
Clerk assigned in recording the expenditures to, henceforth, review those
which pertain to the prior period, and journalize these items as adjustments to
equity and corresponding real or accrued accounts, rather than to current
period expenditures or nominal accounts, to reflect the actual financial
performance of the City Government of Bayawan, in conformity with Section
119 of PD 1445 and IPSAS 1.
2.9 The City Accountant also confirmed to the Audit Team that no necessary
adjustments would be made in the CY 2024 financial statements, because the
particular accounts only affected the CYs 2022 and 2023 financial statements and
would counterbalance in CY 2024.

3.10 We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to make the adjustments
to remove the said account from the books of the City Government. We also
recommended, and City Treasurer also agreed, to establish a monitoring
system of all its bank accounts to prevent the same issue going forward.

4.7 We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to revise the Notes to the
Financial Statements to include the principal amount, interest rate, maturity
date, purpose of the loan, and interest expense of the Loans Payable –
Domestic account, to properly inform the users of the financial statements the
financial performance of the City Government, particularly in financial
management.

5.9 We recommended and the City Treasurer agreed to ensure that the cash
accountabilities of AOs are in accordance with their existing bonds, pursuant
to Treasury Circular No. 02-2019. We also recommended that Management
review and evaluate the maximum accountabilities of AOs and apply for an
increase in the amount of bond, when necessary, in accordance with RA No.
7160 and Treasury Circular No. 02-2019.
5.10 The City Treasurer confirmed that they have already evaluated the bonds of their
AOs and drafted a proposal for the next bond application.

6.8 We recommended and Management agreed to stop the practice of assigning
the Cashier to act as a treasurer for other associations or as a collector of funds
for purposes other than his role as a cashier in the City Government. We
further recommended and Management also agreed that the Cashier refrain
from keeping any other collections in the vault, except for the funds intended
for the government.

7.8 We recommended and the City Treasurer agreed to, henceforth:
7.8.1 Require the outgoing AO to submit their cashbook to the City Treasurer
and City Accountant for verification and recording purposes.
7.8.2 Instruct the newly appointed or designated AO to use a new cashbook
in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002.
7.8.3 Brief the newly appointed or designated AOs on the proper recording
of transactions and other matters relating to their role as a disbursing
officer.

8.12 We recommended and the City Mayor agreed, together with the Sanggunian,
to evaluate the legality and validity of Ordinance No. 20, series of 2002,
regarding the appropriation and grant of financial assistance to its
employees, regardless of their employment status, vis-à-vis Section 12 of RA
No. 6758.
8.13 We further recommended and Management agreed to desist from granting
financial assistance using the Ordinance as a basis and appropriately assign
the eligibility evaluation and case study report preparation of the grant of
financial assistance to the City Social Welfare and Development (CSWD),
pursuant to its policies and guidelines on the grant of financial assistance in
conformity with DSWD MC No. 11, s. 2019, as amended.
8.14 Lastly, we recommended, and the City Treasurer agreed to ensure that all
financial assistance disbursements are supported by the appropriate and
complete requirements, pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD 1445.
8.15 The City Mayor emphasized that the Ordinance was made in a time when the
AICS was not sufficient to cover medical or burial expenses, to which the Audit
Team agreed. Management also explained that the intent of the appropriation was
to address the urgent assistance needed by an employee, especially if accidents
happen during the performance of his/her job. However, the City Mayor and
Management assured the Audit Team that the recommendations would be
implemented.

9.9 We recommended and Management agreed to desist from conducting training,
meetings, seminars, workshops, or similar activities outside the geographical
island and henceforth observe the principle of prudent use of scarce financial
resources of the government as prescribed in DILG MC No. 2011-59 and COA
Circular No. 2012-003.
9.10 Management explained that the conduct of the planning workshop of the ELA at
Tagaytay City also included a benchmarking activity of Tagaytay’s Skateboarding
Park, thus influencing Management’s choice of the venue. However, Management
agreed and understood that prudence shall be observed in the disbursement of
government funds, especially with regard to the conduct of workshops and other
similar activities. Management assured the Audit Team that, henceforth, they will
be prudent in the conduct of similar activities.

10.9 We recommended and Management agreed that henceforth, the Bids and
Awards Committee first secure the appropriate authority to purchase motor
vehicles, in compliance with DBM BC No. 2022-1, and DILG MC No. 2022-
105.
10.10 Management accepts the recommendation. The BAC Chair, however,
emphasized that other vehicle purchases were secured the appropriate
authority. The BAC Chair assured the Audit Team that before processing the
procurement of all vehicles, the appropriate APMV shall be required from the
requestor or end-user.

11.9 We recommended and Management agreed to secure the applicable NTC
permits to purchase, possess, and operate the radio communication
equipment, in compliance with RA No. 584 and NTC MC No. 2-05-88.

12.8 We recommended and Management agreed to be guided by the pertinent
provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2010-101 dated September
23, 2010, and COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013, and desist
from using standees of the City Mayor and henceforth ban the use of names
or initials and/or images or pictures of government officials of government
programs, projects, and properties of the City Government to prevent any
perception of impropriety.
12.9 We further recommended and Management also agreed to limit affixing
government vehicles to the official seal or name of the LGU and the words
“FOR OFFICIAL USE ONLY” pursuant to DILG MC No. 2010-101.
12.10 Management explained that the stickers indicating the known alias of the head of
the agency on the patient transport vehicles were freebies from the supplier of
the said vehicles. However, the City Mayor assured the Audit Team that these
stickers would immediately be removed to avoid unnecessary issues and comply
with existing regulations. He also confirmed that the recommendations of the
Audit Team would be implemented immediately.

13.14 We recommended and the IAS Head agreed to direct his teams to continue
and complete its conduct of the Baseline Assessment of Internal Control
System, in accordance with the 2020 Revised PGIAM.
13.15 We also recommended and the IAS Head also agreed to fully adopt the
IASPPS and ICSPPS in the performance of their functions, in accordance
with COA Circular No. 2018-003 dated November 21, 2018.

14.10 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to avoid assigning
the Head of the IAS responsibilities for non-audit operational activities
which may impair the independence and objectivity of his internal audit
functions.
14.11 Further, we recommended and the Head of the IAS also agreed that, before
accepting any roles and responsibilities outside of internal auditing, assess
first its impact on his independence or objectivity.
14.12 Lastly, we recommended and the Head of the IAS agreed to promulgate an
Internal Audit Charter, in accordance with IASPPS, to establish rules and
restrictions regarding internal auditors assuming non-audit operational
activities.
14.13 The Head of IAS added that his office already prepared an Internal Audit
Charter which is ready for presentation to the Local Chief Executive, for
endorsement to the Sanggunian.

15.16 We recommended and the City Assessor agreed to:
15.16.1 Fast-track the preparation of the updated SMVs of the City,
seeking technical assistance from the Provincial Assessor and/or
the DOF-Bureau of Local Government Finance, pursuant to
Section 212 of RA No. 7160; and
15.16.2 Initiate the immediate conduct of a general revision of real
property assessments and thereafter, ensure that this is done every
three years as required under Section 219 of RA No. 7160 and
DILG-DOF JMC No. 2010-001.
15.17 We also recommended and Management agreed that the Sanggunian
enact an Ordinance, incorporating the Assessor’s proposed SMVs,
assessment, level, and tax rate, as a result of the GRRPA.
15.18 The City Assessor emphasized that his Office has been regularly conducting
tax mapping to maximize the demandable collection of the Office of the
Treasurer. However, Bayawan has properties which the City Government
cannot impose property taxes, because these properties have no titles, and the
City does not have legal basis to collect. He assured the Audit Team that his
Office, along with the Office of the Treasurer, is doing its best to maximize the
City Government’s revenue-raising power even in the absence of the required
general revision of real property assessments.

16.9 We recommended and the City Treasurer agreed to boost tax collection by
proactively including barangays in its campaign program to enhance the
collection of RPT/SET Receivables and enforce remedies through the
issuance of a warrant on or before, or simultaneously with, the institution of
the civil action for the collection of delinquent taxes.
16.10 The City Treasurer commented that their Office would also be willing to enter
into a compromise with taxpayers to increase collections, when necessary.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Inventory Committee prioritize the full implementation of one-time cleansing of PPE accounts pursuant to the provisions set forth in COA Circular                  No. 2020-006 to afford management with PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year after due consideration of payments for delinquent taxes at the beginning of the year in accordance with Section 20 of the Manual on NGAS, Volume 1.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer ensure the daily and intact remittance of collections by the Collecting Officers to the Liquidating Officer pursuant to Section 29 of the Manual on NGAS, Volume I by making daily monitoring of collections and remittances in order to prevent misappropriation or loss of public funds and facilitate smooth accounting and recording of collections.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant issue demand letters to all concerned officers and employees, requiring immediate settlement of their outstanding cash advances. Otherwise, we recommend the withholding or suspension of salaries of officials and employees for non-compliance therewith pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 89 of P.D. 1445.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant issue demand letters to concerned Punong Barangays and Barangay Treasurers on the immediate submission of liquidation reports on the financial assistance given by the municipal government.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Engineer properly evaluate the reason and supporting documents for any delays incurred during the project implementation and issue time extension orders only for those grounds allowed per Items 11.2 to 11.5 of Annex E of the R-IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive submit a written explanation on the delayed drawing of cash advance.  It is also recommended that management be mindful on the timing of the granting and liquidation of cash advances for confidential fund in order to ensure effective and efficient attainment of the purpose of the cash advance.

We recommended and Management agreed that submission of copies of the perfected government contracts, purchase orders and all supporting documents be made within five (5) working days from execution/issuance in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001 to facilitate timely auditorial and technical review of contracts and purchase orders and to avoid imposition of administrative disciplinary action prescribed by laws.

1.8. We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Accountant
prepare the necessary adjusting entries to record the actual RPT and SET
Receivables using the amounts per Tax Delinquency Listing certified correct
by the Municipal Treasurer as of December 31, 2023. And, set-up at the
beginning of the succeeding year the amount based on the Assessment Rolls
provided by the Municipal Assessor, in the absence of a complete RPTAR.

1.9. Management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer furnish the Municipal
Accountant at the beginning of each year a duly certified list showing the
name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the preparation of
the necessary accounting entries.

1.10. Management further agreed that the Municipal Assessor, Municipal
Treasurer and Municipal Accountant exert concrete efforts to reconcile the
records on real property taxes in order to establish the actual RPT/SET
receivables accruing to the municipality.

2.8. We recommended that management comply with the guidelines and
procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020
for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances and require the
Inventory Committee to:
a. Prepare and submit the Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) containing, at the
least, the specific assignments/duties of the Committee members, the cut-
off date and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory
taking activities from the start up to the targeted completion of the
physical inventory for approval to the Head of the Agency to ensure that
the conduct of inventory is properly and adequately planned;
b. Submit a Copy of the approved PIP to the COA Audit Team at least 10
calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory taking activities;
and
c. Complete the physical count of the property, plant and equipment and
submit copies of the reports to the Municipal Accountant for the proper
recording of adjustments and to the Auditor for verification.

2.9. We also recommended that General Services Office update the property
records based on the results of the physical inventory and reconciled with
accounting records to come up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable
as to its existence, condition and accountability.

3.15. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the following
adjusting journal entry to correct the erroneous recording of transactions:
General Fund:
Dr CIP – Infrastructure Assets ₱2,548,537.06
Cr Other Structures ₱2,548,537.06
To adjust and recognize the construction in progress at 68.88% of 65 units
130w Solar Lights
Trust Fund:
Dr CIP – Infrastructure Assets ₱749,250.00
Cr Advances to Contractors ₱749,250.00
To adjust and recognize the recoupment of the advance payment in reference
with DV No. 100-23-08-2552 (GF)

3.16. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all
financial transactions are recorded using the appropriate accounts, and
review the report of the bookkeepers in order to detect and minimize
erroneous entries.

4.11. We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Budget
Officer and the Municipal Accountant revisit the guidelines in IPSAS 24 in
the preparation of the Statement of Comparison Between Budget and Actual
Amounts (SCBAA), and effect the necessary revision in order to present
accurately the changes in the budget information of the municipality.

5.5. We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Accountant
provide a detailed disclosure on the breakdown of the available, utilized and
balances of the LDRRMF in the Notes to the Financial Statements as
required in Item 5.1.16 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

6.10. We recommended that management discontinue the delegation of unrelated
duties and responsibilities to the Internal Auditor-designate and prioritize
the discharge of the intended function of the Internal Audit Service/Unit
(IAS/U).

6.11. We also recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer
(HRMO), per discussion with the concerned employee, formulate an
Individual Development Plan (IDP) to document the needed steps that will
be undertaken to address performance gaps and provide opportunities for
enhancing competencies. Thereafter, the Internal Auditor-designate attend
capacity-building trainings on Internal Auditing Standards for the
Philippine Public Sector (IASPPS) and on Internal Audit Manual (IAM) for
LGUs.

6.12. Further, we recommended that the local sanggunian consider the creation of
its Internal Audit Service/Unit through ordinance including the creation of
plantilla positions and appropriate funds thereof, in order to ensure that the
IAS/U adds value to the municipality by strengthening its internal control as
mandated.

7.6. We recommended that management expedite the hiring of personnel to fill-
up these vacant positions and/or fast track the promotion of deserving
workers among the present pool of manpower resources in the municipality.
Otherwise, plantilla position no longer needed be abolished.

8.13. We recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer review the
agency’s functions, systems and procedures, organizational structure, and
staffing pattern to be able to address any needs and gaps in the human
resource complement of the municipality in order to prepare an updated
organizational structure and indorse the creation of the necessary plantilla
positions.

8.14. We also recommended that the Internal Audit Services conduct personnel
audit and evaluate the existing manpower complement to discontinue the
engagement of the excessive and unnecessary COS/JOs workers who are
performing redundant functions with other workers and regular plantilla
personnel to align with the mandates of the municipality and their assigned
department/offices.

8.15. We further recommended that management maximize the services of the
permanent employees for regular agency functions and limit the hiring of
casuals/job order employees for intermittent, emergency jobs or specific
piece of work with short duration as guided by CSC-COA-DBM Joint
Circular No. 1, s. 2017.

9.8. We recommended that the General Service Officer together with the Disposal
Committee finalize the necessary documents/forms and file an application for
the disposal of unserviceable properties to the COA Auditor in accordance with
Section 79 of PD No. 1445 and Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property
Management in the Local Governments.

9.9. Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant, upon disposal,
drop the cost of the unserviceable/obsolete PPEs from the books to reflect the
accurate value of PPE in the financial statements.

10.9. We recommended that management prepare and submit a fund utilization
report for the Local Government Support Fund - Financial Assistance to
Local Government Units; and effect the immediate return of the unexpended
balance of ₱4,250,750.00 to the Bureau of Treasury (BTr).

10.10. We further recommended that management implement the calibrated
measures provided under GPPB Circular No. 03-2019 dated 8 March 2019
if a contractor incurs negative slippage during the contract duration of the
ongoing and upcoming infrastructure projects in order to minimize similar
disadvantageous instances in the future.

11.10. We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer
and the Municipal Accountant immediately effect the actual transfer of
funds for the intra-agency receivables and payables of the municipality in
order to restore the cash back up per fund.

11.11. We recommended and management further agreed that the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer make regular replenishment or
transfers from the SEF and Trust Fund to the General Fund Proper for
funds used to remit taxes and other liabilities.

12.8. We recommended and management agreed that the General Service Officer
prepare an updated Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR), formerly
Acknowledgement Receipt of Equipment (ARE), and transfer the property
to the intended user pursuant to the municipal ordinance authorizing the
procurement of the service vehicle.

12.9. We also recommended and management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant and the HRMO Office deduct the corresponding Transportation
Allowance (TA) of ₱7,650.00 in line with Item 8.2.1 of Local Budget Circular
No. 103.

12.10. We further recommended and management also agreed to discontinue the
practice of circumventing the Amended Rules and Regulation on the Grant
of Representation and Transportation Allowance by letting personnel below
SG 24 sign the PAR/ARE for the assigned service vehicle.

13.8. We recommended that the Municipal Engineer and the project
manager/project-in-charge ensure that all infrastructure projects are
provided with the prescribed signboards with complete information
including the status of the project in order to promote good governance and
secure the right of the people to information on matters of public concern.

14.4. We recommended that the corresponding contracts for infrastructure
projects with total amount of ₱102,447,696.00, and other contracts perfected
in CY 2023, be submitted immediately to COA Auditor to facilitate the
auditorial and technical review.

14.5. We further recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC),
through the Secretariat, furnish copies of the perfected contracts and
purchase orders, with complete supporting documents within five (5) days
after approval.

15.6. We recommended that the LDRRM Officer promptly submit the monthly
Report of Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, duly certified correct by the
Municipal Accountant, on or before the 15th day after the end of each
month.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) Require the GSO and the Inventory Committee to conduct the one-time
cleansing of the PPE accounts for CY 2024 as prescribed in COA Circular
2020-006.
b) Direct the GSO and City Accounting Office to coordinate and conduct the
reconciliation of the results of the physical inventory and accounting
records, identify and address the nature and causes of the variances and hold
responsible the designated supply officers/custodians and keep up
reconciled reports at all times.
c) Require the City Accountant-Designate to:
(1) review the PPE balances together with the corresponding
depreciation and prepare a JEV for the necessary adjustments;
(2) record accounting entries for the recognition of PPE found at
station, if any, derecognition of lost/non-existing/missing PPEs,
writing off/dropping from the books of accounts of the remaining
amounts lumped under the unreconciled subsidiary ledgers of PPEs,
when authorized, and present the necessary disclosures in the Notes
to the Financial Statements;
(3) stop the practice of directly adding to the PPE all partial
payments of infrastructure projects, instead, follow the provisions of COA
Circular No. 2015-009 on debiting the cost of these uncompleted projects
to Construction in Progress account to present fairly the yearend balance
of each PPE item;
(4) prepare the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to drop from the books all
infrastructure projects which were duly transferred and acknowledged
received by the recipients and end-user Barangays and ensure that these
are properly recorded in the Barangay books; and
(5) drop from the LGU books the carrying amount of all unserviceable
properties, as soon as the authority to derecognize is granted by
appropriate office to bring the reported balance of the PPE account in
the financial statements in order.

We recommended that the City Accountant-Designate:
a) Document all payable accounts which have been outstanding for more
than two years against which no actual claim has been filed or not
covered with valid contracts. Prepare adjusting entry to drop from the
Accounts Payable unsubstantiated claims and those which are more
than two years;
b) Require all End-User Units to submit all documents for the
procurement of goods and services charged from the current year
appropriation to facilitate the recording of the expenses in the books of
accounts of the City;
c) Keep the original file of the vouchers and its supporting documents
pertaining to all recorded Accounts Payable until it is paid; and
d) Record only the accounts payable which were obligated and have
complete supporting documents.

We recommended that the OIC-General Services Officer demand from the
custodians the submission of the RSMIs together with the RIS for all issued
inventory items and the Distribution Lists duly signed by the recipient
beneficiaries, copy furnished the Audit Team of the reports, for review and
validation.

We also recommended that all custodians in each department submit
promptly the monthly report of supplies and materials issued together with
the RIS to the Supply Officer as basis in the submission of the SSMI and
submit these to the accounting office for the proper issuance of a Journal
Entry Voucher.

We further recommended that the City Accountant-Designate:

a) Record all inventories in their appropriate account upon delivery based
on the Acceptance and Inspection Reports;

b) Update the Subsidiary Ledgers for the inventories to reflect the actual
issuances; and

c) Reconcile the submitted semestral RPCI of the GSO with the balances
of Inventories in the books, and make the necessary adjusting journal
entries for any difference noted.

We reiterated our prior recommendation that the City Development Council,
through the City Mayor as Chairman:
a. Evaluate the projects from prior years’ AIP together with the current
year’s identified projects and undertake only those of utmost priority
taking into account the available technical personnel and the number
of projects funded by national government agencies.
b. Include in the AIP only those priority projects which are ready for
implementation in the current budget year to ensure optimal utilization
of the 20% DF; and
c. Review the unimplemented projects in prior years funded from the
Continuing Appropriations of 20% DF during budget preparation to
determine its viability and necessity for inclusion in the AIP, otherwise,
recommend reversion of the funds if no longer needed.

We also recommended that the City Engineer:
a. Conduct a detailed engineering of all projects prior to its inclusion in
the AIP which includes the preparation of the Program of Work with
the Detailed Estimates, specific date and timetable and
plans/specifications;
b. Fast-track the implementation of priority projects identified for CY
2023 and in the subsequent years; and
c. Submit to the Bids and Awards Committee the Program of Work with
the Detailed Estimates, plans/specifications and Purchase Request
within the timetable indicated in the AIP, taking into consideration the
period within which the procurement process and project
implementation will be completed.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that the City Mayor:
a. Require the City Accountant Designate to issue demand letters to the
barangays whose Financial Assistance remain unliquidated 30 days
after the date of project completion as stipulated in provision no.5 of
the MOA;
b. Require the City Engineer to submit to the City Mayor and the City
Accountant a report on the status of implementation of the projects
with details as to the actual date of completion to serve as basis in the
grant of additional funds to the barangays; and

(also implied in the narrative) c. Require the barangays to liquidate and return funds as per MOA -
though the explicit prior-year recommendation text is as above.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the City Mayor, as
Chairman of the CDRRM Council, through the CDRRM Officer undertake
the following actions:
a. Conduct a quarterly monitoring on the implemented projects and
activities with reference to the LDRRMFIP.
b. Re-examine and evaluate the PPAs of prior years, and determine the
issues/concerns for the non-implementation of certain projects, and
consider them in the preparation of the Annual LDRRMFIP.
c. Include in the LDRRMF IP for the current year the unspent balance of
previous years which have been transferred to the Special Trust Fund.
d. Optimally utilize the LDRRM Fund to adequately equip the
constituents in preparing to manage disaster risks towards a resilient
community in times of calamities.

We reiterated the prior year’s audit recommendation that the City Mayor:
a. Instruct the departments concerned to hasten the implementation of
the on-going and unimplemented PPAs funded by various NGAs; and
b. Require the City Accountant-Designate to prepare the Disbursement
Voucher for the return of the unutilized amount totaling ₱2,898,056.00
from completed projects to the Source Agencies and submit the
required Liquidation Reports to the funding agencies concerned or
request the transfer thereof to the General Fund of the City, if the
disposition of the unexpended balances is not provided in the MOA.

We further recommended that the Local Finance Committee and other
concerned City personnel update and submit the Liquidation Report/Report
of Disbursement on a monthly basis for all NGA-funded projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor thru the City Planning and
Development Officer and City Budget Officer prepare a supplemental Local
Development Investment Program and Annual Investment Program for CY
2024 to include the on-going project on the Opening with Concreting of Road
at Brgy. Bunga, Baybay City totaling ₱6,606,882.17 funded from the LGSF-
SBDP NTF-ELCAC, in accordance with DBM LBC No. 151 dated March 30,
2023.

We further recommended, that, the City Treasurer should obtain a copy of the
MOA or guidelines for any receipt of downloaded funds, to ensure proper
disposition and uses of said funds and to coordinate with the department heads
concerned.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. Ensure that all transactions charged against the Confidential Funds be
accounted thru Cash Advance;
b. Assume the responsibility as Special Disbursing Officer of the
Confidential Funds of the City, or designate a regular/permanent
employee of the City for that purpose;
c. Require the City Accountant to submit all Disbursement Vouchers of
cash advance with complete supporting documents to the COA-Audit
Team Leader within seven (7) working days from release of the check,
as well as the liquidation thereof in accordance of Section 6.2 of COA-
DBM-DILG-GOCC-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01;
d. To approve and transmit quarterly accomplishment report on the use
of Confidential Funds to the Secretary of the DILG, copy furnished
COA; and
e. Ensure compliance with COA-DBM-DILG-GOCC-DND Joint
Circular No. 2015-01 and related issuances of government oversight
bodies on Confidential Funds.

We also recommended that the City Accountant Designate:

a. Record the cash advance in the books of accounts and maintain a
separate subsidiary record of cash advances for Confidential Funds per
Special Disbursing Officer; and

b. Record the liquidation in the books within five days after the receipt of
the copy of the transmittal of documents duly acknowledged by the
Intelligence and Confidential Funds Audit Unit (ICFAU) and submit the
corresponding Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to the COA-ATL.

We recommended that the management should adhere to the provisions
stipulated in the Memorandum of Agreement between the City Government
of Baybay and the Office of the President; and

Stop utilizing the funds intended for specific projects/program for other
purposes.

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor direct the City
Accountant to:
a. enforce the immediate liquidation of the unliquidated cash advances;
b. send demand letters requiring the employees concerned to immediately
liquidate/settle the long outstanding cash advances especially on the above-
mentioned prepayments for travel and those granted to former SDOs;
c. withhold the salaries of the accountable officers who are still connected with the
City, and not defer the liquidation of cash advances until retirement of employees
concerned;
d. refrain from granting cash advance to undesignated SDOs; and assign a staff to
monitor closely and facilitate the liquidation of outstanding cash advances.

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor require the City
Accountant to:
a. Reclassify the completed projects to the Road Network account;
b. Prepare the necessary journal entries to correct the erroneous balances of PPE
accounts; and
c. Be guided with the descriptions of the accounts provided under COA Circular No.
2015-009, so that the appropriate accounts will be used for each completed
infrastructure project, and prevent the misstatement of PPE accounts.

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor direct the City
Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries, and comply with the correct
procedure in recording the collections of business taxes in the General Fund, Special
Education Fund and Trust Fund.

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Accountant to:
(i) assign two or more personnel focused on the reconciliation of employee’s
individual accounts only;
(ii) compare the employee’s individual account (EIA) remittances with the
amount withheld for each EIA in previous years;
(iii) separate the surcharges and penalties charged to employee’s individual
accounts to their usual contributions from previous years;
(iv) send demand letters to employees with negative balances for their
personal contributions or payables; and
(v) prepare necessary adjusting entries.
b. City Human Resource and Management Officer to:
(i) Notify the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth of any changes in the
employment status of employees such as: promotion, resignation,
retirement, or death for proper billing;
(ii) Regularly coordinate with the City Accounting Office for the
reconciliation of the EIA balances with the amount withheld and
remitted.

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor direct the City
Accountant to record the payments for consulting services under the Consultancy
Services account so that fair presentation of account balances in the Statement of
Financial Performance will be achieved.

We recommend, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Accountant to prepare the adjusting journal entry to record the collected
Building Permit Fee in the Trust Liability account and correct the balances of the
affected accounts; and
b. City Engineer and City Treasurer to submit a Report on the Collection of Building
Permit Fees as basis of the City Accountant for the preparation of the appropriate
journal entry.

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor:
a. Direct the City Planning and Development Coordinator, together with the
Technical Working Group (TWG) of the LPOC to ensure that:
(i) The PAPs reflected on the POP for the year is anchored and taken from the
three-year Peace and Order and Public Safety Plan; and
(ii) The approval of POP and release of the CF shall be evidenced by a certified
copy of the minutes of meeting specifying the computation of the at least 2/3
votes of the LPOC.
b. Direct the City Accountant to:
(i) Submit within seven (7) working days from the release of check, the cash
advance voucher and its supporting documents to the COA ATL concerned;
(ii) Observe the proper timeframe on the liquidation of cash advance for
confidential fund; and
(iii) Always provide the COA ATL concerned a copy of the transmittal letter, duly
received by the Intelligence and Confidential Fund Audit Office (ICFAO).

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Accountant to prepare correct journal entries for the receipt and transfer of
fund;
b. The Local Finance Committee to:
(i) prepare the Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Program
Implementation within 20 days after the end of the quarter;
(ii) post the same report in the Full Disclosure Policy Bulletin Board; and
(iii) reconcile the reported completion and accomplishment in the SWA, RGPPA
and RFUSPI.
c. City Planning and Development Officer to:
(i) change the password and other credentials, and update the information in
the City’s website; and
(ii) assign personnel that is tasked and focused on posting the Quarterly Report
on Fund Utilization and Status of Program Implementation of LGSF-SBDP;
d. BAC Chairman to submit the contract within five working days from the time the
contract is perfected; and
e. City Engineer to monitor and inspect the implementation of the LGSF-SBDP, and
make sure that the project will be completed on the target date of completion.

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor direct:
a. City Planning and Development Officer to:
(i) ensure that the approved AIP is derived from the LDIP;
(ii) lead in identifying which plans are still relevant and which plans need to be
updated based on the LGU vision and responsiveness to prevailing situation,
and coordinate with the concerned department heads and sectoral
committees;
(iii) properly screen the projects and prioritize those that are readily
implementable and procurement ready to come up with readily feasible
projects to ensure their implementation and completion;
(iv) work with the City Budget Officer in ensuring that proposed projects
chargeable against the 20% DF are properly itemized in the AIP and the
related information such as the specific timeline and expected output are
defined in the AIP; and
(v) collaborate with the City Engineer and City Budget Officer in planning and
monitoring the utilization and inclusion of projects in the priority list to be
consistent with the AIP, LDIP, and CDP.
b. City Budget Officer to:
(i) prepare the budget document based on the approved AIP and LDIP; and
(ii) assist the CPD Officer in updating the LDIP should there be changes in the
AIP.
c. City Engineer to:
(i) conduct field survey and site investigation before endorsement to CPDO of
the listing of the projects in the AIP, LDIP, and CDP; and
(ii) work with the CPDO in monitoring and inspecting the implementation of the
projects under 20% Fund, and making sure that the project will be
completed on the target date of completion.
d. City Accountant and City Treasurer to submit official receipt to support
payments to contractors.

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Engineer to:
(i) evaluate the work suspension requests of the contractors as to the
appropriateness of the time extension, and duly validate the reasons with the
report of the local PAGASA, as the case maybe, and approve requests with
valid reasons only in conformity with the provisions of the RIRR of RA No.
9184;
(ii) address the issues on Road Right-of-Way, and relocation of informal settlers,
and conduct eminent domain proceedings, and facilitate the completion of the
17en incomplete infrastructure projects;
(iii) submit complete QRGPPAs, and reports on the actual status of work
accomplishments, and ensure the timely implementation of the projects during
the budget year;
(iv) properly fill out the QRGPPAs form, supplying information for Date Started,
and Target Date of Completion, among other;
b. City Planning and Development Officer and City Engineer to:
(i) address issues such as Road Right-of-Way and viability of the project sites
during the feasibility or preliminary engineering study, procurement
planning, and preliminary design and construction studies, among others,
before project implementation in accordance with the RIRR of RA No. 9184.
These problems should be disclosed during the planning stage and extensively
discussed when deliberations for the project’s implementation are conducted
with the city officials to avoid delay in the implementation of the projects; and
(ii) review and investigate the extended implementation of the eighteen
infrastructure projects and impose liquidated damages, if warranted.
c. Bids and Awards Committee should not bid, and most importantly, should not
award contracts for infrastructure projects without properly conducting
sufficient detailed engineering investigations, surveys and designs, which includes
settlements of Road Right-of-Way problems.

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor direct the City
DRRM Officer to:
a. ensure and expedite the implementation of identified PPAs, to strengthen the
agency’s capacity in mitigating and preparing for, responding to and recovering
from the impact of disasters;
b. refrain from utilizing portions of the fund for non-disaster related programs,
projects and activities;
c. ensure that all disbursements were supported with complete supporting
documents; and
d. ensure the correctness on the preparation of the Report on Sources and
Utilization of DRRM Fund to provide reliable data.

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Engineer and City Treasurer to reconcile their records to determine the
correct amount of collected Building Permit Fees and charges; and
b. City Treasurer to compute the 5% share of the DPWH from the correct Building
Permit Fees collected from CY 2013 to 2023, and remit the same to DPWH
through the Land Bank of the Philippines.

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor direct the:
a. Appraisal Committee to furnish GSO and COA copies of the Appraisal Report
and checklist for unserviceable equipment;
b. GSO to properly fill out the IIRUP, providing information on the description of
the assets, date of acquisition, and carrying amount, among others; and submit a
corrected copy of the IIRUP, taking into consideration the deficiencies noted in
audit;
c. Appraisal Committee and GSO to recheck and verify the Appraisal Process,
ensuring that the guidelines were complied with, to validate the appropriateness
of the appraised values of the unserviceable properties.

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Accountant to:
(i) ensure sufficient and complete documentation of consultancy services;
(ii) apply the correct way of paying consultancy services, which is directly to
individual consultant rather than through cash advance; and
(iii) comply with the withholding of 10% tax on management and technical
consultants and remit the same to BIR, otherwise penalties and interests shall
be borne by those responsible for the withholding and remittance of taxes.
b. City Human Resource and Management Officer to update the Database of the
COS and furnish a copy of the same to the Audit Team.

We recommended, and the management agreed, that the City Mayor require the:
a. Local School Board to formulate a strategic prioritization policy for the allocation
and utilization of Special Education Fund for Projects, Programs, and Activities
that are geared toward building a sturdy foundation for quality education as
enumerated in the Joint Circular, and not to fund ineligible disbursements.
b. City Budget Officer to charge expenditures under the appropriate fund; and
c. City Treasurer to pay expenditures from the appropriate fund.

Recommendation:
We recommended the following:
The Municipal Treasurer provide the Municipal Accountant the certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible from them as required by Section 20 of the Manual of NGAS, Volume 1;
The Municipal Accountant maintain SLs based on the certified list of taxpayers provided by the Municipal Treasurer to support the reported RPT/SET Receivable control accounts; and
Henceforth, the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant update both their records of any changes in the list of taxpayers to reflect realistic RPT/SET receivables and collections.

Recommendation:
We recommended that management observe and implement the guidelines on one-time cleansing of all covered dormant accounts provided under COA Circular No. 2023-008 thereby facilitating their subsequent derecognition, in order to attain fair presentation of the Other Receivables account in the financial statements.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant identify the discrepancies between the reciprocal accounts, Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds, reconcile the same, and effect the necessary adjustments, as warranted, to reflect the correct account balances for fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

Recommendation:
We recommended to management the following:
Direct the Municipal Health Officer to inform and/or report to the GSO and Municipal Accountant all the receipt and distribution of donated vaccines and medicines;
Create an Appraisal Committee to assess the value of these vaccines and medicines still on hand; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to record the same in the books by debiting Drugs and Medicines for Distribution and crediting Grants and Donations in Kind accounts.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the previously classified PPE items with carrying amounts from ₱15,000.00 to below ₱50,000.00 as semi-expendable property, subject to the computation of the corresponding impairment. Secondly, tangible items to be acquired in the ensuing years shall be properly recorded as semi-expandable property.
We also recommended that the GSO use the new forms, registries, and reports prescribed in the Circular, where necessary, to strengthen controls over the semi-expandable items.

Recommendation:
We recommended that management conduct the disposal proceedings necessary for those PPE items that are both fully depreciated and unserviceable, to allow fair presentation of balances in the financial statements.
Also, we recommended that management take heed and observe the updated guidelines on the disposal of government properties set by the newly-issued COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2024-1 to facilitate systematic and timely disposal proceedings which are essential to avoid further deterioration of the properties and consequent depreciation in their value.

Recommendation:
We recommended that management adhere to the provisions of COA Circular 2020-006 on the one-time cleansing of the PPE account of the LGU, specifically, the following:
A.	The Inventory Committee:
a. 		Ensure that the physical count is recorded/documented daily in a standard Inventory Count Form (ICF), using the format in Annex A of this Circular, which shall be used as the basis in the preparation of the RPCPPE after the physical count;
c. 	Prepare and submit the Report on Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) covering all the PPEs of the LGU;
B. 	Property and Accounting Unit
a. 	The Property and Accounting Units shall undertake collaborative procedures to ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that the Property Cards (PCs) maintained by the Property Unit and the PPELCs maintained by the Accounting Unit are reconciled. The reconciliation shall be completed within ten (10) days from rendition of the RPCPPE by the Inventory Committee.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer adopt necessary control measures in the handling and deposit of cash collections so as not to expose the scarce government monies to the risk of possible loss and misuse by establishing a cut-off period for collections at the end of the year to give ample time to deposit the same within the year.

Recommendation:
We recommended the following:
The Local Chief Executive to enjoin the department head’s compliance with the requirements of the circular and direct the Municipal Engineer to provide the local accountant and the general services officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;
That thereafter, the Municipal Accountant and General Services officer to prepare and maintain all required reports, records and ledgers as mentioned in COA Circular No. 2015-008; and
Lastly, the Municipal Accountant to revisit the Infrastructure Assets Accounts, ascertain the estimated useful life used for each component of the asset, determine the proper valuation of the local road assets and compute depreciation to arrive at the correct book value of each component as of December 31, 2023.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the Budget Officer properly assess and validate the charging of PPAs to the LDRRMF and ensure that such expenditures are in accordance with the policies and guidelines provided under NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 to efficiently utilize the funds and to do away with PAPs that are not related to disaster risk reduction management activities.

Recommendation:
We recommended to Management that the Gender and Development (GAD) Focal Point System endorse to the Committee on Family, Women, Children and Elderly of the SB, the passing of an ordinance localizing RA No. 11313, which if passed, shall be posted in conspicuous places. Further, include in the ordinance the following: a) measures to prevent gender-based sexual harassment in educational institutions, b) imposition of fines/penalty for violation thereof, and c) creation of sexual harassment hotlines.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant for CY 2023 and onwards prepare and submit on time the BRS for a timely verification and audit of the accounts and stop the practice of preparing the latter by batches.

Recommendation:
We recommended the following to management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to submit the Monthly Trial Balance within the 20th day after the end of the month and the Quarterly Financial Statements to enable the Auditor to conduct a timely verification and audit of the accounts of the Municipality otherwise the sanctions as provided in Section 122 of P.D. 1445 be imposed; and
Submit justification for failure to submit the required above-mentioned reports.

Recommendation:
We recommended that management steers towards the digitalization of documents through implementing the preservation of electronic vouchers, supporting documents, and other records and reports, and their corresponding submission to the Audit Team in electronic form pursuant to existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations.

a. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the difference between the accounts RPT and SET Receivables and their contra accounts
Deferred RPT and SET with year-end balances of P21,013,251.90 and P15,681,073.69, respectively.
b. Municipal Accountant to make the necessary entries to eliminate the adjustments, furnishing the Office of the Auditor with a copy of the JEV
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer already reconciled the difference between the accounts RPT and SET.
The necessary adjusting adjustments, furnishing the Office of the Auditor with a copy of the JEV. The discrepancy was already made by the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a reconciliation of net cash flows from operating activities to surplus/deficit and disclose the same either on the face of the SCF or in the NTFS in compliance with Section 29 of IPSASs No. 2.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant prepared the reconciliation of net cash flows from operating activities to surplus/deficit and provided the necessary disclosure in the NTFS for CY 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to disclose the amount and details of the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF in the NTFS pursuant to Sections 5.1.12 and 5.1.16 of the COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant already provided proper disclosure of the amount and details of the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF in the NTFS for CY 2023.

observations.recommendation:
a. Instruct the OIC-MGSO and the Municipal Accountant to continue the periodic reconciliation of their records and make the necessary corrections or adjustments to the discrepancies noted between their records; and
b. Require the OIC-MGSO to submit the RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year.
observations.managementAction:
The periodic reconciliation of records was made by the OIC-MGSO and the Municipal Accountant, hence, arrived at reconciled amount of PPE as of year-end.
The RPCPPE was submitted by the OIC-MGSO within the prescribed deadline.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to ensure the timely implementation of all the planned PPAs included in the AIP of the 20 percent DF to attain the optimal utilization of the fund and to help achieve the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Out of the total appropriation of P40,454,910.60, a total amount of P40,033,236.93 or 98.96 percent was utilized as of December 31, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to look into the cause/s of the variances and thereafter, make the necessary adjustments, as warranted and submit the same to the Audit Team. Henceforth, both should regularly reconcile their records so that errors could be readily detected and adjusted to ensure reliability of the book balances reconciled with the bank.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to conduct immediate reconciliation of the individual balances of the Cash-in-Bank account with the pertinent Cashbooks and the Cash-in-Bank accounts and to make the necessary adjustments thereon.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to prepare a request for authority to write-off the dormant receivable accounts amounting to ₱865,723.87 following the procedures in Sections 8.2 and 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: Municipal Accountant to prepare for request for authority to write off the dormant receivables in accordance with the aforementioned guidelines.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management continue its practice of:

Requiring the Municipal Accountant and Property Officer to reconcile the discrepancies noted between the accounting records and the RPCPPE and effect the necessary adjustments to come up with the correct balances.

Henceforth, require both officials to maintain and regularly update the PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards to facilitate reconciliation of the account balances.

observations.managementAction: MGSO to submit the Report on Local Road Network and update the Property Cards.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace and analyze the transactions, and reconcile the difference of ₱ 3,127,339.25 in the reciprocal accounts Due to/Due from Other Funds and prepare the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances of the accounts Due from Other Funds and Due To Other Funds as of December 31, 2022.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant will reconcile the reciprocal accounts.
observations.reason: Reconciliation of the balances requires researching past year’s transaction and would necessitate time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management require the Accounting Unit to submit the required reports to the audit team pursuant to the forecited regulations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit the Monthly Trial Balance and the Quarter and Year-end Financial Statements within the prescribed timeframe in accordance with existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management:

  Require the Property Officer to submit copies of Contracts/POs to the Auditor, with the required supporting documents attached thereto, within five days after perfection thereof, and the related Notification of Delivery of Services/Supplies and Materials, within 24 hours after acceptance of such deliveries of services/supplies and materials, in compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular 96-010.

 Explain/ justify as to why the Notification of Delivery of Services/Supplies and Materials were not furnished the auditor within 24 hours from acceptance.

 Impose liquidating damages to defaulting supplier.
observations.managementAction: The Management will devise and or establish a system of tracking the submission of the approved contracts and purchase order to COA within five working days from its execution including the notification within 24 hours from acceptance of deliveries of procured goods and services;
observations.reason: Voluminous documents

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Insure with the GSIS and the LTO Registration of the 10 motorcycle to ensure security and financial assurance in case of fortuitous events that may have an adverse effect on the said property; and

Properly monitor the registration and insurance of all properties with the LTO and GSIS, respectively, to avoid payment of unnecessary penalties and surcharges.

Prepare and submit the Property Inventory Form (PIF) to GIF, GSIS, and to the Auditor, in compliance with COA Circular 2018-002
observations.managementAction: The GSO will process the GSIS and LTO registration of the 10 motorcycles.
observations.reason: Long and tedious process of registration and insurance

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management identify the creditors comprising the unliquidated payables in CY 2022 to establish the validity of the accounts payable, otherwise, the same must be reverted if no valid claims exist.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to recognize in the books the donations-in-kind received and the corresponding inventory accounts in accordance with existing regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare an aging of accounts receivables, make the final reconciliation, classification and accounting of these account balances and prepare the DVs together with the supporting schedule for the Municipal Treasurer to issue a check payable either to the source Agency.

Those funds which are considered as special purpose funds shall be retained and those balances for which original releases is in the nature of financial assistance or subsidy and for which the agreement do not mention of the disposition of the unused balance, maybe reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the municipality, future utilization thereof subject to the usual accounting, auditing and budgeting laws and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The Management shall require the Accountant to reconcile the records and make the necessary adjustments.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:

Intensify the tax collection campaign to maximize collection of RPT due to the Municipal Government in order to increase its revenue and have extra funds available to finance the delivery of basic services and facilities; and

Send demand letters to collect taxes from delinquent taxpayers and in case of failure to collect, avail of the remedies through levy on real property or judicial actions provided under the Local Government Code of 1991.
observations.managementAction: The Management will intensify its tax collection strategies to increase its revenues.
observations.reason: Tax remedies such as levy of the real properties requires the involvement of the Provincial Government for its execution.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Stop the practice of giving different rates to JOs having the same position in the LGU in order not to create inequality in the workforce of the LGU; and

Require the HRMO to adopt a schedule of standard rates for JOs per position that is based on qualification and the nature of work/assignment taking into consideration the prevailing minimum wage in the locality.

We recommended that Management come up with a workable solution in complying with the mandated daily minimum wage rate prescribed by the RTWPB for Region 5 given the limited resources of the Municipality such as reviewing the propriety of the number of JOs being hired.

We recommended to the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management (LDRRM) Officer to optimize the utilization of the LDRRMF of the LGU in order to strengthen its disaster preparedness and avoid potential damages during disasters.

We recommended that Management, in coordination with the Local School Board, ensure that the total share on the additional one (1%) per cent tax on Real Property and the unexpended balances of the SEF budget from the previous year be completely appropriated in succeeding year’s SEF Annual Budget to ensure funding of priority programs, projects and activities of public schools in the Municipality.
observations.reason: The Local School Board only meets quarterly.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterate our previous recommendations that Management:

 Require the Municipal Planning and Development Office and Municipal Engineer’s Office to fast track the implementation of the various projects in order to achieve the desired outcomes which are essential in the attainment of its goals and objectives in improving the quality of life of its constituents; 

 Maximize the utilization of funds allocated for the implementation of various projects that are envisioned to help in the attainment of social, economic and environmental development during the year; and

 Adopt strategies, despite personnel limitations, so that planned projects by the LGU may be timely implemented within the budget year and completed within the stipulated time frames.
observations.managementAction: Management will continue its practice and improve its process to fast track the implementation of the various projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management prepare and submit the necessary reports enumerated in item No. 1 of Part II of the above-quoted DILG MC by the end of current year for the proper and effective implementation of PPAs in the succeeding year.
observations.managementAction: The Management will continue to maximize the utilization of funds allocated for the implementation of various projects in the Work and Financial Plan of the LCPC.

observations.recommendation:
We commended the Management for having accomplished majority of its planned activities for LCPC for CY 2022.

We further recommended that the Management maximize the utilization of funds allocated for the implementation of various projects in the Work and Financial Plan of the LCPC in order to deliver the vital services to the children of the Municipality.

Require the Accountant to expedite the reversion to the National Treasury the total amount of ₱2,935,078.46 as of November 30, 2021, these accounts are various balances of discontinued programs of the municipality in prior years and are lodged in the Cash in Bank account that remained inactive for years.

Require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit immediately to the Bureau of Treasury the total amount ₱2,935,078.46.

Require the Accountant to merge/consolidate the inactive bank accounts totaling ₱18,732,567.41 with the same purpose as that of the latest/active bank accounts in order to avoid additional cost/bank charges in the keeping and maintaining of these bank accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare an aging of accounts receivables, make the final reconciliation and accounting of these account balances and prepare the DVs together with the supporting schedule for the Municipal Treasurer to issue a check payable to the Source Agency.

We further recommended that remaining balances which are considered as special purpose funds should be retained and fund balances corresponding to original releases such as financial assistance or subsidy, as well as funds which the disposition of the unused balance is not specified, maybe reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the Municipality for future utilization thereof subject to the usual accounting, auditing and budgeting laws and regulations.

We recommended that Management to require the GSO Officer to monitor and comply strictly with the timely submission of Contracts involving government spaces, market/slaughterhouse stalls leased to government/private individuals pursuant to the above-mentioned regulation.
observations.managementAction: The Management commits to submit the remaining contract this CY 2024
observations.reason: Some of the contracts are yet to be submitted to the Audi Team

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE provide justification why the mandatory requirement for a CPES is not observed.  Henceforth, we enjoin LCE to revisit the provisions as provided under Section 13 of the Annex “E” of the R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR on the evaluation of contractor’s performance.  Further, that concerned department representatives be convened to discuss on the subject matter to ascertain commitment/s in terms of agency resources towards conformance to the mandatory requirement for a CPES and its necessary impact to the operations and structure of the LGU.

We recommended that Management, through the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), expedite the full establishment of the GAD database in order to have a basis for planning and programming PPAs and formulating policies that are gender-responsive and to be able to undertake thorough gender analysis based on relevant data to properly identify existing gender issues and ensure that the approved PPAs to be incorporated in the GAD Plan and Budget are gender responsive.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the GAD Focal Person continue to develop and implement GAD related programs/projects/activities that advocate women empowerment, promote gender equality and improve the welfare of women, and ensure that planned GAD PPAs are all timely implemented based on its approved GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to formulate/prepare the required Three-Year Strategic Program and consider the recommendations of the DepED PSDS and SDS to serve as reference in the preparation of the SEF budget. 

Henceforth, the LSB strictly follow the required processes and documents in planning and budgeting for SEF under the revised guidelines on the use of SEF pursuant to DepED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2017.
observations.reason: Some of the required documents are submitted to the Audit Team, however there and the 3 Year Strategic Plan is yet to be submitted to the Audit Team
