observations.recommendation: We recommended that LSB strictly observe the submission of the SEF Utilization Report to the concerned oversight department/agency to promote transparency and accountability on the SEF.
observations.reason: There has been partial compliance however, most of the data came from other Accounting reports, the utilization report was yet to be formally transmitted to the Audit Team

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require:
The custodian of accountable forms to only issue cash tickets to the concerned Collecting Officer equivalent to three months’ use at the most and replenish the same as the need arise; and

The Municipal Treasurer to perform the required verification procedures for collections and accountable forms prescribed under COA Circular No.92-382 dated July 3, 1992, every time that the Collecting Officer makes a remittance.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant (MA) to review the validity of unliquidated obligations or payables which are outstanding for more than two to over five years and if found to have no valid claims against the LGU, cause the reversion of the same to the unappropriated surplus (Government Equity) pursuant to Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445, Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 99-004 and DBM and COA Joint Circular No. 99-6.

We recommended that Management require the MA to:

Trace back the beginning balances for the Due to BIR, Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-ibig and Due to PhilHealth accounts to gradually sanitize records and reconcile the GLs of said accounts with the FS; and

Maintain GLs in accordance with the above-cited regulations prescribed by the NGAS Manual for LGUs to facilitate reconciliations.

We recommended that Management request for the copy of the MOA for those funds received by the LGU which are not supported by MOA and ascertain whether the intended purposes of the said funds as listed under the Due to NGAs, GOCCs and DILG accounts are already completed/implemented or not. In case it is already completed, any balance of the funds should be returned to the Source Agencies. 

If not yet implemented, we recommended that Management fast track the implementation in accordance with the requirements provided in the MOA and their implementation be closely monitored in order to achieve the desired outcomes essential in the attainment of its goals and objectives.

We recommended that Management direct the MA to revert back to the General Fund the unutilized equity Trust Funds in keeping with Section 94 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1 and pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994, thus, allowing the agency of the use of such funds for other purposes that will benefit the general public.

We recommended that Management prepare and submit within the prescribed period the BRS together with the bank statements and attachments and to promptly prepare the adjusting entries for the reconciling items to arrive at a correct balance for the Cash in Bank account. Further, for Management to make proper adjustments in the books for the closure of LBP CA No. 3872100481.

We recommended that Management ensure that officers/employees who perform duties requiring control, custody or possession of government funds or property be   appropriately bonded in accordance with the law.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer orient the AO in maintaining, ruling and footing the prescribed cashbook and accompanying records and in accomplishing the required certification therein at the end of each month, pursuant to Section 28 of the Manual on NGAS for Local Government Units, Volume II.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to identify the specific sources of funds and establish whether these were essentially idle funds in accordance with Sections 21 and 22 of COA Circular No. 92-382. In cases when there are idle funds identified, investing such should have prior authority of the Sanggunian and the approval of the Local Chief Executive.

We recommended that:

The Property and Accounting Units undertake the necessary reconciliation procedures as outlined in COA Circular No. 2020-006 to establish the correct balance of the PPE and record the necessary adjustments;

The Management reconstitute the Inventory Committee and furnish the Audit Team a copy of the Executive Order reconstituting the same; and

Henceforth, the members of the Inventory Committee certify the RPCPPE and submit the same to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended that Management:

Require the Accountable Officers and concerned offices to immediately submit the lacking documentary requirements to enable the evaluation of their requests for relief from accountability;

Direct the GSO to prepare the required reports to present lost and missing properties; and

Direct the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the lost and damaged properties in Cagsawa Ruins Park and Resort.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

Cause the preparation of the IIRUP by the Property Officer/Unit to facilitate the disposal of unserviceable properties; and

Direct the Disposal Committee to expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 92-386 to prevent further deterioration and to realize income therefrom if found to be saleable.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require:

The GSO to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map and keep a complete LRNPCs for all roads and its components;

 The Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects; and

The Municipal Accountant to keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network and reconcile the same with the LRNPCs and the result of the physical inventory taking at the end of each year and submit upon request of the Audit Team for verification.
We recommended that the Municipality expedite the securement of the title to the land so as to have an unimpeded exercise of all legal rights as the owner of the land.
We recommended that Management address this issue on the absence of right-of-way so as not to affect the feasibility of using the subject property for the development projects of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management identify the creditors comprising the unliquidated payables in CY 2021 to establish the validity of the accounts payable, otherwise, the same must be reverted if no valid claims exist.

We commended the Municipality of Daraga for its effort in achieving a vaccination rate of 75.90 per cent of its target population, thus reducing hospitalization cases of its constituents. 

We recommended that Management continue its implementation of the vaccination and booster dose program and widen the scope of its vaccine information drive to strengthen its constituents’ knowledge and protection against hospitalization and death from COVID-19.

We recommended that Management:

Require the MHO to render a report to the Municipal Accountant on the receipt, issuance, and balances of vaccines directly received from outside sources; and 

For the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the MHO, to determine the full cost of ending inventory of vaccines as at year end and henceforth recognize and record future receipts and issuances of the same.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:

The Municipal Treasurer and other officials concerned to exert more efforts to collect the rentals and business taxes from delinquent market stall occupants using the procedures provided under the Contract of Lease and those included in the internal policy of the Municipality and enforce the civil remedies provided by the present Revenue Code, if necessary, in order to generate additional income that will be used for the implementation of programs, projects and activities of the Municipality; and

The Municipal Treasurer to periodically provide the Market Administrator of the list of delinquent stallholders and the amount still due and demandable for monitoring purposes, and strictly enforce the terms and conditions stipulated in the market stall contracts relative to the automatic revocation or cancellation of the contract upon non-compliance of the terms and conditions stipulated in the Contract of Lease.
observations.reason: Reiterated in CY 2023 Audit

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Require the Municipal Treasurer to provide the Licensing Office the list of all delinquent business owners before the ensuing year commences;

Require the Licensing Office to closely monitor that all business establishments have paid all permits necessary to commence and continue its operations within the reglementary period; thereafter prepare a report to the Office of the Mayor on business establishments who are operating illegally after their conduct of inspection; and

Enforce its police power by issuing a closure order for business establishments who are operating illegally.

We recommended that Management formulate thru an appropriate ordinance from the Sanggunian Bayan the fixed rental fees and charges for the use of facilities, taking into consideration the expenses to be incurred in providing and maintaining the venue for lease.

We recommended that Management direct the issuance of a Contract of Lease in CY 2023 to qualified lessees, incorporating therein the duties and responsibilities of the lessees as well as other necessary stipulations to safeguard the interest of the Municipality. 

Henceforth, the Municipality should see to it that contracts are properly prepared and issued in all its lease agreements.
We recommended that Management strengthen its Internal Control System relating to the collection of revenues by adopting the standards prescribed in Title 2, Book III of the GAAM.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer:

With the help of the Municipal Assessor, create strategies to intensify tax collection campaign in order to increase local resources which would help finance the delivery of basic services and facilities;

Regularly update the list of delinquent real property tax thus create impact on the total amount due for collection; and

Coordinate or request the Provincial Office to send final demand letters to delinquent taxpayers and avail of the remedies through levy on real property or judicial actions provided under the Local Government Code of 1991.

We recommended the Management to take the necessary actions for the revision of the Schedule of Fair Market Values of real properties in the Municipality and publication of the same to avoid further loss of additional income from the increased assessment.

We recommended that the Municipal Assessor henceforth request the concerned Registrar of Deeds to furnish their Office every year a copy of the abstract of the Registry of Property, accompanied by copies of corresponding deeds of sale, donation, or partition, or other forms of alienation pursuant to the above-cited Manual.

We recommended that Management:

Require the maintenance of attendance logbooks or any other means of recording attendance indicating respective names and signatures as well as the time of arrival and departure from the office assigned in support to the manual DTR; and

State in the Job Order, Contract of Service or Memorandum of Agreement the duties and responsibilities, expected outputs, and the rate per day of the workers.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that the Management strictly observe the proper charging of salaries and wages of JO personnel, to the specific office or LEEs where they are assigned to attain the proper matching of income and expenses and the fair presentation of the financial statements. Further, consider the options provided under LBC No. 100 if there are LEEs determined to be not economically viable.
We recommended that the Municipality strictly observe the rules and regulations in procuring consulting services under the RIRR of RA 9184 whenever hiring a private lawyer to act as a consultant.

We recommended that Management:

Submit all lacking documents relating to the cash advance and liquidation documents for the salaries and wages of JO and COS personnel as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and in compliance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445; and

Make sure that the JO/COS personnel with a valid Job Order contract should be the ones to render his/her duties and responsibilities, and sign in the payroll and Accomplishment Report.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management ensure that the certification as to availability of funds must first be secured before hiring JOs to protect both the interests of the LGU and the JO personnel.

We recommended that Management:

Conduct a thorough evaluation of documents submitted by applicants prior to hiring; 

Strictly adhere to Sections 123 and 124 of PD No. 1445 on the implementation of a sound internal control system particularly on the recruitment process of the Municipality in order to avoid situations of hiring personnel who are already employed or hired by other agencies; and

Require the concerned personnel to refund the amount of P59,699.94 paid to him as salaries/wages for the period July 1 to September 30, 2022 as it constitutes double compensation.

We recommended that Management come up with a workable solution in complying with the mandated daily minimum wage rate prescribed by the RTWPB for Region 5 given the limited resources of the Municipality such as reviewing the propriety of the number of JOs being hired.

We recommended the Management to adhere strictly with the provision of Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and submit regularly to the COA Audit Team of the Municipality the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council and the Municipal Development Council.
We recommended that the Local DRRM Office be institutionalized and organized according to law by assigning full-time permanent employees thereat to maximize its efficiency and effectiveness.

We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE direct the LSB to work on improving the formulation and utilization of the SEF Budget geared towards its responsiveness to the needs of the local schools under its jurisdiction, as its responsibility delineated under DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017. Further, that the LSB formulate and approve the SEF Budget for the ensuing year not later than October 31 of the current year as provided in the said JC.

The Audit Team recommended that the BAC:

Comply strictly with the provisions of the relevant RIRR of RA No. 9184 on evaluating bids and prepare the Abstract of Bids in accordance with Section 32.2.4 of RA No. 9184; and

Perform diligently the procedures required by the relevant RIRR of RA No. 9184 to ensure that the bidding process employed will result in the award of contracts to the most qualified and complying bidder.
We recommended that the Municipality issue a Supplemental Bid/Bulletin in order to clarify or modify any provision of the bidding documents in accordance with Section 22.5 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184.
We recommended the Municipality to require all winning bidders to post the required performance security in accordance with Section 39 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipality: (i) submit plausible explanations for all the deficiencies noted and lacking supporting documents to arrive at a decision in audit; (ii) strictly comply with the provisions of the RIRR of RA No. 9184 on the procurement of various infrastructure projects with regard to post-qualification, contract award and contract signing, among others, to enhance transparency and establish regularity of the procurement process and award of contracts.

We recommended that the Municipality comply strictly with the provision of RA No. 9184 and its RIRR particularly on the completion of procurement process within the required logical occurrence of activities.

The Audit Team recommended that the Municipality submit all lacking documents relating to the procurement of various infrastructure projects as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and in compliance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445.

The Audit Team recommended that the Municipality address this issue on ownership of the location of the project to protect the interest of the LGU. Henceforth, for the Municipality to secure a notarized deed of sale or deed of donation on the property where the project will be constructed prior to the award of the contract in accordance with Section 17.6 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that Management continue to improve practices in programming, prioritizing and implementing programs and projects to maximize the utilization of the Fund in attaining desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes for the benefit of the community.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Municipal Planning and Development Council to adhere to the guidelines set under DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017, regarding the classification of projects.

We recommended that the Municipality through the Sangguniang Bayan enact a supplemental budget whenever modifying specific program, projects and activities (PPAs) covered by appropriations prior to implementing new PPAs pursuant to Section 447(2)(i) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that Management:

Direct the MPDC to closely coordinate with the Engineering Office as to the status or percentage of completion of the project as well as the other necessary information to be presented in the accomplishment report;

Conduct thorough check and review to ensure accurate, consistent, and reliable reporting of PPAs; and

Provide the Audit Team at the beginning of the year with a list of all on-going government projects/programs/activities ("PPA") and those that are to be implemented during the year with all the necessary information required per Section 2.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004.

We recommended that Management may either:

Undertake public bidding for the procurement of POL and treat the payment as an inventory of POL stored within the business premises of the supplier and not as cash deposit, thereby agreeing upon the quantity of stored POL (number of liters in the case of diesel and gasoline) subject to withdrawal by the LGU and not affected by any fluctuations in oil prices; or

Arrange an on-credit procurement of POL under a periodic billing system with a reputable supplier to do away with advance payment, as allowed per Section 53.14 of the RIRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that Management:

Ensure proper coordination between the HRMO and Accounting Office with regard to processing of loan applications with lending institutions and to adopt policies and internal controls to monitor loan accommodations and other payroll deductions such that the monthly net take home pay of any Municipal employee does not fall below the prescribed ceiling of ₱5,000.00; and

Limit the payroll deductions to the order of preference provided in the GAA and ensure that an employee's net take home pay shall not be less than the minimum allowed take home pay of ₱5,000.00. In the event that loan payments can no longer be accommodated due to take home pay becoming less than ₱5,000.00, the employee-borrower shall directly make payment arrangement with the lending institution to avoid penalties/surcharges.

We recommended that Management further improve its performance in the submission of financial reports and accounts by:

Requiring the concerned Accountable Officer to submit the accounts and financial reports to the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to the timelines of the abovementioned existing laws, rules and regulations; 

Directing the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting Office all the disbursements vouchers, payrolls, official receipts, including the corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month or pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of rules and regulations, as applicable; and

Directing the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the timelines on the recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.
observations.reason: Reiterated in CY 2023 Audit

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management further improve its performance in the submission of the Trial Balances and the Financial Statements.
We recommended that Management submit immediately the lacking required supporting documents to sufficiently establish the validity of the transaction.

We recommended that Management:

Limit the appropriation and disbursement of funds for Representation Expenses to the limitation prescribed under Section 325(h) of RA 7160 which is two percent of the actual real property tax collection in the next preceding year; and

Refrain from appropriating budget for Representation Expenses to other municipal offices since only the LCE is allowed pursuant to Section 325(h) of RA 7160.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct Ms. Morada to refund the honoraria received as BOT member and that she be relieved as BOT member in compliance with the requirement under CHED Memorandum Order No. 42 series of 2016. We further recommend that the LGU be circumspect in appointing individuals to positions in committees/boards to avoid payment of irregular expenditures.

In line with the foregoing observation, we recommended that Management:

Require the end-user/implementing units to prepare the PPMPs in accordance with Section 7.1 of the RIRR of RA 9184 and make sure that only those considered crucial to the efficient discharge of government functions shall be included; and

Require the Municipal Budget Office to thoroughly evaluate the submitted PPMPs in coherence with the proposed budget.

We recommended that the Agency require the end-user/implementing units to fill-up all the information required in the preparation of the PPMP under Section 7.3.2 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184 and submit the same on a timely manner.

We recommended that the LCE direct the    offices/departments/responsibility centers of the LGU to conform with the timely and proper accomplishment of the PPMP and its consolidation to the APP which should include the mode of procurement among others for each of the planned procurement activities for the budget year.

We recommended that the agency prepare its APCPI and immediately submit a copy to GPPB TSO. Whereas, APCPI results are among the required documents to be submitted to GPPB for the grant of Performance-Based Bonus every year starting CY 2015.

We recommended that the LCE, in consultation with the concerned department heads of the BAC, Accounting, Budget and Engineering, be able to provide the Audit Team with the basis and necessary reference documents attached thereto such as planning documents for the lot classification of component infrastructure projects procured in CY 2021. 

Otherwise, we recommended that the Municipality with the LCE as directly responsible to install, implement and monitor a sound system of internal control to craft a policy for the same premised on the PD 1445 to address the risk on the timeliness and efficiency of project implementation.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineering Office conduct a satisfactory site inspection of all infrastructure projects in order to come up with appropriate design plans and program of works to minimize if not avoid variation orders.

Further, we recommended that the LCE direct the department head and concerned officials in the Municipal Engineering Office to make the needed review of its procedures in the project planning to address deficiencies noted as per audit.

We recommended that the Municipality under the direction of the LCE:

 Ensure that Certificate of Final Acceptance for the infrastructure projects be issued only upon the lapse of one-year period from date of its completion; 

Direct the Municipal Engineering Office through its Department Head and inspectorate team or counterpart thereto to conduct necessary and thorough determination of any resulting defects occurring within the periods mentioned, defects liability period to the structural defects period for the filing of appropriate claims with the contractor and parties liable in accordance with Sections 62.2.2 and 62.2.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of the R.A. No. 9184; and

Provide the Audit Team with the result of the preceding recommended action on the determination of any resulting defects during the defects liability period until the structural defects period.

We recommended that the LCE provide justification why the mandatory requirement for a CPES is not observed.  Henceforth, we enjoin LCE to revisit the provisions as provided under Section 13 of the Annex “E” of the R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR on the evaluation of contractor’s performance.  Further, that concerned department representatives be convened to discuss on the subject matter to ascertain commitment/s in terms of agency resources towards conformance to the mandatory requirement for a CPES and its necessary impact to the operations and structure of the LGU.

We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC to perform its function in strict compliance with what is mandated per RA 9184 and its IRR to impress upon stakeholders that procurement activities conducted are aboveboard.

We commended Management for the substantial implementation of the projects under the 20 per cent Development Fund and recommended that it continue to improve practices in programming, prioritizing and implementing programs and projects to maximize the utilization of the Fund in attaining desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes for the benefit of the community.

We recommended that the LCE direct the LSB to work on improving the formulation and utilization of the SEF Budget geared towards its responsiveness to the needs of the local schools under its jurisdiction, as its responsibility delineated under DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended that the Agency identify specific individuals to represent the above-mentioned four sectors in full compliance with the required organizational structure of the SWMB under the RA No. 9003.

We recommended that the MENRO prepare the required reports on the performance status and analysis of implementation and enforcement of solid waste management ordinance pursuant to Section 2 of Rule II of Executive Order No. 46, Series of 2021.

We recommended that Management, henceforth:

Require the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements information pertaining to DRRM not evident on the face of the financial statements.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to determine the validity of the unliquidated obligations or Accounts Payable amounting to ₱3.61 million which are outstanding for more than two years and if found to have no legitimate claim against the Municipality, cause the reversion of the same to the government equity.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to conduct immediate reconciliation of the individual balances of the Cash-in Local Treasury account with the pertinent Cashbooks and the Cash-in Bank accounts and to make the necessary adjustments thereon.

Further, direct the Municipal Accountant to record the reconciling items in the books of accounts based on the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) prepared. Moreover, for the Municipal Accountant to also furnish the Municipal Treasurer copies of the BRS for the recording of reconciling items, if any, pertaining to her records.

We recommended that the Management establish the Municipal Internal Audit Office following the guidelines set forth under DBM Local Budget Circular (LBC) No. 110 in the organizational structure and staffing of office to further improve and strengthen the internal control system of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:

Review the balances of payable accounts and identify the accounts with no named claimants or creditors as well as supporting documents; 

Verify accounts with identified creditors, through confirmation, whether indeed they are the valid claimants or creditors and these are supported with documents for said claims; and after which

Certify the outstanding payables, for which there are no valid claimants/creditors and no supporting documents; and 
 
Revert the verified amounts to the Government Equity for the General and Special Education Funds by preparing the necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) as basis for recording in the books of accounts of the Municipality; and

As regards the TF accounts, return the unutilized balances to the respective Source Agencies, unless otherwise stated in the MOA, in which such amount may form part of the unappropriated surplus of the LGU.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to review and recheck the validity and correctness of claims appearing in the trial balance and financial statements. Necessary adjustments may be effected in case of invalid or insufficient claims by the agency to reflect the correct amounts, thus, present reliable accurate financial reports of the agency. A request for write-off maybe filed with COA for those long outstanding accounts as long as there is a proof that the agency has exhausted all available legal and administrative remedies to enforce collections pursuant to existing COA Regulations.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to prepare and submit the required quarterly and annual reports to the oversight Department/Agency in the prescribed form and within the prescribed period and observe the posting of Utilization Report in the Agency/LGU website to facilitate effective monitoring of the allocation and utilization of the SEF thereby, promoting transparency and accountability.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the BRS within the prescribed timeframe in accordance with existing rules and regulations, together with the corresponding Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs), paid checks and original copies of Debit and Credit Memos for review and verification.
We recommended that the management require the Accounting Unit to submit the required reports to the audit team pursuant to the aforecited regulations.

We recommended that Management identify the creditors comprising the unliquidated payables in CY 2022 to establish the validity of the accounts payable, otherwise, the same must be reverted if no valid claims exist.

We reiterated the recommendation that the Municipal Accountant prepare an aging of accounts receivables, make the final reconciliation and accounting of these account balances and prepare the DVs together with the supporting schedule for the Municipal Treasurer to issue a check payable either to the Source Agency or direct to the National Treasury.
 
 We further recommended that remaining balances which are considered as special purpose funds should be retained and fund balances corresponding to original releases such as financial assistance or subsidy, as well as funds which the disposition of the unused balance is not specified, maybe reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the Municipality for future utilization thereof subject to the usual accounting, auditing and budgeting laws and regulations.

We recommended that Management ensure that officers/employees who perform duties requiring control, custody or possession of government funds or property be   appropriately bonded in accordance with law.

We recommended that Management require the General Services Officer to immediately submit the Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption for CY 2022. Henceforth, submit the report within ten days after the end of the month to enable the Auditor to conduct prompt verification thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: 
 Municipal Health Officer to furnish the Municipal Accountant with the receipt and utilization of all donations-in-kind received by the municipality; and   

 Municipal Accountant to recognize in the books the donations-in-kind received and the corresponding inventory accounts in accordance with existing regulations.

We recommended Management to:
 Submit a valid justification for our review and evaluation why said transaction should not be disallowed in Audit, and legal sanction instituted against persons liable.

 Stop the practice of splitting of requisitions/contracts/ payments and consolidates the same to strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184 and COA Circular No. 76-41 in all their procurements.
 
 Enjoin the Supply officer to ensure that the deliveries of goods/services are supported with inspection/acceptance report in order to determine that goods/services conformed with the right specification and quantity. 

 Enjoin the Accountant to ensure that before payment of all claims should be supported with complete documents and ensure that documents are properly filled up with the required data to warrant the propriety of the disbursement.

We recommended that the Management:
 Submit the lacking supporting documents for review and evaluation purposes; and  

 Strictly comply with the provisions set forth in Section 4 (5 & 6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445; COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012.

We recommended that the Management strictly observe the proper charging of salaries and wages of JO personnel, to the specific office or LEEs where they are assigned to attain the proper matching of income and expenses and the fair presentation of the financial statements. Further, consider the options provided under LBC No. 100 if there are LEEs determined to be not economically viable.

We recommended that Management come up with a workable solution in complying with the mandated daily minimum wage rate prescribed by the RTWPB for Region 5 given the limited resources of the Municipality such as reviewing the propriety of the number of JOs being hired.
We recommended that Management establish a Monitoring and Evaluation System to enable it to assess the effectiveness and efficiency of its gender mainstreaming efforts.

We reiterated the recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the SWM Committee to comply with the instructions of the EMB and revise the SWM Plan to comply with requirements of the Ecological SWM Act of 2000 with the aim of protecting the health of its constituents and the environment.

We recommended that the Management prepare and submit the necessary reports enumerated in item No. 1 of Part II of the above-quoted DILG MC by the end of current year for the proper and effective implementation of PPAs in the succeeding year.

We recommended that Management:

 Instruct the Property Officer to prepare, in coordination with the Municipal Engineering Office, and submit the Property Insurance Form to the General Insurance Fund, GSIS, copy furnished the Auditor, in compliance with COA Circular 2018-002. Ensure that all the insurable properties and assets of the Municipality are included in the said listing;
 Provide adequate funds in the succeeding Annual Budget to cover the Insurance Expenses for all insurable properties of the LGU and secure insurance coverage for all insurable properties; and

 Ensure that the insurance coverage of the insurable properties and assets of the Municipality are promptly renewed every year and that adequate insurance coverage are secured to provide protection and indemnification for any damage to or loss the assets due to fire, earthquake, storm or other calamities.

We recommended that Management must ensure the following:

That the surety bond issued by contractors as performance bonds on infrastructure projects shall contain the specific dates of coverage, specifically the date starting from the effectivity of the bond up to the time of completion of the project and up to one year from completion, including the expiration date thereof. Henceforth, surety bonds posted by contractors with inconsistent or unclear stipulations relative to the validity period are not accepted as Performance Bond.

That projects with approved contract time extensions are supported with evidence that the validity of the performance security is correspondingly extended pursuant to Section 11.5 of Annex “E” of the IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that Management establish the Municipal Internal Audit Office following the guidelines set forth by DBM Local Budget Circular (LBC) No. 110 in the organizational structure and staffing of IAS/U to further improve and strengthen the internal control system of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to formulate/prepare the required Three-Year Strategic Program to serve as reference in the preparation of the SEF budget. 

Henceforth, the LSB strictly follow the required processes and documents in planning and budgeting for SEF under the revised guidelines on the use of SEF pursuant to DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2017.
We recommended that LSB strictly observe submission of the required reports to the concerned oversight department/agency to promote transparency and accountability on the SEF.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer and GSO prepare the necessary reconciliation to established the correct balance of the Local Road Network for CY 2022.

We recommended that Management identify the creditors comprising the unliquidated payables in CY 2022 to establish the validity of the accounts payable, otherwise, the same must be reverted if no valid claims exist.
We recommended that the management require the Accounting Unit to submit the required reports to the audit team pursuant to the a forecited regulations.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit to the Audit Team all the monthly CRAAF for CY 2022.
 
Likewise, we reiterated our recommendation to ensure the timely submission and faithful compliance of the monthly the CRAAF in order to prevent possible loss and misuse of accountable forms.

We recommended that the Management require the Accountant to prepare a reversion entry to recover the accounts written-off amounting to ₱558,205.18. Also, to submit the applicable supporting documents specified in Item 8.3 of COA Circular 2016-005 in order to process the Municipal Government request for write-off of dormant accounts.

We recommended that Management require the General Services Officer to immediately submit the Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption for CY 2022. Henceforth, submit the report within ten days after the end of the month to enable the Auditor to conduct prompt verification thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace and analyze the transactions, and reconcile the difference of ₱ 38,875.64 in the reciprocal accounts Due to/Due from Other Funds and prepare the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances of the accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds as of December 31, 2022.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to recognize in the books the donations-in-kind received and the corresponding inventory accounts in accordance with existing regulations.

We recommended that the Management to require the Municipal Accountant to check the correctness of the computation of the basic RPT shares of each concerned LGUs and ensure that the discount and penalties are properly apportioned as prescribed in the Section 22 to 23, Chapter 3 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for the Local Government Unit (LGU). Also, prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balance of the affected accounts.

We recommended that the Management comply with the quarterly remittance of the basic RPT shares of all barangays. Also, to release the portion of the Barangay for the December 2022 basic RPT collections amounting to ₱33,062.60.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all cash advances for payment of wages of job orders for the calendar year 2023 and henceforth, be duly supported with Approved Job Order Contracts (every new contract and/or renewal of contract), Approved Payroll duly acknowledged/signed by payees and Daily Time Records. 
 
Further, we recommended that Management direct the Disbursing Officer to ensure that all liquidation of cash advances for payment of wages of job orders for calendar year 2022 and henceforth, be duly supported with duly verified/accepted accomplishment report, pursuant to specific sections of PD 1445 and COA Circular 2012-001.

We recommended that Management adhere strictly with the provision of Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and submit regularly to the Audit Team the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, prepared by the Municipal Accountant using the proposed format by the Audit Team in “Annex A”, on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council and the Municipal Development Council.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Account to promptly prepare the disbursement voucher on the monthly remittance of the provincial share from the additional one percent (1%) tax on real property and to require the Municipal Treasurer to effect the payment of thereof within the reglementary period. Also, to remit immediately the unreleased provincial share of ₱72,257.57 for the month of October 2022 to December 2022.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the discount and penalties arising from the collection of the Special Education Tax is properly distributed to the LGUs in accordance to Section 22 and 23 of the LGU-NGAS Volume I. Also, to prepare the necessary adjusting entry to correct the balance of the affected accounts.

We recommended that the Management stop the practice of splitting of requisitions/contracts/ payments and consolidates the same to strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184 and COA Circular No. 76-41 in all their procurements.

We recommend that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents needed and ensure that payments for garbage collection and disposal (tipping) be duly supported with the prescribed documents as mentioned in Item 9.1.3.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

Further, we reiterated our previous recommendations that Management:

Require the Municipal Planning and Development Office and Municipal Engineer’s Office to fast track the implementation of the various projects in order to achieve the desired outcomes which are essential in the attainment of its goals and objectives in improving the quality of life of its constituents; 

Maximize the utilization of funds allocated for the implementation of various projects that are envisioned to help in the attainment of social, economic and environmental development during the year; and

Adopt strategies, despite personnel limitations, so that planned projects by the LGU may be timely implemented within the budget year and completed within the stipulated time frames.
We recommended that the GAD Focal Person submit the CY 2022 GAD Plan and Budget as well the as GAD Accomplishment Report to the Audit Team for evaluation and verification.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this AAR

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE provide justification why the mandatory requirement for a CPES is not observed.  Henceforth, we enjoin LCE to revisit the provisions as provided under Section 13 of the Annex “E” of the R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR on the evaluation of contractor’s performance.  Further, that concerned department representatives be convened to discuss on the subject matter to ascertain commitment/s in terms of agency resources towards conformance to the mandatory requirement for a CPES and its necessary impact to the operations and structure of the LGU.
We recommended that the Local DRRM Office be institutionalized and organized according to law by assigning full-time permanent employees thereat to maximize its efficiency and effectiveness.

We recommended that Management: 

Pursue the immediate approval of the Ten-Year ESWMP as required under R.A. No. 9003 to ensure its sustainability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international progress in the area of Solid Waste Management.
Properly supervise the maintenance of all Material Recovery Facilities in all the Barangays of the Municipality to achieve its purpose of ensuring the cleanliness of the entire community.

We recommended that Management require concerned officials to submit copies of the 13 unsubmitted contracts amounting to ₱5,376,988.14, together with its complete supporting documents for review; and

Henceforth, require the submission of advance copies of contracts/POs with its complete supporting documents, within five days after perfection thereof, in compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the LCE direct the OIC-MA to prepare the corresponding JEVs to capitalize the borrowing costs totaling ₱8.11 million of each project funded from the aforecited loans and disclosed the necessary details in the Notes to FS as at December 31, 2018. Also, to furnish the Audit Team with the JEVs, Loan Agreements with the financial institutions and the updated subsidiary ledgers for reference in audit

We recommended that Management direct the MA to prepare an Analysis of the Government Equity Account as supporting disclosures in the CY 2017 Consolidated Notes to Financial Statements at year-end and henceforth, prepare the same.

We recommended that Management strictly observe the provisions of COA Circular 97-002 in the granting, utilization, and liquidations of cash advances. Also, refrain from granting cash advances other than those designated as special disbursing officer or personnel of the Municipality.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management require the:

Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to reconcile their records of the PPE accounts and make the necessary adjustments thereto;

GSO to maintain complete Property Cards, respectively, in accordance with the forms prescribed in Annexes 9 and 37 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume II;

Municipal Accountant to continue with the updating of the PPELC and providing complete details of every PPE items. Henceforth, conduct periodic reconciliation of their respective records with the GSO, at least on a quarterly basis, to ensure accuracy of the PPE accounts; and

Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical inventory of the Municipality’s properties. Thereafter, prepare the RPCPPE in the prescribed form, i.e per PPE classification and submit the same to the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year.

General Services Officer to prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) of the identified unserviceable properties as initial step in the disposal thereof.  Thereafter, proceed with the disposal of the same in accordance with existing regulations in order that only serviceable properties remain in the books as PPE.

We also recommended that Management conduct the one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, so that only serviceable and existing items at year-end remain as PPE in the books to allow fair presentation of the PPE accounts in the year-end financial statements.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to withheld retention money equivalent to 10% from each payment and ensure that it shall only be due for release upon final acceptance of the project or upon the request of the contractor to substitute the same with an irrevocable standby letter of credit/surety bond callable on demand or other acceptable documents.

We recommended that Management, through the BAC and/or Municipal Accounting Office, require the submission of PhilGEPS registration number from the suppliers, otherwise, payments shall be disallowed in audit. Henceforth, require the submission of Omnibus Sworn Statement for procurement with ABCs above ₱50,000.00.

We recommended that Management:

Stop the practice of splitting of requisitions/contracts and consolidates the same to strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184 and COA Circular No. 76-41 in all procurements;

Enjoin the BAC to meticulously plan their procurement and deliberate properly on the appropriate mode of procurement to avoid resorting to splitting of contracts; and

Submit justification on the splitting of requisitions and contracts.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the mandatory requirements of the National Building Code of the Philippines before commencing the construction of building projects and occupancy thereof.

We recommended that the Management (i) provide a written justification on why TUPAD payments were released to a representative, without the required TUPAD ID of the beneficiary and valid ID of the representative; and henceforth (ii) administer the proper implementation of the program in accordance with the provisions of the MOA and DOLE DO No. 219, series of 2020.

We also required that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all the payrolls are supported with complete supporting documents, required under the MOA, DOLE DO No. 219, series of 2020 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 to ascertain the propriety of the transaction.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the BAC, to:

Render an explanation or justification why no public bidding was conducted, instead the alternative method of Small Value Procurement was resorted to;

Adhere to the general policy of conducting public bidding in all of its procurement and avoid resorting to splitting of contracts to evade the conduct of public bidding; and

Resort only to alternative modes of procurement if the conditions are met as provided in the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MSWDO to secure daily attendance sheet duly signed by the parent/guardian of the children in the conduct of Supplemental Feeding and attach the same to the Liquidation Reports to establish propriety of claims.
observations.managementAction: Documents were presented by the accountant.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:

Prepare journal entry vouchers at the beginning of the year thereafter to establish the receivable for Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax; and 

Analyze and record adjusting/correcting entries in the books to rectify the noted overstatement/understatement of RPT and SET Receivables and its related accounts.

We also recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant with a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for each year thereafter to establish the real property tax receivable and special education tax receivable.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the HRMO to:

Prepare and issue a staffing pattern plan and policy guidelines in the hiring of employees under JO and COS;

Assess the manpower needs and staffing requirement per office or department and establish a reasonable number of plantilla positions for the regularization of qualified and competent contractual and JO employees, if warranted, in order to avoid the hiring of excessive JO employees and the duplication of work performed by permanent employees and JO personnel; and

Indicate in the job order contracts executed by the LGU the proper designation and expected outputs/work assignments of the hired JO workers.
observations.managementAction: The Human Resource and the Mayor’s Office collected a staffing pattern plan and organizational structure from each department to assess manpower needs and staffing requirements, which was submitted to the Office of the Auditor.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:

Submit all lacking documentary requirements in the liquidation documents for the salaries and wages of JO and COS personnel as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and in compliance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445; 

Strictly require the submission of accomplishment reports of JO and COS personnel as well as identification documents if the salaries and wages of such workers are claimed by a person than the payee named in the payroll; and

Attach the DTRs, accomplishment reports, and other documentary requirements prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 to the payroll.

We recommended that the Management:

Initiate the preparation of a workable solution in complying with the provisions of Item 9 of COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2, s. 2020 so that appropriate wages are paid to the job order workers hired by the Municipality; and

Comply with the provisions of COA Circular 2015-009 in relation to the charging of the wages of job order personnel to the proper expense accounts.
observations.managementAction: Daily Wage Rate was increased in CY 2023.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Enjoin the MDRRMO in the timely implementation of the identified DRRM related PPAs;

Require the MDRRMO to identify PPAs, which shall be incorporated in the LDRRMFIP, to utilize the various cash donations according to its specific purpose and to address the needs of the intended beneficiaries; and

Require the MDRRMO and Municipal Accountant to closely coordinate on the monitoring and utilization of the LDRRMF.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management require the Municipal Accountant to record the transfer of unexpended balance of QRF and DRRM -MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the Trust Liability – DRRM account in the trust fund books at year-end.
We recommended the Management to require the Municipal Accountant to observe proper recording of transactions in accordance with the IPSAS.
observations.managementAction: Adjusting entries were already made by the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous recommendation that the LSB:

Prepare the SEF Budget following the prescribed format in the determination of estimated income for a particular budget year as provided in the Circular; and

Strictly observe the timelines in the planning and budgeting process as prescribed under the DepEd, DBM & DILG Joint Circular.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Local School Board strictly observe the preparation and submission of the required reports in its proper format and within the prescribed period to allow the Audit Team ample time in the verification of the utilization of the Special Education Fund.

We recommended that Management plan and integrate budget of at least 5 percent for GAD PPAs. Further, we recommended that Management revisit the GAD PPAs to consider prioritizing realizable programs adaptable to the current situation to optimally utilize allocated funds and commitments to gender equality and GAD goals.

We recommended that the Management require the GAD Focal Point System to use the HGDG as tool in identifying PPAs attributed to GAD budget for efficiency in identifying the issues, analysis, and monitoring of its effectiveness.
observations.managementAction: Did not use HGDG but have conducted the use of tools for the GAD Plan and Budget.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Local Chief Executive direct the:

GAD Focal Person to ensure that the programs, projects and activities included in the GAD Plan and Budget are only gender-related issues and concerns to ensure proper and effective utilization the GAD fund.

Budget Officer to charge the Assistance to Individuals/Families in Crisis Situation (AICS) in the appropriate fund.

	We also recommended that Management:

Establish specific guidelines on the grants of financial assistance to indigent constituents which shall be in line with DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 11, series of 2019: Revised Guidelines on the Implementation of Assistance to Individuals in Crisis Situation to ensure equitable distribution of financial assistance among the qualified beneficiaries;

Ensure that only the MSWDO personnel access and evaluate applicants requesting financial assistance out of the AICS program including the determination of the amount of assistance to be provided; and

Establish partnerships with stakeholders such as hospitals, drugstores/pharmacies, transport companies/travel agencies, funeral parlors and other service providers to ensure that referred clients are able to receive the needed services immediately. Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) may be entered into by the LGU with qualified service providers in accordance with pertinent regulations.
